> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 What is a Thermodynamic Cycle?

A **Thermodynamic Cycle** is a series of thermodynamic processes that bring a system back to its initial state, such that the net change in the system's internal energy is zero ($\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$). The working fluid undergoes a sequence of state changes and returns to its starting pressure, temperature, and volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A thermodynamic cycle consists of a series of processes (e.g., isothermal, adiabatic, isochoric, isobaric) through which a system (gas/vapour) is taken and returned to its original state. The two main classes are **Power Cycles** (heat engine cycles that produce net work) and **Refrigeration Cycles** (reverse cycles that consume work to transfer heat from low to high temperature).

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a roller coaster that starts and ends at the exact same platform. The car (system) goes through ups (heat addition), downs (work output), and turns (compression, expansion), but it must come back to where it started for the ride to be repeatable. A thermodynamic cycle is the engineering version of this repeatable "ride."

### 1.2 The Carnot Cycle

The **Carnot Cycle** is a *reversible* thermodynamic cycle proposed by **Sadi Carnot (1824)** consisting of two isothermal processes and two isentropic (reversible adiabatic) processes. It operates between two thermal reservoirs at temperatures $T_H$ (high) and $T_C$ (low).

- It is the most efficient cycle possible operating between two given temperature limits (**Carnot's Theorem**).
- It is an *idealized* cycle — no real engine achieves it, but it serves as the *benchmark* of perfection.

**Intuition:** Think of the Carnot cycle as a "perfectly frictionless" mechanical system. Just as a frictionless pendulum swings forever, the Carnot cycle represents the theoretical maximum efficiency any heat engine could ever attain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Carnot's Theorem:** No heat engine operating between two given temperature reservoirs can be more efficient than a reversible (Carnot) engine operating between the same reservoirs. All reversible engines have the same efficiency regardless of working fluid.

### 1.3 The Otto Cycle

The **Otto Cycle** is the idealized cycle for **Spark Ignition (SI)** engines (petrol/gasoline engines). It consists of two isentropic (reversible adiabatic) processes and two isochoric (constant volume) processes.

- Heat addition and rejection occur at **constant volume**.
- This is the theoretical model behind your motorcycle/car petrol engine.

**Intuition:** The Otto cycle is like a "balloon pop" — fuel-air mixture is compressed, then ignited suddenly (volume doesn't change during the burn, only pressure jumps), then the high-pressure gas pushes the piston out (expansion).

### 1.4 The Diesel Cycle

The **Diesel Cycle** is the idealized cycle for **Compression Ignition (CI)** engines (diesel engines). It consists of two isentropic processes, one isobaric (constant pressure) process, and one isochoric (constant volume) process.

- Heat addition occurs at **constant pressure**.
- Heat rejection occurs at **constant volume**.

**Intuition:** The Diesel cycle is the "slow burn" cousin of the Otto cycle. Instead of a sudden spark, fuel is injected gradually, and burns at nearly constant pressure as the piston moves outward.

### 1.5 Internal Combustion (IC) Engines

An **Internal Combustion Engine** is a heat engine in which combustion of the fuel takes place inside the engine cylinder, converting the released chemical energy into mechanical work (rotational).

#### 1.5.1 SI Engine (Spark Ignition / Petrol Engine)

- Uses **petrol/gasoline** as fuel.
- Air-fuel mixture is prepared in the carburettor (or via MPFI).
- Ignition by an **electric spark plug** at the end of compression.
- Compression ratio is lower (typically 8:1 to 12:1) because higher compression would cause pre-ignition (knocking).

#### 1.5.2 CI Engine (Compression Ignition / Diesel Engine)

- Uses **diesel** as fuel.
- Only air is compressed (high compression ratio 14:1 to 25:1), which raises its temperature above the auto-ignition temperature of diesel.
- Fuel is injected into hot compressed air — it self-ignites (no spark plug).
- More fuel-efficient and durable than SI engines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU:** **SI = Spark Ignition = Spark plug = Petrol = Lower compression = Constant volume heat addition (Otto)**. **CI = Compression Ignition = No spark plug = Diesel = Higher compression = Constant pressure heat addition (Diesel)**.

### 1.6 Two-Stroke and Four-Stroke Engines

A **stroke** is the movement of the piston from one extreme position to the other. The two extreme positions are:
- **TDC** (Top Dead Centre) — piston closest to cylinder head
- **BDC** (Bottom Dead Centre) — piston farthest from cylinder head

#### 1.6.1 Four-Stroke Engine

One complete cycle in **4 piston strokes (2 crank revolutions)**:
1. **Suction Stroke** — Piston moves TDC → BDC; inlet valve open, exhaust closed; fresh charge drawn in.
2. **Compression Stroke** — Piston moves BDC → TDC; both valves closed; charge compressed.
3. **Power/Expansion Stroke** — Piston moves TDC → BDC; both valves closed; combustion occurs, high pressure pushes piston down.
4. **Exhaust Stroke** — Piston moves BDC → TDC; exhaust valve open; burnt gases expelled.

#### 1.6.2 Two-Stroke Engine

One complete cycle in **2 piston strokes (1 crank revolution)**:
1. **Up Stroke** — Piston moves BDC → TDC; compression of charge above, fresh charge drawn into crankcase below.
2. **Down Stroke** — Piston moves TDC → BDC; combustion pushes piston down; fresh charge from crankcase pushed into cylinder through ports (scavenging).

### 1.7 Major Parts of an IC Engine

- **Cylinder block** — main body housing cylinders
- **Piston** — slides inside cylinder, transmits force
- **Connecting rod** — connects piston to crankshaft
- **Crankshaft** — converts reciprocating motion to rotary
- **Cylinder head** — closes top of cylinder, houses valves
- **Valves** — inlet valve (admits fresh charge) and exhaust valve (expels burnt gases)
- **Spark plug** (SI) / **Fuel injector** (CI)
- **Cam shaft and timing chain** — operates valves
- **Flywheel** — stores rotational inertia
- **Crankcase** — houses crankshaft

### 1.8 Advanced Engine Concepts

#### 1.8.1 MPFI (Multi-Point Fuel Injection)

In MPFI, each cylinder has its **own fuel injector** positioned near the intake valve. This provides precise fuel metering per cylinder, improving fuel efficiency, reducing emissions, and producing smoother idling compared to a single carburettor.

**Analogy:** Like having a personal waiter at each table in a restaurant instead of one waiter for the whole floor — service is faster and more accurate.

#### 1.8.2 CRDI (Common Rail Direct Injection)

In CRDI, a **single high-pressure "common rail"** supplies diesel fuel to all injectors electronically. The rail pressure is maintained independently of engine speed, allowing multiple injections per cycle (pilot, main, after) for better atomization, lower noise, and lower emissions.

**Analogy:** Like a centralized overhead water tank supplying multiple taps with constant pressure, regardless of how many taps are open.

#### 1.8.3 Hybrid Engines

A **Hybrid Vehicle** uses two or more distinct power sources — typically a **combustion engine (ICE)** + an **electric motor** with a **battery**. Types:
- **Series Hybrid** — ICE drives a generator; electric motor drives wheels.
- **Parallel Hybrid** — Both ICE and electric motor can drive wheels.
- **Series-Parallel (Power-Split)** — Combines both modes (e.g., Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive).

**Analogy:** A hybrid car is like a person who can either run (ICE for highway) or bike (electric motor for city) — picking the best option for the situation.

### 1.9 Refrigeration and Refrigeration Cycles

**Refrigeration** is the process of removing heat from a low-temperature body and transferring it to a high-temperature body. This is essentially a **reversed heat engine**, requiring external work input.

#### 1.9.1 Unit of Refrigeration

The standard commercial unit of refrigeration is the **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition of 1 TR:** The amount of heat removed (i.e., rate of refrigeration) required to freeze **1 ton (1000 kg) of water at 0°C into ice at 0°C in 24 hours**.

$$1 \text{ TR} = \frac{1000 \text{ kg} \times 335 \text{ kJ/kg}}{24 \times 3600 \text{ s}} = 3.517 \text{ kW} \approx 3.5 \text{ kW}$$

(Using latent heat of fusion of ice = **335 kJ/kg**)

#### 1.9.2 Reversed Carnot Cycle

The **Reversed Carnot Cycle** is a reversed heat engine cycle consisting of two isothermal and two isentropic processes. It is the *ideal* refrigeration cycle, providing the **maximum possible COP** for given temperature limits.

#### 1.9.3 Coefficient of Performance (COP)

**COP** is the figure of merit for refrigerators and heat pumps. For a refrigerator:

$$\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{\text{Desired Output (Refrigeration Effect, RE)}}{\text{Work Input (W)}} = \frac{Q_C}{W} = \frac{Q_C}{Q_H - Q_C}$$

For a heat pump:

$$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_C}$$

**Intuition:** COP is like "miles per gallon" for a refrigerator. A higher COP means more cooling for less electricity. A COP of 3 means 3 units of cooling per 1 unit of work input.

For a **Reversed Carnot Refrigerator**:

$$\text{COP}_{Carnot,ref} = \frac{T_C}{T_H - T_C}$$

#### 1.9.4 Vapour Compression Cycle (Description)

The **Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)** is the most widely used refrigeration cycle (used in domestic refrigerators, ACs). It has four main components and four processes:

1. **Compressor** — compresses low-pressure refrigerant vapour to high pressure (isentropic compression).
2. **Condenser** — high-pressure vapour rejects heat to surroundings and condenses to liquid (isobaric heat rejection).
3. **Expansion valve / Throttle** — high-pressure liquid expands to low pressure, temperature drops (isenthalpic throttling).
4. **Evaporator** — low-pressure liquid absorbs heat from cold space and evaporates (isobaric heat absorption).

**Refrigerants used:** R-134a, R-410A, R-22 (older), R-1234yf (newer eco-friendly).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Carnot Cycle — Process by Process

The Carnot cycle on a **P-V diagram** forms a closed loop (a "rounded rectangle"-like shape), and on a **T-S diagram** it forms a perfect rectangle.

| Process | Type | Heat Transfer | Work Done |
|---------|------|---------------|-----------|
| 1→2 | Isentropic Compression | Zero ($Q=0$) | Work input on gas |
| 2→3 | Isothermal Heat Addition | $Q_H$ added at $T_H$ | Work done by gas |
| 3→4 | Isentropic Expansion | Zero ($Q=0$) | Work done by gas |
| 4→1 | Isothermal Heat Rejection | $Q_C$ rejected at $T_C$ | Work input on gas |

**Key Concept — Why isothermal?** During heat addition, the working fluid must remain at the same temperature as the hot reservoir ($T_H$) to allow *reversible* heat transfer (zero temperature difference). This is the only way to ensure no entropy is generated during heat transfer.

### 2.2 The Otto Cycle — Process by Process

Used to model SI engines. Heat addition and rejection happen at **constant volume** (the piston momentarily doesn't move when combustion occurs; pressure rises sharply).

| Process | Type | Description |
|---------|------|-------------|
| 1→2 | Isentropic Compression | Piston moves BDC → TDC |
| 2→3 | Constant Volume Heat Addition | Combustion (spark) at TDC |
| 3→4 | Isentropic Expansion | Power stroke, TDC → BDC |
| 4→1 | Constant Volume Heat Rejection | Exhaust at BDC |

- **Compression ratio** $r = \frac{V_1}{V_2}$
- The air-standard assumption treats the working fluid as ideal air with constant specific heats.

### 2.3 The Diesel Cycle — Process by Process

Used to model CI engines. Heat addition occurs at **constant pressure** (fuel is gradually injected and burns as the piston moves out).

| Process | Type | Description |
|---------|------|-------------|
| 1→2 | Isentropic Compression | Piston BDC → TDC |
| 2→3 | Constant Pressure Heat Addition | Fuel injection while piston moves out |
| 3→4 | Isentropic Expansion | Power stroke, TDC → BDC |
| 4→1 | Constant Volume Heat Rejection | Exhaust |

- **Cut-off ratio** $\rho = \frac{V_3}{V_2}$ (ratio of volumes at start and end of heat addition)

### 2.4 Refrigeration Cycle Components in Detail

| Component | Function | Thermodynamic Process |
|-----------|----------|----------------------|
| Compressor | Raises pressure of refrigerant vapour | Isentropic compression |
| Condenser | Rejects heat to surroundings | Isobaric heat rejection |
| Expansion Valve | Reduces pressure, causes temperature drop | Isenthalpic throttling |
| Evaporator | Absorbs heat from cooled space | Isobaric heat absorption |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the **high-yield formula table** — memorize these for the KTU exam. All formulas are mathematically derived and standard in air-standard cycle analysis (assume ideal gas with constant specific heats, $\gamma = C_p / C_v$).

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Carnot Efficiency (Heat Engine) | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$ | $T_H, T_C$ in **Kelvin** (absolute) |
| 2 | Otto Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | $r = V_1/V_2$, $\gamma \approx 1.4$ for air |
| 3 | Diesel Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | $r$ = compression ratio, $\rho$ = cut-off ratio |
| 4 | COP of Refrigerator | $\text{COP}_{ref} = \dfrac{Q_C}{W} = \dfrac{Q_C}{Q_H - Q_C}$ | $Q_C$ = heat extracted, $W$ = work input |
| 5 | COP of Heat Pump | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_C}$ | $Q_H$ = heat rejected to hot space |
| 6 | Reversed Carnot COP (Refrigerator) | $\text{COP}_{Carnot,ref} = \dfrac{T_C}{T_H - T_C}$ | Temperatures in **Kelvin** |
| 7 | Reversed Carnot COP (Heat Pump) | $\text{COP}_{Carnot,HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_C}$ | — |
| 8 | 1 Ton of Refrigeration | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ | $= 200 \text{ BTU/min} \approx 3.5 \text{ kW}$ |
| 9 | Heat Rejection in Cycle | $Q_H = Q_C + W$ | Energy balance (1st Law) |
| 10 | 4-Stroke Engine Power | $\text{Power} = \dfrac{P_{ind} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot k}{60}$ | $k=1$ for 4-stroke, $k=2$ for 2-stroke |
| 11 | Mean Effective Pressure | $\text{MEP} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{V_1 - V_2}$ | Indicated work per swept volume |
| 12 | Compression Ratio | $r = \dfrac{V_{BDC}}{V_{TDC}}$ | $V_{BDC} > V_{TDC}$ |
| 13 | Cut-off Ratio | $\rho = \dfrac{V_3}{V_2}$ | Diesel cycle only |
| 14 | Isentropic Relation | $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{const}$, $TP^{(1-\gamma)/\gamma} = \text{const}$ | Reversible adiabatic process |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Carnot efficiency** sets the upper bound; no real engine can beat it. The difference between actual and Carnot efficiency tells engineers *how much room for improvement exists*.
- **Otto cycle analysis** helps design petrol engines for high power-to-weight ratio (motorcycles, small cars).
- **Diesel cycle analysis** helps design heavy-duty engines (trucks, ships, locomotives) with high thermal efficiency and durability.
- **COP** guides refrigerator/AC design — energy efficiency ratings (EER, SEER, ISEER) are derived from COP.
- **CRDI** is the dominant technology in modern BS-VI/Euro-6 diesel vehicles due to precise emission control.
- **Hybrid engines** form the bridge to fully electric mobility, especially in countries like India where charging infrastructure is still developing.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Carnot Cycle Efficiency

**Given:** A Carnot heat engine operating between a hot reservoir at temperature $T_H$ and a cold reservoir at temperature $T_C$, with the working fluid being an ideal gas.

**To find:** Thermal efficiency $\eta_{th}$

**Step 1 — Define Thermal Efficiency**

Thermal efficiency is the ratio of net work output to heat input:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the First Law to the Cycle**

For a complete cycle, the working fluid returns to its initial state, so $\Delta U = 0$. By the first law:

$$Q_{net} = W_{net} \implies Q_H - Q_C = W_{net}$$

**Step 3 — Write the Efficiency in Terms of Heat**

Substituting the first law into the efficiency expression:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_H - Q_C}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_C}{Q_H}$$

**Step 4 — Use Reversible Process for Heat Ratios**

For a *reversible* cycle, the entropy change of the universe (system + reservoirs) is zero. The entropy change of the reservoirs equals $\pm Q/T$, and the working fluid returns to its initial state ($\Delta S_{sys} = 0$):

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_C}{T_C}$$

This is the defining property of a **reversible** cycle — the entropy transfer out of the hot reservoir equals the entropy transfer into the cold reservoir.

**Step 5 — Solve for the Heat Ratio**

Rearranging the reversibility condition:

$$\frac{Q_C}{Q_H} = \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Back into Efficiency**

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_C}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}}$$

where $T_H$ and $T_C$ **must be in absolute units (Kelvin)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Significance:** $\eta_{Carnot}$ depends *only* on the temperature limits. To increase efficiency, you must either **raise $T_H$** or **lower $T_C$**. Practical limits: raising $T_H$ is limited by material strength; lowering $T_C$ is limited by the ambient sink (≈ 300 K for air, ≈ 280 K for cooling water).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Otto Cycle Efficiency

**Given:** Air-standard Otto cycle with compression ratio $r = V_1/V_2$, working fluid is ideal gas, constant specific heats.

**Step 1 — Identify Heat Transfers**

Heat is added during process 2→3 (constant volume, $V_2 = V_3$):

$$Q_{in} = Q_{23} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$$

Heat is rejected during process 4→1 (constant volume, $V_4 = V_1$):

$$Q_{out} = Q_{41} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

**Step 2 — Write the Efficiency Formula**

$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{m C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{m C_v (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Isentropic Relations for Processes 1→2 and 3→4**

For reversible adiabatic (isentropic) processes of an ideal gas:

$$TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{constant}$$

So for process 1→2:

$$T_1 V_1^{\gamma-1} = T_2 V_2^{\gamma-1} \implies \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1} = r^{\gamma-1}$$

For process 3→4 (note: $V_3 = V_2$ and $V_4 = V_1$):

$$T_3 V_3^{\gamma-1} = T_4 V_4^{\gamma-1} \implies \frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{V_4}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma-1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1} = r^{\gamma-1}$$

**Step 4 — Express $T_2$ and $T_3$ in terms of $T_1$ and $T_4$**

From the relations above:

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} \quad \text{and} \quad T_3 = T_4 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the Efficiency Expression**

$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_4 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} - T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{(T_4 - T_1) \cdot r^{\gamma-1}}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify**

The factor $(T_4 - T_1)$ cancels from numerator and denominator:

$$\boxed{\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}}$$

**Key Insight:** Otto efficiency depends *only* on the compression ratio $r$ and the specific heat ratio $\gamma$. For air ($\gamma = 1.4$), if $r = 8$, then $\eta_{Otto} = 1 - 1/8^{0.4} \approx 0.5647 = 56.47\%$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Diesel Cycle Efficiency

**Given:** Air-standard Diesel cycle with compression ratio $r = V_1/V_2$ and cut-off ratio $\rho = V_3/V_2$.

**Step 1 — Identify Heat Transfers**

Heat is added at constant pressure (2→3). For constant pressure process of ideal gas:

$$Q_{in} = Q_{23} = m C_p (T_3 - T_2)$$

Heat is rejected at constant volume (4→1):

$$Q_{out} = Q_{41} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

**Step 2 — Write the Efficiency Formula**

$$\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{m C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{m C_p (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

(using $C_p/C_v = \gamma$)

**Step 3 — Apply Constant Pressure Relation (2→3)**

At constant pressure, $V/T = \text{const}$:

$$\frac{V_2}{T_2} = \frac{V_3}{T_3} \implies \frac{T_3}{T_2} = \frac{V_3}{V_2} = \rho$$

**Step 4 — Apply Isentropic Relations (1→2 and 3→4)**

Same as Otto:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = r^{\gamma-1} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{T_3}{T_4} = r^{\gamma-1}$$

So:

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} \quad \text{and} \quad T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma-1}} = \frac{T_2 \cdot \rho}{r^{\gamma-1}} = \frac{T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} \cdot \rho}{r^{\gamma-1}} = T_1 \cdot \rho$$

Wait — let me re-derive carefully. From $T_3/T_4 = r^{\gamma-1}$:

$$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma-1}} = \frac{\rho \cdot T_2}{r^{\gamma-1}} = \frac{\rho \cdot T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}}{r^{\gamma-1}} = \rho \cdot T_1$$

**Step 5 — Express $T_1$, $T_2$, $T_3$, $T_4$ in Compact Form**

Let $T_1$ be the reference. Then:
- $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$
- $T_3 = T_2 \cdot \rho = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} \cdot \rho$
- $T_4 = T_1 \cdot \rho$

**Step 6 — Substitute into Efficiency Expression**

$$\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{T_1 \rho - T_1}{T_1 r^{\gamma-1} \rho - T_1 r^{\gamma-1}}$$

$$= 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{T_1(\rho - 1)}{T_1 r^{\gamma-1}(\rho - 1)}$$

Cancel $T_1$ and $(\rho - 1)$:

$$\boxed{\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** As $\rho \to 1$ (heat addition over infinitesimally small volume change), the Diesel cycle approaches the Otto cycle, and indeed the formula reduces to $\eta_{Otto}$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of COP of a Reversed Carnot Refrigerator

**Given:** A reversed Carnot cycle operating between $T_H$ and $T_C$.

**Step 1 — Write the Definition of COP**

$$\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{Q_C}{W_{net,in}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply First Law (Work Required)**

For the cycle, since the working fluid returns to its initial state, $\Delta U = 0$:

$$W = Q_H - Q_C$$

**Step 3 — Substitute**

$$\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{Q_C}{Q_H - Q_C}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Reversibility Condition**

For a reversible cycle, the entropy of the universe does not change:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_C}{T_C} \implies Q_H = Q_C \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_C}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $Q_H$**

$$\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{Q_C}{Q_C \cdot \dfrac{T_H}{T_C} - Q_C} = \frac{Q_C}{Q_C \left(\dfrac{T_H}{T_C} - 1\right)} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{T_H - T_C}{T_C}}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify**

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{Carnot,ref} = \frac{T_C}{T_H - T_C}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the relationship:** $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_{ref} + 1$. This is because the heat pump "delivers" $Q_H = Q_C + W$ to the hot space, while the refrigerator "delivers" $Q_C$ to the cold space.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A Carnot engine operates between a hot reservoir at 800 K and a cold reservoir at 300 K. If the engine receives 1000 kJ of heat, find:
(a) The thermal efficiency.
(b) The work output.
(c) The heat rejected.
(d) The COP if this cycle is reversed (acting as a refrigerator).

**Solution:**

(a) Thermal efficiency:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625 = 62.5\%$$

(b) Work output:

$$W_{net} = \eta \cdot Q_H = 0.625 \times 1000 = 625 \text{ kJ}$$

(c) Heat rejected:

$$Q_C = Q_H - W_{net} = 1000 - 625 = 375 \text{ kJ}$$

(Verification: $Q_C/Q_H = 375/1000 = 0.375 = T_C/T_H$ ✓)

(d) COP (refrigerator):

$$\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{T_C}{T_H - T_C} = \frac{300}{800 - 300} = \frac{300}{500} = 0.6$$

This means 0.6 kJ of refrigeration is achieved per 1 kJ of work input — a very low COP indicating that the temperature lift is very large.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always convert temperatures to Kelvin first. Most marks are lost when students plug in Celsius values.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Carnot Cycle on P-V and T-S Diagrams

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State 1: P1, V1, T1<br>Start of Compression] -->|Isentropic<br>Compression<br>1 to 2| B[State 2: P2, V2, T2<br>High P, Low V]
    B -->|Isothermal<br>Heat Addition<br>QH at TH<br>2 to 3| C[State 3: P3, V3, T3<br>Expansion begins]
    C -->|Isentropic<br>Expansion<br>3 to 4| D[State 4: P4, V4, T4<br>Low P, High V]
    D -->|Isothermal<br>Heat Rejection<br>QC at TC<br>4 to 1| A
    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style B fill:#FFB6C1
    style C fill:#90EE90
    style D fill:#87CEEB
```

**T-S Diagram Description:** On a T-S diagram, the Carnot cycle is a **perfect rectangle**:
- Bottom edge (1→2): vertical isentropic compression at $S_1$
- Top edge (2→3): horizontal isothermal heat addition at $T_H$ (entropy increases from $S_1$ to $S_2$)
- Right edge (3→4): vertical isentropic expansion at $S_2$
- Bottom edge (4→1): horizontal isothermal heat rejection at $T_C$ (entropy decreases from $S_2$ back to $S_1$)
- The enclosed area represents **net work output**.

### 4.2 Otto Cycle Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State 1<br>V1 BDC, T1<br>Start] -->|Isentropic<br>Compression<br>1 to 2| B[State 2<br>V2 TDC, T2<br>High Temp T2]
    B -->|Constant Volume<br>Heat Addition<br>QH in<br>Spark Ignition<br>2 to 3| C[State 3<br>V2 TDC, T3<br>Very High P, T3]
    C -->|Isentropic<br>Expansion<br>Power Stroke<br>3 to 4| D[State 4<br>V1 BDC, T4<br>Low P]
    D -->|Constant Volume<br>Heat Rejection<br>QC out<br>Exhaust<br>4 to 1| A
    style A fill:#FFFACD
    style B fill:#FFA07A
    style C fill:#FF6347
    style D fill:#F0E68C
```

### 4.3 Diesel Cycle Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State 1<br>V1 BDC<br>Air Intake Start] -->|Isentropic<br>Compression<br>1 to 2| B[State 2<br>V2 TDC<br>High T, P]
    B -->|Constant Pressure<br>Heat Addition<br>QH in<br>Fuel Injection<br>2 to 3| C[State 3<br>V3 Greater than V2<br>Piston Moving Out]
    C -->|Isentropic<br>Expansion<br>Power Stroke<br>3 to 4| D[State 4<br>V1 BDC<br>Low P]
    D -->|Constant Volume<br>Heat Rejection<br>QC out<br>Exhaust<br>4 to 1| A
    style A fill:#E6E6FA
    style B fill:#FFB6C1
    style C fill:#90EE90
    style D fill:#FFD700
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Four Stroke SI Engine Cycle] --> B[Stroke 1 SUCTION<br>TDC to BDC<br>Inlet Valve OPEN<br>Exhaust Valve CLOSED<br>Fresh Charge Drawn In]
    B --> C[Stroke 2 COMPRESSION<br>BDC to TDC<br>Both Valves CLOSED<br>Charge Compressed<br>High Temp T2 Achieved]
    C --> D[Stroke 3 POWER<br>TDC to BDC<br>Both Valves CLOSED<br>SPARK IGNITION<br>Combustion, Piston Pushed Down<br>Net Work Output]
    D --> E[Stroke 4 EXHAUST<br>BDC to TDC<br>Exhaust Valve OPEN<br>Burnt Gases Expelled]

    style E fill:#FFA07A
```

```mermaid
    B --> C[Stroke 2 DOWN STROKE<br>TDC to BDC<br>Above Piston: Combustion and Power<br>Spark at TDC<br>Below Piston: Fresh Charge Pushed<br>via Transfer Port into Cylinder<br>SCAVENGING Occurs]
```

```mermaid
    B -->|Condenses to<br>High P Liquid| C[Expansion Valve<br>Throttle<br>Pressure and Temp Drop]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define the Carnot cycle and state Carnot's theorem. Why is the Carnot cycle not realized in practice?
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The Carnot cycle is a reversible thermodynamic cycle consisting of two isothermal processes and two isentropic (reversible adiabatic) processes, operating between a hot reservoir at $T_H$ and a cold reservoir at $T_C$.
>
> **Carnot's Theorem:** (i) No heat engine operating between two given temperature reservoirs can be more efficient than a reversible (Carnot) engine operating between the same reservoirs. (ii) The efficiencies of all reversible heat engines operating between the same two reservoirs are the same, independent of the working fluid.
>
> **Why not realized in practice:**
> 1. Isothermal processes require very slow heat transfer (impractical).
> 2. Isentropic processes require no friction, no turbulence, no heat loss (impossible in real systems).
> 3. Perfect isothermal and isentropic processes cannot be achieved simultaneously in real engines.

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Define Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator. Why is the COP of a heat pump always greater than the COP of the corresponding refrigerator by 1?
>
> **Model Answer:**
> **Definition:** COP of a refrigerator is the ratio of the desired refrigeration effect (heat extracted from the cold space) to the work input required to achieve it:
> $$\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{Q_C}{W}$$
>
> **Proof of relationship:**
> $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W} = \frac{Q_C + W}{W} = \frac{Q_C}{W} + 1 = \text{COP}_{ref} + 1$$
>
> A heat pump delivers *both* the heat extracted from the cold space ($Q_C$) *and* the equivalent of work input (as heat) to the hot space, while a refrigerator only delivers $Q_C$. Therefore, $\text{COP}_{HP} > \text{COP}_{ref}$ always.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Pattern: KTU ESE Module Choice]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern]**
> **(a)** Derive an expression for the efficiency of a Carnot cycle operating between two thermal reservoirs. (7 marks)
> **(b)** A Carnot refrigerator operates between a cold space at $-10°C$ and surroundings at $35°C$. If the refrigerator removes 1200 kJ of heat per minute from the cold space, calculate: (i) the COP, (ii) the work input per minute, and (iii) the heat rejected to the surroundings per minute. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Carnot Efficiency:**

A Carnot cycle consists of four reversible processes:
- Process 1→2: Isentropic compression
- Process 2→3: Isothermal expansion at $T_H$ (heat $Q_H$ added)
- Process 3→4: Isentropic expansion
- Process 4→1: Isothermal compression at $T_C$ (heat $Q_C$ rejected)

The thermal efficiency is defined as:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H}$$

By the First Law applied to a complete cycle (where $\Delta U = 0$):

$$Q_{net} = W_{net} \implies Q_H - Q_C = W_{net}$$

Substituting:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_H - Q_C}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_C}{Q_H}$$

For a reversible cycle, the total entropy change of the universe is zero. Since the working fluid returns to its initial state, only the reservoirs undergo entropy change:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_C}{T_C} = 0 \implies \frac{Q_C}{Q_H} = \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

Substituting into the efficiency expression:

$$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}}$$

where $T_H$ and $T_C$ are in **absolute units (Kelvin)**.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $T_C = -10°C = -10 + 273 = 263 \text{ K}$
- $T_H = 35°C = 35 + 273 = 308 \text{ K}$
- $Q_C = 1200 \text{ kJ/min} = 20 \text{ kJ/s}$

**(i) COP of the refrigerator:**

$$\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{T_C}{T_H - T_C} = \frac{263}{308 - 263} = \frac{263}{45} = 5.844$$

**(ii) Work input per minute:**

From the definition of COP:

$$\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{Q_C}{W} \implies W = \frac{Q_C}{\text{COP}_{ref}} = \frac{1200 \text{ kJ/min}}{5.844} = 205.34 \text{ kJ/min}$$

**(iii) Heat rejected to surroundings per minute:**

By the First Law (energy balance):

$$Q_H = Q_C + W = 1200 + 205.34 = 1405.34 \text{ kJ/min}$$

**Verification using reversibility:**

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{1405.34}{308} = 4.563 \text{ kJ/(min·K)} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{Q_C}{T_C} = \frac{1200}{263} = 4.563 \text{ kJ/(min·K)} \checkmark$$

Both equal — confirming the cycle is reversible (Carnot).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat P-V and T-S diagram, explain the working of an Otto cycle. Derive an expression for its air-standard efficiency. (7 marks)
> **(b)** An Otto cycle has a compression ratio of 8:1. At the beginning of the compression stroke, the pressure is 1 bar and temperature is 30°C. If the maximum temperature in the cycle is 1500°C, find: (i) the temperature and pressure at the end of compression, (ii) the heat added, (iii) the thermal efficiency, and (iv) the work output per kg of air. Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $C_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ for air. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Otto Cycle Description and Derivation:**

**P-V Diagram:** Closed curve with:
- 1→2: Isentropic compression (steep curve, V decreases)
- 2→3: Constant volume heat addition (vertical line, P rises sharply)
- 3→4: Isentropic expansion (steep curve, V increases)
- 4→1: Constant volume heat rejection (vertical line, P drops)

**T-S Diagram:** 
- 1→2: Vertical line (isentropic)
- 2→3: Curve rising to the right (heat addition)
- 3→4: Vertical line (isentropic)
- 4→1: Curve descending to the left (heat rejection)

**Derivation of Efficiency:**

Heat added at constant volume (2→3):

$$Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$$

Heat rejected at constant volume (4→1):

$$Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

Thermal efficiency:

$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

Applying isentropic relation $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{const}$ to processes 1→2 and 3→4 (with $V_1/V_2 = V_4/V_3 = r$):

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} \quad \text{and} \quad T_3 = T_4 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$$

Substituting back:

$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $r = 8$
- $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar} = 100 \text{ kPa}$
- $T_1 = 30°C = 303 \text{ K}$
- $T_3 = 1500°C = 1773 \text{ K}$
- $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- Working fluid: air

**(i) Temperature and pressure at end of compression (State 2):**

Isentropic compression 1→2:

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 303 \times 8^{0.4}$$

Computing $8^{0.4}$:

$$8^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 8} = e^{0.4 \times 2.0794} = e^{0.8318} = 2.297$$

$$T_2 = 303 \times 2.297 = 696.0 \text{ K}$$

Pressure at state 2 (using ideal gas + isentropic relation $T_2/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$):

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} = 2.297^{1.4/0.4} = 2.297^{3.5}$$

Computing $2.297^{3.5}$:

$$2.297^{3.5} = e^{3.5 \ln 2.297} = e^{3.5 \times 0.8318} = e^{2.9113} = 18.38$$

$$P_2 = 1 \times 18.38 = 18.38 \text{ bar}$$

**(ii) Heat added:**

First, find $T_3$ ratio or directly use:

$$Q_{in} = C_v (T_3 - T_2) = 0.718 \times (1773 - 696) = 0.718 \times 1077 = 773.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(iii) Thermal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{8^{0.4}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.297} = 1 - 0.4354 = 0.5646 = 56.46\%$$

**(iv) Work output per kg of air:**

$$W_{net} = \eta_{Otto} \times Q_{in} = 0.5646 \times 773.3 = 436.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

---

### KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Celsius instead of Kelvin:** The single most common error. $\eta_{Carnot}$ formula **requires absolute temperatures**. Plugging in 35°C and -10°C gives a *nonsensical* negative efficiency. **Always convert to Kelvin first.**
>
> 2. **Confusing Otto and Diesel cycle formulas:** Students often swap $r$ and $\rho$, or use Otto's formula for a Diesel problem. **Tip:** If the problem says "constant pressure heat addition" → use Diesel. If it says "constant volume heat addition" → use Otto.
>
> 3. **Units of 1 TR:** Students write "1 TR = 3.5 kW" without showing the derivation. KTU sometimes awards partial credit for the **derivation** ($1000 \times 335 / 86400$). Always show the calculation.
>
> 4. **Forgetting $\Delta U = 0$ for a cycle:** The First Law for a cycle must be stated clearly: $Q_{net} = W_{net}$.
>
> 5. **Mixing up the compression ratio direction:** $r = V_{BDC}/V_{TDC}$ (larger/smaller), *not* the other way.
>
> 6. **Heat pump vs Refrigerator COP:** Use $\text{COP}_{ref} = T_C/(T_H - T_C)$ for refrigerators; $\text{COP}_{HP} = T_H/(T_H - T_C)$ for heat pumps. The two differ by 1.
>
> 7. **Reversibility assumption:** The Carnot cycle is *reversible*. If the problem does not specify reversibility, the Carnot efficiency formula **does not apply directly** (it becomes an upper bound).

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **Carnot cycle = 2 isothermal + 2 isentropic** (reversible); efficiency = $1 - T_C/T_H$ (K only).
- [ ] **Carnot efficiency depends only on temperature limits**, not on the working fluid.
- [ ] **Otto cycle = 2 isentropic + 2 constant volume** (SI engines, petrol); efficiency = $1 - 1/r^{\gamma-1}$.
- [ ] **Diesel cycle = 2 isentropic + 1 constant pressure + 1 constant volume** (CI engines, diesel); efficiency = $1 - (1/r^{\gamma-1}) \cdot (\rho^\gamma - 1)/[\gamma(\rho - 1)]$.
- [ ] **Compression ratio $r = V_{BDC}/V_{TDC}$**; **cut-off ratio $\rho = V_3/V_2$** (Diesel only).
- [ ] **SI engines:** spark plug, lower compression (8–12), petrol, Otto cycle.
- [ ] **CI engines:** no spark plug, higher compression (14–25), diesel, Diesel cycle.
- [ ] **4-stroke engine:** 4 strokes = 2 crank revolutions = 1 cycle (Suction, Compression, Power, Exhaust).
- [ ] **2-stroke engine:** 2 strokes = 1 crank revolution = 1 cycle (Up, Down; scavenging in crankcase).
- [ ] **MPFI** = individual injector per cylinder; **CRDI** = common high-pressure rail + electronic injectors.
- [ ] **Hybrid engine** = ICE + electric motor + battery (Series, Parallel, or Series-Parallel).
- [ ] **1 TR** = heat to freeze 1 ton of water at 0°C in 24 hours = **3.517 kW** (≈ 3.5 kW).
- [ ] **COP of refrigerator** = $Q_C/W = Q_C/(Q_H - Q_C)$.
- [ ] **Reversed Carnot COP (refrigerator)** = $T_C/(T_H - T_C)$; **Heat pump** = $T_H/(T_H - T_C)$.
- [ ] **$\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_{ref} + 1$** — always.
- [ ] **Vapour Compression Cycle components:** Compressor → Condenser → Expansion Valve → Evaporator → (back to Compressor).
- [ ] **Reversibility** = entropy change of universe = 0; the foundation of Carnot cycle formulas.
- [ ] **First Law for a cycle:** $Q_{net} = W_{net}$ (since $\Delta U = 0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *"Carnot = reversible benchmark, Otto = SI petrol, Diesel = CI diesel, 4-stroke = 2 revolutions/cycle, 2-stroke = 1 revolution/cycle, CRDI = common rail diesel, MPFI = multi-point petrol injection, 1 TR = 3.5 kW, COP = desired/work, VCRC = 4 components in a closed loop."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO MECHANICAL ENGINEERING & CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definitions of Dry, Wet, and Dew Point Temperatures, Specific Humidity, and Relative Humidity; Psychrometric Chart; Cooling and Dehumidification; Layout of Central Air Conditioning Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus):** The Dry Bulb Temperature is the temperature of air as registered by an ordinary thermometer whose bulb is dry and exposed to the air stream, shielded from direct radiation and moisture.

- **Standard Unit:** °C or K
- **Symbol:** $T_{db}$ or $DBT$
- **Measurement Instrument:** Standard liquid-in-glass thermometer or RTD/thermocouple

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine stepping outside on a hot summer afternoon in Kerala. The temperature your regular thermometer shows is the **Dry Bulb Temperature**. It is simply the "real" temperature of the air, unaffected by any cooling due to water evaporation. Think of it as the air's "honest" temperature reading.

---

## 1.2 Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus):** The Wet Bulb Temperature is the temperature a parcel of air would attain if it were cooled adiabatically to saturation by the evaporation of water into it, at constant total pressure.

- **Standard Unit:** °C or K
- **Symbol:** $T_{wb}$ or $WBT$
- **Measurement Instrument:** Thermometer with a muslin wick wet with distilled water covering the bulb, with air circulated over it (sling psychrometer)

### Intuitive Analogy
Picture the "feels like" temperature in monsoon Kerala when there is high moisture. Your skin feels cooler when you sweat because evaporation extracts heat. The **Wet Bulb Temperature** is the lowest temperature air can reach purely by evaporative cooling. The lower the humidity, the greater the gap between DBT and WBT. On a humid day, this gap is small because air already holds lots of moisture and cannot accept much more.

---

## 1.3 Dew Point Temperature (DPT)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus):** The Dew Point Temperature is the temperature at which moist air becomes saturated (RH = 100%) when cooled at constant pressure and constant specific humidity.

- **Standard Unit:** °C or K
- **Symbol:** $T_{dp}$ or $DPT$
- **Key Physical Meaning:** Below DPT, water vapor condenses into liquid water (fog, dew, or condensate on cooling coils)

### Intuitive Analogy
Take a cold water bottle out of your refrigerator during a humid Kerala night. After a few minutes, water droplets form on its surface. The temperature of that bottle surface is exactly the **Dew Point Temperature** of the surrounding air. It is the "condensation threshold" — cool air below this temperature and water vapor must turn to liquid.

---

## 1.4 Specific Humidity (Humidity Ratio, $\omega$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus):** Specific Humidity is the mass of water vapor contained in a unit mass of dry air. It is also called the Humidity Ratio or Moisture Content.

- **Standard Unit:** kg of water vapor / kg of dry air (kg/kg of dry air)
- **Symbol:** $\omega$ (omega)
- **Range:** 0 to ~0.030 kg/kg for typical atmospheric conditions

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of dry air as a "sponge" carrying water vapor. **Specific Humidity** is literally how much water (in kg) is stuck to every kg of dry air. It does not change with temperature (as long as no water is added or removed), making it a very useful property for tracking moisture during air conditioning processes.

---

## 1.5 Relative Humidity (RH or $\phi$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus):** Relative Humidity is the ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapor in the air to the saturation pressure of water vapor at the same Dry Bulb Temperature, expressed as a percentage.

- **Standard Unit:** % (dimensionless ratio)
- **Symbol:** $\phi$ (phi) or $RH$
- **Range:** 0% (bone dry) to 100% (saturated)

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a towel. **Relative Humidity** tells you how "wet" the air-towel is compared to its maximum capacity at the current DBT. On a hot day, the towel can hold more water, so the same amount of water gives lower RH. On a cool day, the towel is nearly full, so RH is high. That is why Kerala feels more "humid" on a slightly cooler, cloudy day than on a hot, sunny one — even if the actual water content is similar.

---

## 1.6 Psychrometric Chart (Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus):** A Psychrometric Chart is a graphical representation of the thermodynamic properties of moist air at a constant total pressure (usually 1 atm or 101.325 kPa).

It plots **DBT on the X-axis** and **moisture-related properties** (humidity ratio, enthalpy, etc.) on the Y-axis. A typical chart includes lines of constant relative humidity, wet bulb temperature, specific volume, and enthalpy.

### Intuitive Analogy
The psychrometric chart is like a "map" of air states. Just as a geographical map helps you find the shortest path between two cities, the psychrometric chart helps engineers visualize the path of air as it is heated, cooled, humidified, or dehumidified inside an HVAC system.

---

## 1.7 Cooling and Dehumidification (Overview)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus):** Cooling and Dehumidification is the simultaneous process of reducing both the Dry Bulb Temperature and the Humidity Ratio of air, typically by passing it over a cooling coil whose surface temperature is below the Dew Point Temperature of the incoming air.

When air contacts a cold surface below DPT, water vapor condenses out as liquid, reducing both temperature and moisture.

### Intuitive Analogy
Your home air conditioner in Trivandrum does exactly this. It blows room air over an evaporator coil that is colder than the dew point. Water drips off the coil (the dehumidification), and the air leaves cooler and drier — a perfect real-world example of cooling and dehumidification.

---

## 1.8 Layout of Central Air Conditioning Systems (Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus):** A Central Air Conditioning System is one in which air is conditioned (cooled, heated, cleaned, humidified/dehumidified) at a central plant and then distributed to multiple zones through a network of ducts.

Typical components include: **Central Plant (Chiller/AHU) → Supply Ducts → Diffusers (in rooms) → Return Ducts → Mixed Air Plenum → Filters & Cooling Coils → Fans → Exhaust**.

### Intuitive Analogy
A central AC is like a municipal water supply system. A central "water treatment plant" (the AHU/Chiller) conditions a fluid (air), and pipes (ducts) deliver it to many "houses" (rooms) simultaneously. This is why large buildings, malls, and airports in Kochi or Calicut use central AC rather than individual units.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Dry Bulb Temperature — Theoretical Depth

The Dry Bulb Temperature is a **true thermodynamic temperature** of the air-vapor mixture. It is one of the two fundamental independent variables (along with atmospheric pressure) required to fully define the state of moist air on a psychrometric chart.

- **Property Type:** Intensive, state variable
- **Reference:** Ordinary mercury or alcohol thermometer, properly shielded
- **Key Point:** DBT is unaffected by humidity — it only tells you the kinetic energy of the air molecules.

### Why DBT Matters
In air conditioning design, DBT is the primary indicator of **sensible heat load** — the heat you can feel as "warmth." The temperature at which you set your AC remote in Kerala is essentially setting the target DBT.

---

## 2.2 Wet Bulb Temperature — Theoretical Depth

WBT is governed by the principle of **adiabatic saturation**. When unsaturated air flows over a wetted surface:

1. Water evaporates into the air (raising its humidity ratio).
2. The latent heat required for evaporation comes from the air itself (lowering its temperature).
3. The process continues until the air reaches saturation.

The temperature at saturation is the **Wet Bulb Temperature**.

- **Key Equation:** The WBT line on a psychrometric chart has a specific slope determined by:
$$\omega = \frac{(2501 - 2.381 \cdot T_{wb}) \cdot \omega_s^* - 1.006 \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb})}{2501 + 1.805 \cdot T_{db} - 4.186 \cdot T_{wb}}$$

where $\omega_s^*$ is the saturation humidity ratio at WBT.

### Why WBT Matters
WBT is a strong indicator of the **total enthalpy** (total heat) of air. It is also used to determine the **apparent temperature** (how hot it actually feels to humans), and is the basis for the **Wet Bulb Globe Temperature (WBGT)** used in industrial safety standards.

---

## 2.3 Dew Point Temperature — Theoretical Depth

DPT is the temperature at which the actual partial pressure of water vapor equals the saturation pressure. It is found by **cooling the air at constant $\omega$ until saturation**.

- **Key Property:** DPT depends ONLY on the actual moisture content ($\omega$), not on DBT.
- **Saturation Condition:** At DPT, $\phi = 100\%$
- **Condensation Point:** Below DPT, water vapor must condense — this is the principle behind chilled mirror hygrometers and the formation of fog/mist.

### Why DPT Matters
- **Equipment Design:** Cooling coils in AC units must be sized to operate below the room DPT to ensure dehumidification.
- **Comfort:** High DPT (even with comfortable DBT) causes clamminess and mold growth.
- **Industrial:** Surface condensation in ducts and on chilled water pipes is prevented by insulation chosen based on DPT.

---

## 2.4 Specific Humidity — Theoretical Depth

Specific humidity is derived from **Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures**. For moist air at total pressure $P$:

$$P = P_a + P_v$$

where $P_a$ is partial pressure of dry air and $P_v$ is partial pressure of water vapor.

Using the ideal gas law for each component and taking the mass ratio:

$$\omega = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P - P_v}$$

The constant **0.622** is the ratio of the gas constant for water vapor to that for dry air:

$$0.622 = \frac{R_a}{R_v} = \frac{0.287}{0.4615}$$

### Why Specific Humidity Matters
- **Process Calculations:** In any air conditioning process that involves moisture addition or removal, $\omega$ changes — DBT and WBT may or may not change.
- **Energy Calculations:** Humidification processes are tracked more easily with $\omega$ than with RH.

---

## 2.5 Relative Humidity — Theoretical Depth

RH is fundamentally a **ratio of pressures**, not of masses or volumes. It is **temperature-dependent** because saturation pressure $P_s$ is a strong function of temperature (Clausius-Clapeyron relation).

$$\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_s(T_{db})}$$

or equivalently using humidity ratios:

$$\phi = \frac{\omega \cdot P}{(0.622 + \omega) \cdot P_s(T_{db})}$$

### Why RH Matters
- **Human Comfort:** RH of 40-60% is the comfort band. Above 60%, the air feels sticky; below 30%, it causes dry skin and static electricity.
- **Process Industries:** Textile, pharmaceutical, and food industries require strict RH control.
- **Limitation:** RH alone is insufficient to specify air state — both DBT and RH are needed.

---

## 2.6 Psychrometric Chart — Theoretical Depth

The chart is constructed at **constant total pressure** (typically 1 atm). Standard axes and lines:

| Element | Position / Type |
|---------|-----------------|
| X-axis | Dry Bulb Temperature (°C) |
| Y-axis | Water vapor pressure (mmHg) or Humidity Ratio (kg/kg dry air) |
| Saturation Curve | Boundary line at $\phi = 100\%$ |
| RH Lines | Curves below saturation, labeled at top |
| WBT Lines | Straight diagonal lines sloping up-left |
| DPT Lines | Horizontal lines (constant $\omega$) |
| Enthalpy Lines | Nearly parallel to WBT lines |
| Specific Volume Lines | Steep lines |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **saturation curve** is also called the **dew point curve** because every point on it represents a state where DBT = WBT = DPT = saturation temperature.

---

## 2.7 Cooling and Dehumidification — Theoretical Depth

This is a **combined sensible + latent cooling** process on the psychrometric chart. It is represented as a line sloping **down and to the left** from the initial state.

**Process Equations:**

$$Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{db,2} - T_{db,1}) \quad \text{(Sensible Heat)}$$

$$Q_l = \dot{m}_a \cdot (h_{fg}) \cdot (\omega_1 - \omega_2) \quad \text{(Latent Heat)}$$

$$Q_t = Q_s + Q_l = \dot{m}_a \cdot (h_2 - h_1) \quad \text{(Total Heat)}$$

where $h_{fg} \approx 2501$ kJ/kg at 0°C is the latent heat of vaporization of water.

### Process Path on Chart
- The line from state 1 to state 2 lies on a **constant WBT line** (approximately) when the cooling coil surface is at a single uniform temperature.
- **Bypass Factor (BF)** is introduced for real coils that don't fully saturate the air:

$$BF = \frac{T_{db,2} - T_{db,s}}{T_{db,1} - T_{db,s}}$$

where subscript $s$ denotes the apparatus dew point (ADP) — the surface temperature of the coil.

---

## 2.8 Central Air Conditioning — Theoretical Depth

Central AC systems are classified into two main types:

1. **All-Air Systems:** Air is the only medium carrying cooling/heating effect. Examples: Single Duct, Dual Duct, Multi-Zone, Variable Air Volume (VAV).
2. **Air-Water Systems:** Air handles ventilation, water (chilled) handles most of the cooling load. Example: Induction Units, Fan Coil Units (FCU).

### Typical Central Plant Layout
- **Chiller:** Produces chilled water using a vapor compression or absorption cycle.
- **Cooling Tower:** Rejects condenser heat to atmosphere.
- **AHU (Air Handling Unit):** Houses filters, cooling/heating coils, fans, dampers, humidifier.
- **Ductwork:** Distributes conditioned air.
- **Controls:** Sensors, thermostats, BMS (Building Management System).

---

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Property / Process** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Notes** |
|------------------------|-------------|-----------|-----------|
| Specific Humidity | $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v}$ | kg/kg dry air | $P$ = total, $P_v$ = vapor pressure |
| Relative Humidity | $\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_s(T_{db})}$ | dimensionless or % | $P_s$ from steam tables |
| RH (in terms of $\omega$) | $\phi = \dfrac{\omega \cdot P}{(0.622 + \omega) \cdot P_s}$ | dimensionless | Used on psychrometric chart |
| DPT relation | $P_v = P_s(T_{dp})$ | Pa or kPa | At DPT, air is saturated |
| Sensible Heat | $Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot 1.006 \cdot \Delta T_{db}$ | kW | $c_p$ of dry air = 1.006 kJ/kg·K |
| Latent Heat | $Q_l = \dot{m}_a \cdot 2501 \cdot \Delta \omega$ | kW | $h_{fg}$ ≈ 2501 kJ/kg |
| Total Heat | $Q_t = \dot{m}_a \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$ | kW | Enthalpy from chart |
| Enthalpy of moist air | $h = 1.006 \cdot T_{db} + \omega \cdot (2501 + 1.805 \cdot T_{db})$ | kJ/kg dry air | Reference at 0°C |
| Bypass Factor | $BF = \dfrac{T_{2} - T_{s}}{T_{1} - T_{s}}$ | dimensionless | $T_s$ = coil surface temp (ADP) |
| Contact Factor | $CF = 1 - BF$ | dimensionless | Measure of coil effectiveness |
| Specific Volume (approx.) | $v = \dfrac{0.00283 \cdot (T_{db} + 273)}{P - P_v}$ | m³/kg dry air | For ideal gas mix |
| Dew Point from RH | $T_{dp} \approx T_{db} - \dfrac{100 - \phi}{5}$ | °C | Approximation, valid for moderate RH |
| Density of moist air | $\rho = \dfrac{1}{v}$ | kg/m³ | Used for air flow calculations |
| Air flow rate (mass) | $\dot{m}_a = \rho \cdot \dot{V}$ | kg/s | $\dot{V}$ in m³/s |
| By-pass factor of coil | $BF = \dfrac{T_{db,2} - T_{db,coil}}{T_{db,1} - T_{db,coil}}$ | - | Real cooling coil performance |

> [!NOTE]
> All temperatures in enthalpy formulas must be in **°C** for the coefficients to be valid as written. The values 1.006, 1.805, 0.622, and 2501 are **standard psychrometric constants** valid at standard atmospheric pressure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Specific Humidity ($\omega$)

Consider a mixture of dry air and water vapor at total pressure $P$ and temperature $T$. Let:
- $m_a$ = mass of dry air
- $m_v$ = mass of water vapor
- $P_a$ = partial pressure of dry air
- $P_v$ = partial pressure of water vapor
- $R_a$ = specific gas constant of dry air = **0.287 kJ/kg·K**
- $R_v$ = specific gas constant of water vapor = **0.4615 kJ/kg·K**

**Step 1:** Apply Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures.

$$P = P_a + P_v$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Ideal Gas Equation of State to each component.

For dry air:

$$P_a \cdot V = m_a \cdot R_a \cdot T$$

For water vapor:

$$P_v \cdot V = m_v \cdot R_v \cdot T$$

**Step 3:** Divide the two equations to eliminate $V$ and $T$.

$$\frac{P_v}{P_a} = \frac{m_v \cdot R_v}{m_a \cdot R_a}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the mass ratio $m_v / m_a$.

$$\frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{P_v}{P_a} \cdot \frac{R_a}{R_v}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $P_a = P - P_v$ from Dalton's Law.

$$\frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{P_v}{P - P_v} \cdot \frac{R_a}{R_v}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute the numerical value of $R_a / R_v$.

$$\frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{P_v}{P - P_v} \cdot \frac{0.287}{0.4615} = \frac{P_v}{P - P_v} \cdot 0.622$$

**Step 7:** Define specific humidity as the mass ratio of water vapor to dry air.

$$\boxed{\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P - P_v}}$$

This is the fundamental psychrometric relation between moisture content and partial pressure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Relative Humidity Relation Using $\omega$

**Step 1:** From the specific humidity derivation:

$$P_v = \frac{\omega \cdot P}{0.622 + \omega}$$

**Step 2:** By definition of relative humidity:

$$\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_s(T_{db})}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the expression for $P_v$.

$$\phi = \frac{\omega \cdot P}{(0.622 + \omega) \cdot P_s(T_{db})}$$

**Step 4:** This is the form used to draw RH lines on the psychrometric chart. For a fixed $\phi$, this is a curve in the $T_{db}$–$\omega$ plane.

$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{0.622 \cdot \phi \cdot P_s(T_{db})}{P - \phi \cdot P_s(T_{db})}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Enthalpy of Moist Air

The enthalpy of a mixture is the sum of the enthalpies of its components per unit mass of dry air. Take 0°C as the reference.

**Step 1:** Enthalpy of dry air component (per kg of dry air):

$$h_a = c_{pa} \cdot T_{db} = 1.006 \cdot T_{db} \quad [\text{kJ/kg dry air}]$$

**Step 2:** Enthalpy of water vapor component (per kg of dry air, accounting for $\omega$ kg of vapor):

$$h_v = \omega \cdot (h_{fg,0} + c_{pv} \cdot T_{db}) = \omega \cdot (2501 + 1.805 \cdot T_{db})$$

Here $c_{pv} = 1.805$ kJ/kg·K is the specific heat of water vapor, and $h_{fg,0} = 2501$ kJ/kg is the latent heat at 0°C.

**Step 3:** Total enthalpy of moist air per kg of dry air:

$$h = h_a + h_v$$

$$h = 1.006 \cdot T_{db} + \omega \cdot (2501 + 1.805 \cdot T_{db})$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange for clarity:

$$\boxed{h = (1.006 + 1.805 \cdot \omega) \cdot T_{db} + 2501 \cdot \omega \quad [\text{kJ/kg dry air}]}$$

**Step 5:** Enthalpy change between two states:

$$\Delta h = h_2 - h_1 = 1.006 \cdot (T_{db,2} - T_{db,1}) + (\omega_2 \cdot (2501 + 1.805 \cdot T_{db,2}) - \omega_1 \cdot (2501 + 1.805 \cdot T_{db,1}))$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Bypass Factor

**Step 1:** Consider a real cooling coil with surface temperature $T_s$ (also called ADP — Apparatus Dew Point).

A fraction of air contacts the coil and reaches temperature $T_s$. The remaining fraction (bypass) is unchanged at $T_1$.

**Step 2:** By mass-weighted energy balance, the mixed outlet temperature $T_2$ is:

$$T_2 = (1 - BF) \cdot T_s + BF \cdot T_1$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to isolate $BF$:

$$T_2 - T_s = BF \cdot (T_1 - T_s)$$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$\boxed{BF = \frac{T_2 - T_s}{T_1 - T_s}}$$

**Step 5:** When BF = 0, perfect cooling (all air reaches coil temperature). When BF = 1, no cooling (coil has no effect).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Cooling and Dehumidification

**Given:** Atmospheric air at $T_{db,1} = 35°C$, $T_{wb,1} = 25°C$ enters an air conditioning unit at a rate of $\dot{V}_1 = 100$ m³/min. The air leaves at $T_{db,2} = 20°C$, $T_{wb,2} = 18°C$. Atmospheric pressure = 101.325 kPa.

**Step 1:** From psychrometric chart, for state 1 (35°C DBT, 25°C WBT):

$$\omega_1 \approx 0.0145 \text{ kg/kg dry air}, \quad v_1 \approx 0.892 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg dry air}, \quad h_1 \approx 71.5 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$

**Step 2:** From chart, for state 2 (20°C DBT, 18°C WBT):

$$\omega_2 \approx 0.0108 \text{ kg/kg dry air}, \quad v_2 \approx 0.842 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg dry air}, \quad h_2 \approx 47.3 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$

**Step 3:** Mass flow rate of dry air:

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{\dot{V}_1}{v_1} = \frac{100}{0.892 \times 60} = 1.868 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4:** Sensible Heat Load:

$$Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot 1.006 \cdot (T_{db,1} - T_{db,2}) = 1.868 \cdot 1.006 \cdot (35 - 20) = 28.19 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5:** Latent Heat Load:

$$Q_l = \dot{m}_a \cdot 2501 \cdot (\omega_1 - \omega_2) = 1.868 \cdot 2501 \cdot (0.0145 - 0.0108) = 17.27 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6:** Total Heat Load:

$$Q_t = Q_s + Q_l = 28.19 + 17.27 = 45.46 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7:** Verification using enthalpy:

$$Q_t = \dot{m}_a \cdot (h_1 - h_2) = 1.868 \cdot (71.5 - 47.3) = 45.25 \text{ kW}$$

(Minor difference due to chart reading precision — both methods agree.)

**Step 8:** Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) of coil: From chart, the WBT of the outlet air is 18°C, so ADP ≈ 15°C (read by extending the WBT line of state 2 down to the saturation curve).

**Step 9:** Bypass Factor:

$$BF = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}} = \frac{20 - 15}{35 - 15} = 0.25$$

This is a typical value for a well-designed chilled water cooling coil.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Property Summary Table

| **Property** | **Symbol** | **Function of** | **Affects** | **Uniqueness** |
|--------------|-----------|------------------|-------------|----------------|
| Dry Bulb Temp | $T_{db}$ | - | Sensible heat | Independent variable |
| Wet Bulb Temp | $T_{wb}$ | $T_{db}, \omega$ | Total heat | State property |
| Dew Point Temp | $T_{dp}$ | $\omega$ only | Condensation | State property |
| Specific Humidity | $\omega$ | $P_v, P$ | Latent heat | Conserved in pure T changes |
| Relative Humidity | $\phi$ | $T_{db}, \omega$ | Comfort, drying | Strongly T-dependent |
| Enthalpy | $h$ | $T_{db}, \omega$ | Total energy | Additive for mixing |
| Specific Volume | $v$ | $T_{db}, P_v$ | Air flow calc | Used in ducts |

| **Process** | **DBT** | **WBT** | **DPT** | **$\omega$** | **$\phi$** | **$h$** |
|-------------|---------|---------|---------|--------------|------------|---------|
| Heating | ↑ | ↑ | = | = | ↓ | ↑ |
| Cooling | ↓ | ↓ | = | = | ↑ (often) | ↓ |
| Cooling & Dehumidification | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ or = | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ |
| Humidification | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Dehumidification | = or ↓ | = or ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | = or ↑ | ↓ |
| Adiabatic Saturation | ↓ | = | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ (=100%) | = |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[State Point P on Chart DBT 30] --> B[Draw horizontal line to saturation curve]
    B --> C[Intersection is Dew Point Temperature Tdp]
    A --> D[Draw WBT line through point P]

    A --> F[Vertical drop to X axis gives DBT]
    F --> G[Reading order on saturation curve: DPT is leftmost, WBT in middle, DBT at right]
```

> [!NOTE]
> On the saturation curve of a psychrometric chart, the points always appear in this order from left to right: **Dew Point Temperature < Wet Bulb Temperature < Dry Bulb Temperature**. This is because the air heats up at constant moisture until it becomes unsaturated, while the WBT lies between DBT and DPT as a measure of evaporative cooling capacity.
