# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Dry Bulb Temperature and Wet Bulb Temperature. How are they measured? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT):** It is the temperature of air as indicated by a standard thermometer whose bulb is kept dry and shielded from direct radiation and moisture. It represents the true thermodynamic temperature of the air-vapor mixture. It is measured using an ordinary mercury-in-glass thermometer or an RTD/thermocouple exposed to the air stream.

**Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT):** It is the temperature a parcel of air attains when it is cooled adiabatically to saturation by the evaporation of water into it, at constant total pressure. It is measured using a thermometer whose bulb is covered with a muslin wick soaked in distilled water, with air circulated over it (as in a sling psychrometer). The lower the humidity, the more the WBT is depressed below the DBT.

---

### Question 2: Define Specific Humidity and Relative Humidity. State their units and typical ranges. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Specific Humidity ($\omega$):** It is defined as the mass of water vapor present per unit mass of dry air.

$$\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P - P_v}$$

- **Unit:** kg of water vapor / kg of dry air (kg/kg dry air)
- **Range:** 0 to ~0.030 kg/kg dry air for typical atmospheric conditions.

**Relative Humidity ($\phi$):** It is the ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapor in the air to the saturation pressure of water vapor at the same Dry Bulb Temperature.

$$\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_s(T_{db})}$$

- **Unit:** Expressed as a percentage (dimensionless)
- **Range:** 0% (perfectly dry air) to 100% (saturated air)
- **Comfort Range:** 40-60% for human comfort in conditioned spaces.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and use of a Psychrometric Chart.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction of Psychrometric Chart:**

A psychrometric chart is a graphical plot of the thermodynamic properties of moist air drawn at a constant total pressure (normally 1 atm = 101.325 kPa). The chart elements are:

1. **Horizontal Axis (Abscissa):** Dry Bulb Temperature in °C, ranging typically from 0°C to 50°C.
2. **Vertical Axis (Ordinate):** Humidity ratio $\omega$ in kg water vapor per kg dry air, ranging from 0 to ~0.030 kg/kg.
3. **Saturation Curve (100% RH Line):** A curved boundary that represents the state of air fully saturated with water vapor. Above this curve, the chart has no physical meaning.
4. **Relative Humidity Lines:** Curves below the saturation curve, labeled for $\phi$ = 90%, 80%, 70%, 50%, 30%, 10%. They diverge as DBT increases.
5. **Wet Bulb Temperature Lines:** Straight diagonal lines that slope upward to the left, intersecting the saturation curve at the WBT value.
6. **Dew Point Temperature Lines:** Horizontal lines (lines of constant $\omega$), since DPT depends only on moisture content.
7. **Enthalpy Lines:** Lines of constant enthalpy, nearly parallel to WBT lines, calibrated in kJ/kg dry air.
8. **Specific Volume Lines:** Steeper than enthalpy lines, calibrated in m³/kg dry air.

**Use of Psychrometric Chart:**

1. To determine any two properties of moist air (e.g., DBT and WBT) and read all other properties (RH, DPT, $\omega$, $h$, $v$).
2. To analyze air conditioning processes: heating, cooling, humidification, dehumidification, mixing of air streams.
3. To compute sensible heat, latent heat, and total heat loads.
4. To determine the apparatus dew point (ADP) and bypass factor of cooling coils.
5. To design duct sizes using specific volume data.

---

**(b) An air-conditioned hall has air entering the cooling coil at 30°C DBT and 22°C WBT. The air leaves the coil at 15°C DBT and 14°C WBT. Calculate:** **(7 Marks)**
**(i) The bypass factor of the coil**
**(ii) The apparatus dew point (ADP)**
**(iii) The total heat removed per kg of dry air**

Take $h_1 = 60.5$ kJ/kg dry air and $h_2 = 39.3$ kJ/kg dry air.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- State 1 (inlet): $T_{db,1} = 30°C$, $T_{wb,1} = 22°C$
- State 2 (outlet): $T_{db,2} = 15°C$, $T_{wb,2} = 14°C$
- $h_1 = 60.5$ kJ/kg dry air, $h_2 = 39.3$ kJ/kg dry air

**(i) Apparatus Dew Point (ADP):**

The ADP is the surface temperature of the cooling coil, which equals the WBT of the leaving air extended to the saturation curve. Given that WBT of outlet is 14°C, and assuming the process line follows the WBT line, the ADP is approximately:

$$T_{ADP} \approx 13°C \text{ (read from chart by extending WBT line of state 2 to saturation curve)}$$

**(ii) Bypass Factor:**

The process follows the WBT line of 14°C. The ADP is the temperature on the saturation curve along this WBT line:

$$BF = \frac{T_{db,2} - T_{ADP}}{T_{db,1} - T_{ADP}}$$

Using the WBT line approximation: For a real coil, the ADP is found such that the WBT line through state 2 passes through it. From the chart, the WBT line of 14°C meets the saturation curve at approximately 13°C, so $T_{ADP} = 13°C$.

$$BF = \frac{15 - 13}{30 - 13} = \frac{2}{17} = 0.118$$

**Note:** A more rigorous approach is to find the WBT line passing through both states 1 and 2, then read the temperature where this line meets the saturation curve. Here, WBT of state 1 is 22°C and WBT of state 2 is 14°C, so they are not on the same WBT line. Therefore, the actual process deviates from a pure WBT line, and ADP is found by extending the line from state 2 backward to the saturation curve along the WBT line of 14°C.

**Assuming standard chart values, $BF \approx 0.12$.**

**(iii) Total Heat Removed per kg of Dry Air:**

$$Q_t = h_1 - h_2 = 60.5 - 39.3 = 21.2 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Define Dew Point Temperature. Derive the relation between specific humidity, partial pressure of water vapor, and total pressure.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Dew Point Temperature (DPT):**

The Dew Point Temperature is the temperature at which moist air becomes saturated (RH = 100%) when it is cooled at constant pressure and constant specific humidity. Below the DPT, water vapor in the air begins to condense into liquid water.

**Derivation of Specific Humidity Relation:**

Consider a closed volume $V$ containing dry air and water vapor at total pressure $P$ and absolute temperature $T$.

**Step 1:** Apply Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures:

$$P = P_a + P_v$$

where $P_a$ = partial pressure of dry air, $P_v$ = partial pressure of water vapor.

**Step 2:** Apply the ideal gas equation to dry air:

$$P_a \cdot V = m_a \cdot R_a \cdot T$$

**Step 3:** Apply the ideal gas equation to water vapor:

$$P_v \cdot V = m_v \cdot R_v \cdot T$$

**Step 4:** Divide equation (3) by equation (2):

$$\frac{P_v}{P_a} = \frac{m_v \cdot R_v}{m_a \cdot R_a}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the mass ratio:

$$\frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{P_v}{P_a} \cdot \frac{R_a}{R_v}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $P_a = P - P_v$ from Step 1:

$$\frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{P_v}{P - P_v} \cdot \frac{R_a}{R_v}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute $R_a = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K and $R_v = 0.4615$ kJ/kg·K, so $R_a / R_v = 0.622$:

$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P - P_v}}$$

This is the required relation between specific humidity, partial pressure of water vapor, and total pressure.

---

**(b) With the help of a line diagram, explain the layout of a Central Air Conditioning System. List the functions of the main components.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Layout of Central Air Conditioning System:**

A central air conditioning system conditions air at a central plant and distributes it to multiple zones through a network of ducts. The main components are:

1. **Outdoor Air Intake (Fresh Air):** Brings in ventilation air from outside, typically with a motorized damper.

2. **Return Air Duct:** Carries warm, stale air from conditioned rooms back to the AHU.

3. **Mixing Chamber / Plenum:** Mixes fresh outdoor air with return air in a controlled ratio (often 10-20% fresh air).

4. **Filters (Pre-filter and Fine Filter):** Remove dust and particulates. Pre-filters catch large particles; fine filters (HEPA/ULPA) catch smaller ones.

5. **Cooling Coil (and Heating Coil):** Cool the air below its dew point (dehumidification) or heat it in winter. Chilled water from the chiller circulates through the cooling coil.

6. **Humidifier:** Adds moisture in winter when heating makes the air too dry. Uses steam injection or water spray.

7. **Supply Fan / Blower:** Provides the pressure to push conditioned air through the ductwork.

8. **Supply Ductwork:** Distributes air to various zones.

9. **Diffusers / Grilles:** Terminal devices that release air into the room with proper throw and drop patterns.

10. **Return Air Grilles:** Pull air from the room back into the return duct.

11. **Chiller Plant:** Produces chilled water using a vapor compression refrigeration cycle. Components: Compressor, Condenser, Expansion Valve, Evaporator.

12. **Cooling Tower:** Rejects heat from the condenser to the atmosphere.

13. **Building Management System (BMS):** Sensors and controllers monitor temperature, humidity, and air quality; control dampers, fans, and chiller capacity.

**Line Diagram:**

[Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.3 of these notes for the schematic layout]

**Function Summary:**

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| Chiller | Produces chilled water for cooling coil |
| Cooling Tower | Rejects condenser heat to atmosphere |
| AHU | Central conditioning unit (filter, coil, fan) |
| Ducts | Transport air to/from rooms |
| BMS | Automatic control of all parameters |

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Psychrometric Questions:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up WBT and DPT:** WBT is always **higher** than DPT for unsaturated air. Many students wrongly write the reverse. WBT represents evaporative cooling effect; DPT represents condensation threshold.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to add 273.15** when using the ideal gas law in Kelvin for temperature-dependent formulas. Some derivations require absolute temperature — re-read the problem.
>
> 3. **Using $h_{fg}$ = 2257 kJ/kg** (latent heat at 100°C) instead of **2501 kJ/kg** (latent heat at 0°C). The psychrometric formula uses $h_{fg,0}$ at 0°C. Wrong value of $h_{fg}$ gives wrong total heat.
>
> 4. **Bypass Factor Confusion:** Bypass Factor is NOT the same as efficiency. A lower BF means BETTER coil performance. CF = 1 - BF is the contact factor (effectiveness).
>
> 5. **Specific Humidity units:** Some students write g/kg or %. The standard unit is **kg of water vapor per kg of dry air** (dimensionless ratio). RH is in %.
>
> 6. **Chart reading errors:** The DPT line is **horizontal**, NOT diagonal. Reading the wrong axis causes up to 3-mark loss.
>
> 7. **Forgetting 0.622 constant:** Many derivations require showing the derivation of 0.622 = $R_a/R_v$. Skipping this step loses process marks.
>
> 8. **In Central AC diagrams, forgetting Cooling Tower and Chiller:** Many students draw only the AHU and ducts. The chiller plant and cooling tower are mandatory for central systems.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1 Psychrometrics & AC**
>
> ✅ **Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT):** True air temperature; measured by a dry thermometer; independent variable on the X-axis of psychrometric chart.
>
> ✅ **Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT):** Temperature attained by adiabatic saturation; measured using a wetted wick; WBT < DBT for unsaturated air; WBT = DBT at saturation.
>
> ✅ **Dew Point Temperature (DPT):** Saturation temperature at constant $\omega$; below DPT, condensation occurs; DPT < WBT < DBT for unsaturated air; DPT is the ADP of a perfect cooling coil.
>
> ✅ **Specific Humidity ($\omega$):** $\omega = 0.622 \cdot P_v / (P - P_v)$; conserved in pure sensible heating/cooling; unit: kg/kg dry air.
>
> ✅ **Relative Humidity ($\phi$):** $\phi = P_v / P_s(T_{db})$; ranges 0-100%; 40-60% is human comfort band; strongly temperature-dependent.
>
> ✅ **Enthalpy of moist air:** $h = 1.006 \cdot T_{db} + \omega(2501 + 1.805 \cdot T_{db})$ kJ/kg dry air; reference 0°C.
>
> ✅ **Sensible Heat:** $Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot 1.006 \cdot \Delta T_{db}$ (kW).
>
> ✅ **Latent Heat:** $Q_l = \dot{m}_a \cdot 2501 \cdot \Delta \omega$ (kW).
>
> ✅ **Total Heat:** $Q_t = Q_s + Q_l = \dot{m}_a \cdot \Delta h$ (kW).
>
> ✅ **Bypass Factor:** $BF = (T_2 - T_{ADP}) / (T_1 - T_{ADP})$; lower is better; CF = 1 - BF.
>
> ✅ **Psychrometric Chart:** DBT on X-axis, $\omega$ on Y-axis; saturation curve is the upper boundary; WBT lines are diagonal, DPT lines are horizontal.
>
> ✅ **Cooling & Dehumidification:** Process line goes down and to the left; occurs when coil surface < DPT; involves both sensible and latent heat removal.
>
> ✅ **Central AC System Components:** Chiller + Cooling Tower + AHU (Filter, Cooling/Heating Coil, Humidifier, Fan) + Ductwork + BMS.
>
> ✅ **Gas Constant Ratio:** $R_a / R_v = 0.287 / 0.4615 = 0.622$ — must show this derivation if asked.
>
> ✅ **Standard Reference Values:** $c_p$ (dry air) = 1.006 kJ/kg·K, $c_p$ (water vapor) = 1.805 kJ/kg·K, $h_{fg,0}$ = 2501 kJ/kg.
>
> ✅ **Order of Temperatures on Saturation Curve (left to right):** DPT → WBT → DBT.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reciprocating Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO MECHANICAL ENGINEERING & CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reciprocating Pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Reciprocating Pump** is a positive-displacement hydraulic machine that converts the mechanical energy of a rotating prime mover (typically an electric motor or IC engine) into hydraulic energy by employing a reciprocating motion of a piston (or plunger) inside a closed cylinder. Liquid is drawn into the cylinder through a **suction valve** during the suction (retraction) stroke and is displaced into the delivery pipe through a **delivery (discharge) valve** during the delivery (forward) stroke.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"A reciprocating pump is a positive displacement pump in which a definite quantity of liquid is delivered for every stroke of the piston, irrespective of the head against which it operates (within the structural limits of the pump)."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **medical syringe**. When you pull the plunger outward, the medicine rushes in through the needle (suction stroke). When you push the plunger inward, the medicine is forced out through the needle (delivery stroke). A reciprocating pump is simply a **mechanically powered, heavy-duty industrial version of this syringe** — typically driven by a crankshaft connected to a piston rod.

| Analogy Element | Real-World Object | Reciprocating Pump Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hand pulling plunger | Finger / thumb | Piston retraction (suction stroke) |
| Hand pushing plunger | Finger / thumb | Piston forward motion (delivery stroke) |
| Medicine sucked in | Liquid entering | Suction through suction valve |
| Medicine expelled out | Liquid leaving | Delivery through delivery valve |
| Needle one-way flow | Non-return action | Foot valve / Delivery valve |

### 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants & Metrics

The following **standard reference values** are used throughout KTU-level derivations and numerical problems for water (the most common pumped fluid):

- **Density of water ($\rho$):** **$1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$**
- **Acceleration due to gravity ($g$):** **$9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**
- **Specific weight of water ($w = \rho g$):** **$9810 \text{ N/m}^3$**
- **Atmospheric pressure head equivalent:** **$10.3 \text{ m}$ of water column** (standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this a "Positive Displacement" pump?**
> Unlike a centrifugal pump (which imparts kinetic energy), a reciprocating pump *traps* a fixed volume of liquid and *physically pushes* it into the discharge line. Therefore, **discharge is a function of piston geometry (Area × Length × Speed)** and is *almost independent* of the delivery head.

---

### 1.4 Classification Snapshot

Reciprocating pumps are classified broadly by:

1. **Number of cylinders** → Single-cylinder or Multi-cylinder
2. **Type of water contact** → Piston-type or Plunger-type
3. **Action per cycle** → **Single-acting** (delivery only on one stroke) or **Double-acting** (delivery on both strokes)
4. **Drive mechanism** → Crank-and-connecting rod (most common) or Cam-driven

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** A question on *"Difference between single-acting and double-acting pump"* or *"Explain the working of a single-acting reciprocating pump with a neat sketch"* carries high weightage. Always draw a labeled diagram.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Main Components of a Reciprocating Pump

A complete reciprocating pump assembly consists of the following sub-systems:

- **Suction Pipe:** Connects the sump/source to the pump cylinder. Liquid travels up this pipe during the suction stroke.
- **Delivery Pipe:** Carries the displaced liquid from the cylinder to the delivery tank/reservoir.
- **Suction Valve (Foot Valve):** A one-way (non-return) valve at the bottom of the suction pipe. *Opens* during suction stroke; *closes* during delivery stroke.
- **Delivery Valve (Reflux Valve):** A one-way valve on the cylinder head. *Closes* during suction stroke; *opens* during delivery stroke.
- **Cylinder with Piston/Plunger:** The heart of the pump where reciprocating motion occurs.
- **Crank and Connecting Rod Mechanism:** Converts rotary motion of the prime mover into linear (reciprocating) motion of the piston.
- **Air Vessel (Accumulator):** A closed chamber containing air (compressed) on top of the water. Placed on the suction and/or delivery pipe to smoothen flow pulsations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Vessel — Why does it matter in KTU problems?**
> The air vessel is a critical component. Without it, the flow in the suction and delivery pipes is *pulsating* (zero velocity at the end of each stroke, max velocity at mid-stroke). With the air vessel, flow is *almost steady*, which drastically reduces the acceleration head losses. Numerical problems in KTU frequently ask you to compute the **position of the air vessel** to make the acceleration head zero in the pipe.

## 2.2 Working Cycle (Single-Acting Pump)

The operating cycle has two strokes for **every revolution** of the crankshaft:

| Stroke | Piston Motion | Suction Valve | Delivery Valve | Fluid Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Suction Stroke** | Retracts (away from head) | **OPEN** | CLOSED | Liquid is sucked from sump into cylinder |
| **Delivery Stroke** | Advances (toward head) | CLOSED | **OPEN** | Liquid is pushed from cylinder into delivery pipe |

> [!NOTE]
> In a single-acting pump, **liquid is delivered only during one stroke per revolution** (the delivery stroke). Hence discharge is lower compared to a double-acting pump.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown — Why Each Formula Exists

The derivations in Section 3 hinge on three logical anchors:

1. **Volume swept per stroke** = Cross-sectional area of piston × Stroke length = $A \times L$
2. **Number of delivery strokes per second** depends on pump type and crank RPM ($N$).
3. **Discharge (Q)** = Volume swept per stroke × Number of delivery strokes per second.

Following the same pattern, the **work done** is computed by integrating the force on the piston over the stroke length, where the force = pressure × area = $\rho g H \times A$ (with $H$ being the head).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following markdown table contains **all exam-critical formulas**. KTU 2024 numericals are *always* solvable using a subset of these.

| # | Parameter | Single-Acting Pump | Double-Acting Pump | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Volume per stroke | $V = A \cdot L$ | $V = A \cdot L$ | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 2 | Delivery strokes per second | $\dfrac{N}{60}$ | $\dfrac{2N}{60}$ | $\text{strokes/s}$ |
| 3 | **Theoretical Discharge $Q_{th}$** | $\dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | $\dfrac{2 \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 4 | **Actual Discharge $Q_{act}$** | $Q_{th} - Q_{slip}$ | $Q_{th} - Q_{slip}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 5 | **Coefficient of Discharge $C_d$** | $\dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | $\dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | **Slip (as a percentage)** | $\dfrac{(Q_{th} - Q_{act})}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ | Same as left | $\vert \cdot \vert$ value in $\vert \cdot \vert$ |
| 7 | Work done per second (no friction, no acc. head) | $\rho g \cdot \dfrac{A L N}{60} \cdot H_s$ | $\rho g \cdot \dfrac{2 A L N}{60} \cdot H_s$ | $\text{Watts (W)}$ |
| 8 | Work done per second (with friction head $h_f$ in delivery) | $\rho g \cdot \dfrac{A L N}{60} \cdot (H_s + h_f)$ | $\rho g \cdot \dfrac{2 A L N}{60} \cdot (H_s + h_f)$ | $\text{Watts (W)}$ |
| 9 | **Shaft Power (Brake Power)** | $\dfrac{\text{Work done per second}}{\eta_{\text{mech}}}$ | Same logic | $\text{W}$ |
| 10 | **Acceleration Head $H_a$** | $\dfrac{L}{g} \cdot \dfrac{A}{a} \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \cos\theta$ | Same expression with $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ | $\text{m of water}$ |
| 11 | Condition for **zero acceleration head in delivery pipe** (air vessel placed at height $h_1$ from cylinder, total pipe length $H_d$) | $h_1 = \dfrac{H_d}{2}$ | Same | $\text{m}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Used (Standard KTU Convention):**
> - $A$ = cross-sectional area of piston ($\text{m}^2$)
> - $a$ = cross-sectional area of pipe ($\text{m}^2$)
> - $L$ = stroke length ($\text{m}$)
> - $N$ = crank speed (rpm)
> - $H_s$ = static head = suction head + delivery head ($\text{m}$)
> - $h_f$ = friction head loss ($\text{m}$)
> - $\omega$ = angular velocity of crank = $\dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s
> - $r$ = crank radius = $L/2$
> - $H_a$ = acceleration head (m)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Reciprocating pumps are the **preferred choice** in applications demanding **high pressure at low discharge**, such as:
- **Hydraulic presses** in manufacturing
- **Oil wells** (oil-field mud pumps and beam pumps)
- **Boiler feed** in thermal power stations (high-pressure water injection)
- **Chemical dosing** and metering (precise volume delivery)
- **Fire-fighting pumps** and high-rise building water supply
- **Sludge and viscous fluid handling** in water-treatment plants

> [!TIP]
> **Centrifugal vs Reciprocating — KTU Exam Comparison:**
> - **Centrifugal**: Continuous flow, lower head at high discharge, priming required.
> - **Reciprocating**: Pulsating flow, high head at low discharge, self-priming (foot valve), no priming air vessel issue.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **complete, examiner-style derivations** that are directly aligned with KTU valuation expectations. Every algebraic step is shown with the corresponding physical interpretation in plain English.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Discharge of a Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump

**Given:** A single-acting reciprocating pump with piston area $A$ ($\text{m}^2$), stroke length $L$ ($\text{m}$), and crank speed $N$ (rpm).

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry of one delivery stroke.**
The piston sweeps a cylindrical volume during one delivery stroke equal to the cross-sectional area times the axial length of travel.

$$
V_{\text{stroke}} = A \cdot L
$$

This is the volume of liquid displaced in a single delivery stroke.

**Step 2 — Determine the number of delivery strokes per second.**
Since the pump is **single-acting**, exactly one delivery stroke occurs per revolution. If the crank rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, the number of revolutions per second is $N/60$.

$$
n_{\text{strokes/s}} = \frac{N}{60}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply to obtain the theoretical discharge.**
Theoretical discharge is the volume delivered per unit time. Multiplying the volume per stroke by the number of strokes per second:

$$
Q_{th} = V_{\text{stroke}} \times n_{\text{strokes/s}} = A \cdot L \cdot \frac{N}{60}
$$

Therefore, the **theoretical discharge of a single-acting reciprocating pump is:**

$$
\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Theoretical Discharge of a Double-Acting Reciprocating Pump

**Given:** A double-acting reciprocating pump has two delivery strokes per revolution (one in the front chamber, one in the rear chamber, ignoring the piston-rod volume for the standard case).

**Step 1 — Note the structural difference.**
In a double-acting pump, the piston works on **both sides of the piston face**, delivering liquid in *both* halves of every revolution.

**Step 2 — Adjust the stroke count.**
Therefore, the number of delivery strokes per second is doubled:

$$
n_{\text{strokes/s}} = \frac{2N}{60}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the discharge equation.**

$$
Q_{th} = 2 \cdot A \cdot L \cdot \frac{N}{60}
$$

Therefore, the **theoretical discharge of a double-acting reciprocating pump is:**

$$
\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{2 \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Slip and Coefficient of Discharge

**Definition of Slip:** In a real pump, the actual discharge is *always slightly less* than the theoretical discharge. This shortfall is called **Slip** and is caused by:
- Leakage past the piston (cylinder clearance)
- Delayed closure/early opening of valves
- Compression of air trapped at pump summits

**Step 1 — Define slip discharge.**
By definition:

$$
Q_{slip} = Q_{th} - Q_{act}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the coefficient of discharge.**
The dimensionless ratio of actual to theoretical discharge:

$$
C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}
$$

**Step 3 — Express slip as a percentage** (a standard KTU question form):

$$
\text{Slip \%} = \left( \frac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \right) \times 100
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative slip (a KTU board favourite):** Under high heads, the delivery valve may open *late* in the delivery stroke, and the pump may not deliver its full theoretical volume. In some cases, the actual discharge can *exceed* the theoretical, giving **negative slip**. This occurs when the head is very high relative to the suction conditions.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Work Done by the Pump (Frictionless Case)

**Assumption:** No friction head loss; no acceleration head loss. Total head = static head = $H_s$.

**Step 1 — Force on the piston during the delivery stroke.**
The liquid column of height $H_s$ exerts hydrostatic pressure on the piston. The pressure at the piston is $P = \rho g H_s$, so the force on the piston area $A$ is:

$$
F = P \cdot A = \rho g H_s \cdot A
$$

**Step 2 — Work done in one delivery stroke.**
Work = Force × Distance moved. The piston travels distance $L$ during the stroke.

$$
W_{\text{stroke}} = F \cdot L = \rho g H_s A L
$$

**Step 3 — Work done per second (multiply by strokes per second).**
For a single-acting pump, the strokes per second is $N/60$:

$$
W_{\text{sec}} = \rho g H_s A L \cdot \frac{N}{60}
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{\text{sec, single}} = \rho g \cdot \frac{A L N}{60} \cdot H_s \;\; \text{W}}
$$

For a double-acting pump (factor of 2):

$$
\boxed{W_{\text{sec, double}} = 2 \cdot \rho g \cdot \frac{A L N}{60} \cdot H_s \;\; \text{W}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Work Done Including Friction Head

**Modification:** If a friction head loss $h_f$ is present in the delivery pipe, the *effective* head the pump must work against is $H_s + h_f$.

**Step 1 — Replace $H_s$ with the total dynamic head.**

$$
W_{\text{sec, single}} = \rho g \cdot \frac{A L N}{60} \cdot (H_s + h_f)
$$

This is the **hydraulic power** delivered to the fluid. The brake power (shaft power input) is higher due to mechanical losses, so:

$$
P_{\text{brake}} = \frac{W_{\text{sec, single}}}{\eta_{\text{mech}}}
$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Acceleration Head and Indicator Diagram

**The physical problem:** When the piston moves, the liquid in the pipe must also accelerate. Because the liquid has mass (and therefore inertia), extra pressure is required to accelerate it. This extra equivalent head is called the **Acceleration Head $H_a$**.

**Step 1 — Velocity of liquid in the pipe.**
If the piston moves with velocity $v_p$, the volume flow rate is $A \cdot v_p$. By continuity in the pipe of area $a$:

$$
v_{\text{pipe}} = \frac{A \cdot v_p}{a}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate to obtain acceleration of the liquid in the pipe.**

$$
a_{\text{pipe}} = \frac{dv_{\text{pipe}}}{dt} = \frac{A}{a} \cdot \frac{dv_p}{dt}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the piston velocity using crank kinematics.**
With crank radius $r$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$, the piston displacement is $x = r(1 - \cos\theta)$, and velocity is $v_p = r \omega \sin\theta$. Differentiating:

$$
\frac{dv_p}{dt} = r \omega^2 \cos\theta
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back to find pipe acceleration.**

$$
a_{\text{pipe}} = \frac{A}{a} \cdot r \omega^2 \cos\theta
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to equivalent pressure head.**
The pressure head equivalent of an acceleration $a$ in a column of water of length equivalent to the pipe is $H_a = \dfrac{L_{\text{eff}} \cdot a_{\text{pipe}}}{g}$. Combining:

$$
\boxed{H_a = \frac{L}{g} \cdot \frac{A}{a} \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \cos\theta \;\; \text{m}}
$$

**Step 6 — Indicator Diagram Insight.**
The indicator diagram is a plot of pressure head (vertical axis) vs. piston displacement (horizontal axis). Three key curves appear on a KTU problem:

| Curve | When Plotted | Shape |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Ideal (no acceleration, no friction)** | Theoretical | Perfect rectangle of height $H_s$ |
| **With acceleration, no friction** | Real | Loops above and below the ideal line |
| **With acceleration and friction** | Real (typical) | Loops *asymmetric* (more loss on delivery side) |

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Problem Solver Checklist

| KTU Problem Type | What to Find | Key Formulas Needed |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Given $A, L, N$ → Find $Q_{th}$ | Theoretical discharge | $Q_{th} = ALN/60$ (single) or $2ALN/60$ (double) |
| Given $Q_{th}, Q_{act}$ → Find $C_d$ and slip | Coefficient of discharge, slip \% | $C_d = Q_{act}/Q_{th}$ |
| Given $A, L, N, H_s$ → Find work done | Hydraulic power | $W = \rho g (ALN/60) H_s$ |
| Given $h_f$ additionally | Power with friction | $W = \rho g (ALN/60)(H_s + h_f)$ |
| Given crank radius, $N$ → Find $H_a$ | Acceleration head | $H_a = (L/g)(A/a)\omega^2 r \cos\theta$ |
| Position of air vessel → zero acc. head in delivery | $h_1 = H_d/2$ | Air vessel at midpoint of delivery pipe |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section provides professional-grade diagrams built in **Mermaid** to visually anchor every theoretical concept. Due to Mermaid's rendering constraints, physical valves and pistons are represented via **labeled flow blocks** that clearly indicate functional roles and signal flow.

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Complete Working of a Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Sump or Source Reservoir] --> SP[Suction Pipe]
    SP --> SV{Foot Valve / Suction Valve Non Return}
    SV --> CYL[Cylinder with Piston]
    CYL --> DV{Delivery Valve Non Return}
    DV --> DP[Delivery Pipe]
    DP --> AV[Air Vessel on Delivery Side Smoothens Pulsations]
    AV --> DT[Delivery Tank or Higher Elevation Outlet]
    CR[Crank and Connecting Rod Mechanism Driven by Motor] --> CYL
    ASV[Air Vessel on Suction Side] --> SP
```

**Reading the diagram (left-to-right flow of liquid):**
The liquid originates at the **sump**, travels up the **suction pipe** (through the foot valve), fills the **cylinder** on the piston retraction, is pushed through the **delivery valve** on the piston advance, and finally flows into the **delivery tank**. The **air vessels** (one on each side) act as pneumatic cushions, storing energy at peak flow and releasing it at zero flow, thereby smoothing pulsations.

## 4.2 State Machine — Valve Operation Through One Crank Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    state SUCTION {
        direction LR
        SUCTION_NOTE: Piston Retracts
        FOOT_OPEN: Foot Valve OPEN
        DELIV_CLOSED: Delivery Valve CLOSED
        LIQ_IN: Liquid Enters Cylinder
    }
    state DELIVERY {
        direction LR
        DELIV_NOTE: Piston Advances
        FOOT_CLOSED: Foot Valve CLOSED
        DELIV_OPEN: Delivery Valve OPEN
        LIQ_OUT: Liquid Exits Cylinder
    }
    [*] --> SUCTION : Crank at 0 deg
    SUCTION --> DELIVERY : Crank at 180 deg
    DELIVERY --> [*] : One Cycle Complete
```

**State machine interpretation:** The pump toggles between two exclusive states. In the **suction** state, the foot valve is open and the delivery valve is shut; in the **delivery** state, the situation is reversed. This bistable behaviour is the hallmark of any positive-displacement pump.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Comparison of Pump Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SA[Single Acting Pump]
        direction TB
        A1[Cylinder One Side Active] --> A2[One Delivery Stroke per Revolution]
        A2 --> A3[Q equals A L N over 60]
    end
    subgraph DA[Double Acting Pump]
        direction TB
        B1[Cylinder Both Sides Active] --> B2[Two Delivery Strokes per Revolution]
        B2 --> B3[Q equals 2 A L N over 60]
    end
    subgraph CC[Centrifugal Pump Reference]
        direction TB
        C1[Continuous Impeller Rotation] --> C2[Flow Proportional to Speed]
        C2 --> C3[Requires Priming]
    end
    SA --> COMPARE((KTU Comparison Matrix))
    DA --> COMPARE
    CC --> COMPARE
```

**Reading the comparison block:** The single-acting, double-acting, and centrifugal pumps are placed in three parallel subgraphs. KTU's comparison matrix would highlight that the **double-acting** doubles the discharge without increasing crank speed — a key efficiency improvement used in industrial reciprocating pumps.

## 4.4 Indicator Diagram Schematic (Block-Representation of Pressure vs. Piston Displacement)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Start of Suction Stroke] --> P2[Middle of Suction Stroke Min Head = Hs minus Ha]
    P2 --> P3[End of Suction Stroke Max Head = Hs plus Ha]
    P3 --> P4[Start of Delivery Stroke Max Head = Hs plus Ha]
    P4 --> P5[Middle of Delivery Stroke Ideal Head = Hs]
    P5 --> P6[End of Delivery Stroke Min Head = Hs minus Ha]
    P6 --> P1
```

**Interpretation:** The loop on the indicator diagram represents one full cycle. The **vertical distance** between the upper and lower curves at any point is $2H_a$ — the maximum swing due to acceleration. With an air vessel placed at the right position, this loop becomes a horizontal line at $H_s$, indicating zero acceleration head.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sketching Note for KTU Board Exam:** While Mermaid is used here for digital clarity, in the actual examination, you are expected to **draw a freehand labeled sketch** of the pump, marking the suction pipe, delivery pipe, foot valve, delivery valve, cylinder, piston, crank, and air vessels. Label arrows indicating liquid flow direction. This sketch alone can earn **3 to 4 marks** in a 14-mark question.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Reciprocating Pump. Mention its Main Parts. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A reciprocating pump is a **positive-displacement pump** in which a definite quantity of liquid is delivered for every stroke of the piston, independent of the head against which it operates (within structural limits).

**Main Parts (2 marks for the list):**
1. Suction pipe with foot valve
2. Delivery pipe with delivery (reflux) valve
3. Cylinder with piston (or plunger)
4. Crank and connecting rod mechanism
5. Air vessels (suction and delivery side)
6. Priming arrangement (foot valve holds prime)

---

### Question 2: Differentiate Between Single-Acting and Double-Acting Reciprocating Pumps. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**Model Answer (in tabular form for 3 marks):**

| Parameter | Single-Acting | Double-Acting |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Delivery strokes per revolution | 1 | 2 |
| Theoretical discharge | $ALN/60$ | $2ALN/60$ |
| Cylinder design | Simple | Has a gland / piston rod on both sides |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Discharge smoothness | More pulsating | Less pulsating |
| Typical application | Small water supply | High-pressure boiler feed |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Part B questions are worth **14 marks** and typically split as **7 marks for sub-part (a) and 7 marks for sub-part (b)**. The internal choice means students must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** A single-acting reciprocating pump has a bore of 150 mm and a stroke of 300 mm. The pump runs at 60 rpm and delivers water against a static head of 30 m. Calculate:
- (i) Theoretical discharge
- (ii) Work done per second
- (iii) Power required (assuming 100% efficiency)

**(b)** If the actual discharge is 4.5 L/s, determine the slip and the coefficient of discharge. Take friction head = 2 m.

**Full Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Numerical**

**Given Data:**
- Bore $D = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- Stroke $L = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.30 \text{ m}$
- Speed $N = 60 \text{ rpm}$
- Static head $H_s = 30 \text{ m}$
- Friction head $h_f = 2 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Calculate piston cross-sectional area $A$.**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.15)^2
$$

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0225 = 0.01767 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the theoretical discharge $Q_{th}$.**

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} = \frac{0.01767 \times 0.30 \times 60}{60}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = 0.01767 \times 0.30 = 0.00530 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 5.30 \;\; \text{L/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the work done per second (hydraulic power).**
With friction head included:

$$
W_{\text{sec}} = \rho g \cdot Q_{th} \cdot (H_s + h_f)
$$

$$
W_{\text{sec}} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.00530 \times (30 + 2)
$$

$$
W_{\text{sec}} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.00530 \times 32 = 1664.2 \;\; \text{W}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kilowatts.**

$$
P = \frac{W_{\text{sec}}}{1000} = 1.664 \;\; \text{kW} \approx 1.66 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

**Part (b) — Slip and Coefficient of Discharge**

**Step 5 — Compute the slip discharge.**

$$
Q_{slip} = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 5.30 - 4.50 = 0.80 \;\; \text{L/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Slip percentage.**

$$
\text{Slip \%} = \frac{0.80}{5.30} \times 100 = 15.09\%
$$

**Step 7 — Coefficient of discharge.**

$$
C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{4.50}{5.30} = 0.849
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students forget to include the friction head $h_f$ in the work-done calculation. KTU valuation strictly docks 1 mark for missing the $(H_s + h_f)$ term. Always add friction head to the *total* head when computing work/power.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a single-acting reciprocating pump. Label all parts.

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **acceleration head** in the suction/delivery pipe of a reciprocating pump. Show how the position of an air vessel on the delivery pipe is determined such that the acceleration head in the delivery pipe becomes zero.

**Full Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Descriptive (7 marks)**

The single-acting reciprocating pump works in two strokes per revolution:

1. **Suction Stroke:** As the crank rotates and the connecting rod pulls the piston backward, a partial vacuum is created inside the cylinder. Atmospheric pressure on the sump surface pushes the liquid up the suction pipe, opening the foot (suction) valve. The delivery valve remains closed due to the weight of the liquid column above it.

2. **Delivery Stroke:** As the crank continues and the connecting rod pushes the piston forward, pressure inside the cylinder rises. The foot valve closes (preventing backflow), and when pressure exceeds the delivery head plus the weight of the delivery valve, the delivery valve opens. Liquid is forced up the delivery pipe into the outlet.

**Sketch (to be drawn in exam):** Cylinder horizontal with piston inside, suction pipe on left (vertical, leading to sump with foot valve), delivery pipe on right (vertical, leading to delivery tank with delivery valve on the cylinder head). Mark the crank at the bottom, connected via the connecting rod to the piston rod. Label all 6 main components.

**Part (b) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Velocity of water in the delivery pipe.**
Let the piston area be $A$ and the delivery pipe area be $a$. By continuity:

$$
v = \frac{A \cdot v_p}{a}
$$

where $v_p$ is the instantaneous piston velocity.

**Step 2 — Acceleration of water in the pipe.**

$$
\text{acc} = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{A}{a} \cdot \frac{dv_p}{dt}
$$

**Step 3 — Piston velocity from crank kinematics.**
With crank radius $r = L/2$ and angular velocity $\omega$:

$$
v_p = r \omega \sin\theta = \frac{L \omega}{2} \sin\theta
$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate the piston velocity.**

$$
\frac{dv_p}{dt} = \frac{L \omega}{2} \cdot \omega \cos\theta = \frac{L \omega^2}{2} \cos\theta
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the acceleration expression.**

$$
\text{acc} = \frac{A}{a} \cdot \frac{L \omega^2}{2} \cos\theta
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to head (acceleration head).**

$$
H_a = \frac{L_{\text{eff}}}{g} \cdot \text{acc} = \frac{L}{g} \cdot \frac{A}{a} \cdot \frac{L \omega^2}{2} \cos\theta
$$

**Step 7 — Condition for zero acceleration head in the delivery pipe.**
When an air vessel is placed on the delivery pipe at a height $h_1$ from the cylinder (with total delivery pipe length $H_d$), the effective accelerating length of the water column becomes $H_d - h_1$ during the delivery stroke.

The acceleration head in the *upper* portion of the delivery pipe (above the air vessel) is:

$$
H_{a,\text{upper}} = \frac{H_d - h_1}{g} \cdot \frac{A}{a} \cdot \omega^2 \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cos\theta
$$

The acceleration head in the *lower* portion (between cylinder and air vessel) is:

$$
H_{a,\text{lower}} = \frac{h_1}{g} \cdot \frac{A}{a} \cdot \omega^2 \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cos\theta
$$

For the **net acceleration head to be zero**, the two must be equal and opposite (the upper portion is accelerating downward, the lower portion is accelerating upward). Setting the magnitudes equal:

$$
h_1 = H_d - h_1
$$

$$
\boxed{h_1 = \frac{H_d}{2}}
$$

Therefore, the **air vessel should be placed at the midpoint of the delivery pipe** to completely nullify the acceleration head in the delivery pipe.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall in (b):** Many students confuse the angular velocity formula. KTU expects $\omega = 2\pi N/60$. Marks are lost if $\omega$ is left as a variable without the explicit numerical conversion. Also, ensure you state clearly that the *direction* of acceleration reverses on either side of the air vessel — this is the key physical insight.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Use this section as a 2-minute pre-exam revision glance.**

- A **reciprocating pump** is a **positive-displacement** pump delivering a **fixed volume per stroke**, almost independent of head.
- **Single-acting:** $Q_{th} = \dfrac{ALN}{60}$ ; **Double-acting:** $Q_{th} = \dfrac{2ALN}{60}$
- **Slip** = $Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ (always positive in normal operation; can be **negative** at very high heads).
- **Coefficient of discharge** $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$; typical range for a healthy pump: **0.85 to 0.99**.
- **Work done per second (single-acting, no friction):** $W = \rho g \cdot \dfrac{ALN}{60} \cdot H_s$
- **With friction:** Replace $H_s$ with $(H_s + h_f)$.
- **Acceleration head formula:** $H_a = \dfrac{L}{g} \cdot \dfrac{A}{a} \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \cos\theta$
- **Air vessel placement** to nullify acceleration head in delivery pipe → **midpoint**, i.e., $h_1 = H_d/2$. Similarly $h_s = H_s/2$ for the suction side.
- **Crank radius** $r = L/2$ (standard KTU assumption unless otherwise stated).
- **Angular velocity** $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s.
- **Indicator diagram** shows a rectangular loop in the ideal case, with smoothening achievable via air vessels.
- **Pulsation frequency** of flow in a single-acting pump = $N/60$ Hz. In a double-acting pump = $2N/60$ Hz.
- **Standard water constants:** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$.
- **Conversion tip:** $1 \text{ L/s} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ and $1 \text{ kW} = 1000 \text{ W}$.
- **Final sanity check:** If the calculated slip is negative, re-check the head values — high heads can cause negative slip due to valve delays.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for the 14-Mark Question:**
> **"PASCALS"** — **P**iston geometry ($AL$) → **A**rea ratio ($A/a$) → **S**peed ($N$ to $\omega$) → **C**rank radius ($L/2$) → **A**ir vessel at midpoint → **L**iquid inertia ($H_a$ from $L/g$) → **S**lip and $C_d$ from $Q_{th}, Q_{act}$.
> If you can recall this acronym, the entire 14-mark answer is structured in your head before you even start writing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Centrifugal Pump; Classification of Hydraulic Turbines; Different Types of Gears and Their Applications (Spur, Helical, Bevel, Worm and Worm Wheel)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO MECHANICAL ENGINEERING & CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Centrifugal Pump; Classification of Hydraulic Turbines; Different Types of Gears and Their Applications (Spur, Helical, Bevel, Worm and Worm Wheel)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Centrifugal Pump

### Formal KTU-Standard Definition
A **Centrifugal Pump** is a **rotodynamic machine** that uses a **rotating impeller** to impart **kinetic energy (velocity energy)** to the fluid, which is subsequently converted into **pressure energy** in the volute/casing, thereby raising the fluid from a lower level to a higher level. It operates on the principle of **forced vortex flow**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The centrifugal pump is a **turbomachine** — students must clearly distinguish it from **positive displacement pumps** (reciprocating/rotary) which trap a fixed volume of fluid and push it out.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a child swinging a bucket of water in a circular path overhead — the water presses hard against the bottom of the bucket due to centrifugal force and stays inside even when upside down. Now imagine the bucket has **holes on its rim**: as it spins fast, water is flung **outward** through these holes. 

A centrifugal pump does exactly the same thing:
- The **impeller** is the spinning "bucket."
- The **casing (volute)** captures the water thrown outward and slows it down, converting high-velocity water into high-pressure water that rises up the delivery pipe.

### Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics
- **Specific gravity of water (at 4°C):** $1.0$ (dimensionless)
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Density of water:** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Mass flow rate:** $\dot{m} = \rho \times Q$ (kg/s)
- **Shaft power formula:** $P = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{1000}$ (kW) for $H$ in m, $Q$ in m³/s

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** Pressure head $h_p = \dfrac{p}{\rho g}$ — always express pressure as metres of water column in fluid mechanics problems for the KTU board exam.

---

## 1.2 Hydraulic Turbines

### Formal KTU-Standard Definition
A **Hydraulic Turbine** is a **rotodynamic fluid machine** that converts the **hydraulic (potential + kinetic + pressure) energy of flowing water** into **mechanical energy (rotational shaft work)**, which is then used to drive an electric generator or other mechanical load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** A pump *adds* energy to a fluid. A turbine *extracts* energy from a fluid. Both are turbomachines, but energy flow directions are opposite.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Picture water at the top of a hill behind a dam. It has enormous **potential energy**. If you let it rush down a pipe and aim it at the curved blades of a wheel, the wheel starts spinning — that's a turbine capturing the falling water's energy to do useful work (like generating electricity).

### Classification Snapshot
Hydraulic turbines are classified on **four** independent bases:
1. **Action vs. Reaction** (energy transfer mechanism)
2. **Direction of water flow** through the runner
3. **Head available** (high, medium, low)
4. **Specific speed** ($N_s$)

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU Module 2, focus on the **action-reaction** and **head-based** classification. The Pelton wheel is the most common exam question.

---

## 1.3 Gears (Spur, Helical, Bevel, Worm & Worm Wheel)

### Formal KTU-Standard Definition
A **Gear** is a **rotating machine element** having **cut teeth** (or cogs) which mesh with another toothed element to **transmit torque and motion** between shafts, without slip. Gears are positive (no slip) drives and provide a **fixed velocity ratio**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Law of Gearing (Conjugate Action)** states: For constant velocity ratio, the common normal at the point of contact must always pass through a **fixed point on the line joining the centres of rotation** — this fixed point is called the **pitch point**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of two bicycle sprockets connected by a chain. The teeth on the sprockets "catch" each other to push the wheel around. Gears do the same thing — but instead of a chain, the teeth **mesh directly**, like two saw blades interlocking. By using small/large gears, you can either speed up (more speed, less torque) or slow down (more torque, less speed) the rotation.

### Quick Gear Family Overview
| Gear Type | Shaft Orientation | Power Range | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spur** | Parallel | Low–Medium | Automobile gearboxes, clocks, small pumps |
| **Helical** | Parallel (but teeth angled) | Medium–High | High-speed gearboxes, turbines |
| **Bevel** | Intersecting (90° typical) | Medium | Differentials, hand drills |
| **Worm & Worm Wheel** | Non-intersecting, perpendicular (skew) | Low–Medium | Lifts, conveyors, steering systems |

> [!NOTE]
> All gear formulas are based on the **Module ($m$)** system or the **Circular Pitch ($p_c$)** system, where the pitch circle is the imaginary circle on which two gears effectively roll. For KTU exams, stick to the **module system** as it is the modern standard.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Centrifugal Pump — Working, Heads, Efficiencies

### Main Parts (from KTU Board perspective)
1. **Impeller** — The rotating wheel with curved/backswept vanes (the "heart" of the pump)
2. **Casing (Volute)** — Spiral-shaped chamber that collects water and converts velocity into pressure
3. **Suction Pipe** — Connects water source to the eye (centre) of the impeller
4. **Delivery Pipe** — Carries pressurized water from the casing outlet to the discharge
5. **Foot Valve** — One-way valve at the bottom of the suction pipe; prevents back-flow when pump is off
6. **Strainer (Foot Strainer)** — Filters debris to protect the impeller
7. **Priming Tank / Air Vent** — Removes air from the casing before starting

### Step-by-Step Working Logic
- **Step 1 — Priming:** The casing and suction pipe are completely filled with water (no air). This is essential because a centrifugal pump cannot suck air.
- **Step 2 — Suction:** The impeller rotates at high speed, creating a low-pressure region (partial vacuum) at its **eye (centre)**. Atmospheric pressure on the water surface in the sump pushes water up the suction pipe.
- **Step 3 — Energy Addition:** Water enters the impeller eye and is flung outward by centrifugal action. The impeller imparts **kinetic energy** to the water.
- **Step 4 — Pressure Conversion:** In the **volute casing**, the cross-sectional area gradually increases, causing the water's velocity to drop. By **Bernoulli's principle**, this drop in velocity is converted into a rise in **pressure**.
- **Step 5 — Delivery:** The high-pressure water is pushed up the delivery pipe to the required height.

### Various Heads in a Centrifugal Pump
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Topic:** Memorize the relationship between these heads — almost every numerical will ask you to identify and combine them.

| Head Symbol | Name | Definition | Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| $H_s$ | **Suction Head** | Vertical height of pump centre above water source | — |
| $H_d$ | **Delivery Head** | Vertical height of delivery point above pump centre | — |
| $H_{stat}$ | **Static Head** | Total vertical lift, ignoring friction | $H_{stat} = H_s + H_d$ |
| $h_{fs}$ | **Frictional Head Loss** (suction side) | Energy lost in suction pipe friction | — |
| $h_{fd}$ | **Frictional Head Loss** (delivery side) | Energy lost in delivery pipe friction | — |
| $H_f$ | **Total Friction Loss** | Combined friction losses | $H_f = h_{fs} + h_{fd}$ |
| $H_m$ | **Manometric Head** | Head developed by the impeller alone | $H_m = H_s + H_d + H_f$ |
| $V^2/2g$ | **Velocity Head at delivery** | Kinetic head of water leaving delivery pipe | $V_d^2/2g$ |
| $H$ | **Total Head** | Pump's effective output head | $H = H_m + \dfrac{V_d^2}{2g}$ |

### Efficiencies
1. **Manometric Efficiency** ($\eta_{man}$): The ratio of manometric head to the head imparted by the impeller.
   - $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{\text{Head actually imparted to water}}{\text{Head imparted by impeller}} = \dfrac{gH_m}{V_{w2} u_2}$

2. **Mechanical Efficiency** ($\eta_{mech}$): The ratio of shaft power (input to impeller) to the power supplied by the prime mover.
   - $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{\text{Shaft power}}{\text{Power supplied to pump}}$

3. **Overall Efficiency** ($\eta_{overall}$): The ratio of useful power output (water) to the power input (electric motor/engine).
   - $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{mech}$

### KTU Formula Sheet — Centrifugal Pump
> [!IMPORTANT]
> All velocities are at the **impeller outlet (subscript 2)** unless otherwise stated. $u_2$ is the **tangential (blade) velocity**, $V_{w2}$ is the **whirl component (tangential absolute velocity)**, and $V_{r2}$ is the **relative velocity**.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Blade velocity at outlet | $u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ | m/s |
| 2 | Work done per unit weight | $W = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$ | m |
| 3 | Manometric head (Euler's) | $H_m = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$ | m |
| 4 | Water Power (output) | $P_{water} = \rho g Q H_m$ | W |
| 5 | Shaft Power (input to impeller) | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{water}}{\eta_{man}}$ | W |
| 6 | Overall Power input | $P_{input} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{\eta_{overall}}$ | W |
| 7 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ | kg/s |
| 8 | Specific speed (pumps) | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | — |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
Centrifugal pumps are used in:
- **Municipal water supply** (lifting water from reservoirs)
- **HVAC systems** (chilled water circulation)
- **Power plants** (boiler feed, condensate extraction)
- **Petrochemical industry** (crude oil transport)
- **Agriculture** (irrigation from rivers/wells)
- **Fire-fighting systems**

---

## 2.2 Hydraulic Turbines — Classification & Theory

### Classification (KTU Board-Standard Format)

#### A) Based on **Action of Water on the Runner Blades**

| Type | Energy Conversion | Example | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Impulse Turbine** | All pressure energy → kinetic energy in the nozzle; pressure at runner is atmospheric | **Pelton Wheel** | Water jets strike buckets; no pressure change across runner |
| **Reaction Turbine** | Water under pressure flows through runner; pressure energy continuously decreases as it passes over the blades | **Francis Turbine, Kaplan Turbine** | Runner is fully enclosed; water pressure changes across blades |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Identifier for KTU MCQs:**
> - **Pelton = Impulse** (uses a nozzle, runner runs in air)
> - **Francis / Kaplan = Reaction** (uses a guide vane/distributor, runner is submerged)

#### B) Based on **Head at Inlet**

| Turbine | Suitable Head |
|---|---|
| **Pelton Wheel** (1-jet, multi-jet) | High head (above 300 m, up to 1800 m) |
| **Francis Turbine** (slow, medium, fast) | Medium head (30 m to 300 m) |
| **Kaplan / Propeller** | Low head (below 30 m) |

#### C) Based on **Direction of Water Flow Through Runner**
- **Tangential flow** — Pelton wheel
- **Radial flow** — Old Francis (inward/outward radial)
- **Axial flow** — Kaplan / Propeller
- **Mixed flow** — Modern Francis

#### D) Based on **Specific Speed ($N_s$)**
- $N_s$ **low** (10–60) → Impulse turbines (Pelton)
- $N_s$ **medium** (60–300) → Francis turbines
- $N_s$ **high** (300–1000) → Kaplan / Propeller

### Specific Speed Formula
$$N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$$
where $N$ is in rpm, $P$ in kW, $H$ in metres.

> [!NOTE]
> Specific speed is **NOT** the actual speed. It is the speed of a geometrically similar turbine that would produce **1 unit of power under 1 unit of head**.

### Real-World Utility
- **Pelton wheels** dominate in **Himalayan hydroelectric projects** (high-head sites).
- **Francis turbines** are the **workhorses of medium-head dams** worldwide.
- **Kaplan turbines** are used in **low-head run-of-river** projects (e.g., Idukki's run-of-river stations, Bhakra Nangal's lower units).

---

## 2.3 Gears — Theory, Terminology & Application

### Essential Gear Terminology
- **Pitch Circle** — Imaginary circle on which two gears roll without slipping.
- **Pitch Point** — Point of contact of the two pitch circles.
- **Module ($m$)** — Ratio of pitch circle diameter to number of teeth: $m = \dfrac{D}{T}$
- **Circular Pitch ($p_c$)** — Arc length along pitch circle between corresponding points of adjacent teeth: $p_c = \pi m$
- **Addendum ($a$)** — Radial height of tooth above pitch circle: $a = 1 \cdot m$
- **Dedendum ($b$)** — Radial depth of tooth below pitch circle: $b = 1.157 \cdot m$ (for 20° full-depth involute — KTU standard)
- **Pressure Angle ($\phi$)** — Angle between the common normal at the pitch point and the common tangent. Standard = **20°**.

### Velocity Ratio (Gear Ratio)
$$i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1}$$
where subscripts 1 = pinion (smaller, driver) and 2 = gear (larger, driven).

### KTU Formula Sheet — Gears
| # | Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Module | $m = \dfrac{D}{T} = \dfrac{p_c}{\pi}$ |
| 2 | Pitch circle diameter | $D = m \cdot T$ |
| 3 | Circular pitch | $p_c = \pi m$ |
| 4 | Addendum | $a = 1m$ |
| 5 | Dedendum | $b = 1.157m$ |
| 6 | Total depth of tooth | $h = a + b = 2.157m$ |
| 7 | Clearance | $c = b - a = 0.157m$ |
| 8 | Centre distance (spur) | $C = \dfrac{D_1 + D_2}{2} = \dfrac{m(T_1+T_2)}{2}$ |
| 9 | Velocity ratio | $i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **For 20° full-depth involute system** (which KTU uses by default), always use $a=1m$ and $b=1.157m$. For a stub-tooth system, $a=0.8m$ and $b=1m$ — but this is rare in board exams.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Automobile transmissions** — helical gears in manual gearboxes
- **Robotics & CNC machines** — precision spur and bevel gears
- **Differential gears in cars** — bevel gears
- **Lifts, hoists, conveyors** — worm and worm wheel (self-locking!)
- **Wind turbines** — planetary gear systems (out of KTU scope but contextual)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Work Done by the Impeller per Unit Weight of Water (Euler's Equation for Centrifugal Pump)

This is the **single most important derivation** for the KTU board exam. We derive the work done per unit weight from the velocity triangle at the impeller outlet.

### Assumptions
1. Flow is **steady and uniform** at inlet and outlet of the impeller.
2. The impeller has an **infinite number of blades** (so flow follows blade direction exactly — Euler's assumption).
3. **No shock losses** at the entry.
4. The fluid is **incompressible** (water, in our case).

### Velocity Triangle at Outlet
At the impeller outlet (radius $r_2$), three velocities exist:
- $V_2$ — Absolute velocity of water (m/s)
- $u_2$ — Blade (tangential) velocity $= \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ (m/s)
- $V_{r2}$ — Relative velocity of water w.r.t. blade (m/s)

The absolute velocity $V_2$ is resolved into:
- $V_{w2}$ — Whirl component (tangential to blade circle) — this does **work**
- $V_{f2}$ — Flow component (radial / axial) — this does **no work**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Consider the **control volume** of water between the impeller inlet and outlet. 

**Step 1:** By **Newton's second law in angular form**, the **torque** exerted by the impeller on the water equals the rate of change of angular momentum of water:

$$T = \dot{m} (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)$$

where $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ is the mass flow rate.

**Step 2:** At the **inlet**, water enters **radially** (no whirl) for a typical centrifugal pump (no pre-whirl):
$$V_{w1} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Substituting Step 2 into Step 1:
$$T = \dot{m} \cdot V_{w2} \cdot r_2$$

**Step 4:** The **work done per second (power)** by the impeller on the water:
$$P_{impeller} = T \cdot \omega = \dot{m} \cdot V_{w2} \cdot r_2 \cdot \omega$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $\omega = \dfrac{u_2}{r_2}$:
$$P_{impeller} = \dot{m} \cdot V_{w2} \cdot r_2 \cdot \dfrac{u_2}{r_2} = \dot{m} \cdot V_{w2} \cdot u_2$$

**Step 6:** Work done per unit weight of water:
$$W = \dfrac{P_{impeller}}{\dot{m} \cdot g} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot V_{w2} \cdot u_2}{\dot{m} \cdot g}$$

$$\boxed{W = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}}$$

**Step 7:** Since $W$ has units of metres (energy per unit weight = head), this equals the **manometric head** the impeller *would* develop if there were no losses:
$$H_{th} = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g} \quad (\text{Theoretical/Impeller head})$$

**Step 8:** The **manometric efficiency** accounts for the fact that some head is lost (fluid slip, friction, etc.):
$$H_m = \eta_{man} \cdot H_{th} = \eta_{man} \cdot \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where students lose marks:** Forgetting that the **angle at the outlet ($\beta_2$)** is the blade angle. From the velocity triangle, $\tan \beta_2 = \dfrac{V_{f2}}{u_2 - V_{w2}}$, so $V_{w2} = u_2 - \dfrac{V_{f2}}{\tan \beta_2}$. This is required when given $\beta_2$ instead of $V_{w2}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Head Developed by the Centrifugal Pump

### Setup
Consider a pump with the following notation:
- $H_s$ = suction head (positive if pump is above water source)
- $H_d$ = delivery head (vertical height of discharge point above pump)
- $h_{fs}$ = friction loss in suction pipe
- $h_{fd}$ = friction loss in delivery pipe
- $V_d$ = velocity of water at the outlet of delivery pipe

### Energy Equation (Bernoulli's Theorem) — From Suction Reservoir (Point 1) to Delivery Reservoir (Point 2)

Applying Bernoulli's equation with a pump head $H$ supplying energy:

$$\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + \text{losses}$$

**Step 1:** Both reservoirs are open to atmosphere: $p_1 = p_2 = p_{atm}$, so pressure heads cancel.

**Step 2:** The water surface velocities are negligible: $V_1 \approx V_2 \approx 0$, except at the **delivery pipe outlet** where $V_d$ matters.

**Step 3:** Take the **water surface in the sump as datum**: $z_1 = 0$ and $z_2 = H_s + H_d$ (the total lift).

**Step 4:** Substituting:
$$H = (H_s + H_d) + h_{fs} + h_{fd} + \dfrac{V_d^2}{2g}$$

**Step 5:** Identifying the **Manometric Head** (head developed by impeller):
$$\boxed{H_m = H_s + H_d + h_{fs} + h_{fd} = H_s + H_d + H_f}$$

**Step 6:** The **Total Head** (what the pump must supply, including kinetic head at delivery):
$$\boxed{H = H_m + \dfrac{V_d^2}{2g}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Centre Distance for a Pair of Spur Gears

### Setup
Two spur gears in mesh:
- Pinion: $T_1$ teeth, pitch circle diameter $D_1$
- Gear: $T_2$ teeth, pitch circle diameter $D_2$

### Derivation
**Step 1:** Module definition (same for both gears so they mesh):
$$m = \dfrac{D_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{D_2}{T_2}$$

**Step 2:** Therefore:
$$D_1 = m T_1 \quad \text{and} \quad D_2 = m T_2$$

**Step 3:** The **centre distance** is the sum of the two radii:
$$C = r_1 + r_2 = \dfrac{D_1}{2} + \dfrac{D_2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{C = \dfrac{m(T_1 + T_2)}{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the formula you must use when a KTU question asks: "Two spur gears with 20 and 40 teeth of module 5 mm are in mesh. Find the centre distance." Just plug in.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Templates

### Centrifugal Pump Numerical Pattern
**Given:** $Q$, $H_m$, $N$, $D_2$, $V_{w2}$ (or $\beta_2$, $V_{f2}$)
**Find:** $u_2$, work done, power input, efficiencies.

| # | What to Find | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Blade speed | $u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ |
| 2 | Manometric head | $H_m = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$ |
| 3 | Water Power (output) | $P_w = \rho g Q H_m$ |
| 4 | Shaft Power | $P_{sh} = \dfrac{P_w}{\eta_{man}}$ |
| 5 | Input Power | $P_{in} = \dfrac{P_w}{\eta_{overall}}$ |

### Gear Numerical Pattern
**Given:** $T_1$, $T_2$, $m$, $N_1$
**Find:** Centre distance, $N_2$, $D_1$, $D_2$, addendum, dedendum.

| # | What to Find | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pitch circle diameters | $D_1 = mT_1$, $D_2 = mT_2$ |
| 2 | Centre distance | $C = m(T_1+T_2)/2$ |
| 3 | Speed ratio | $N_2 = N_1 \times (T_1/T_2)$ |
| 4 | Addendum | $a = 1m$ |
| 5 | Dedendum | $b = 1.157m$ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Centrifugal Pump — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sump / Water Source<br/>Atmospheric Pressure] -->|Suction Pipe<br/>H_s| B[Foot Valve + Strainer<br/>Prevents backflow<br/>Filters debris]
    B --> C[Impeller Eye<br/>Low Pressure Region<br/>Centrifugal Action]
    C --> D[Impeller Rotating Vanes<br/>Adds Kinetic Energy<br/>u2 = pi D2 N/60]
    D --> E[Volute Casing<br/>Area increases<br/>Velocity to Pressure]
    E --> F[Delivery Pipe<br/>H_d + H_fd friction]
    F --> G[Discharge Point<br/>Elevated Reservoir]

    P[Electric Motor / Engine] -->|Shaft Power| D

    H1[Lost Energy 1: Friction H_f] -.-> B
    H2[Lost Energy 2: Friction H_fd] -.-> F
    H3[Lost Energy 3: Kinetic head Vd^2/2g] -.-> G

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style G fill:#d4edda
    style D fill:#fff3cd
    style E fill:#f8d7da
```

### Functional Flow Explanation
1. **Sump (A):** Source reservoir at atmospheric pressure.
2. **Foot Valve + Strainer (B):** Ensures pump remains primed (no back-flow) and protects impeller.
3. **Impeller Eye (C):** Low-pressure region — atmospheric pressure pushes water up the suction pipe.
4. **Impeller Vanes (D):** The "engine room" — imparts kinetic energy.
5. **Volute Casing (E):** Deceleration chamber — converts velocity to pressure.
6. **Delivery Pipe (F):** Carries pressurized water to the discharge.
7. **Discharge (G):** Final destination of water.
