# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
**Define the following terms related to a centrifugal pump: (i) Manometric head, (ii) Priming, (iii) Specific speed.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

**(i) Manometric Head ($H_m$):** It is the head developed by the impeller of the centrifugal pump. It is the sum of the static head, friction losses in suction and delivery pipes, and equals the energy imparted by the impeller per unit weight of fluid.
$$H_m = H_s + H_d + H_f$$

**(ii) Priming:** Priming is the process of filling the suction pipe, casing, and impeller completely with the liquid to be pumped **before starting the pump**. It is essential because a centrifugal pump cannot develop suction if there is air in the casing (air, being lighter, cannot be flung outward effectively).

**(iii) Specific Speed ($N_s$):** It is defined as the speed of a geometrically similar pump that would deliver unit discharge (1 m³/min) against unit head (1 m). It is a dimensionless index used to classify pumps:
$$N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "Specific speed is the actual speed of the pump at a specific condition." This is **wrong** — it is a **dimensionless index** used for classification, not the operating speed.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Compare impulse and reaction turbines (any four points).**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

| # | Impulse Turbine | Reaction Turbine |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | All pressure energy is converted to kinetic energy in the nozzle before water strikes the runner. | Water under pressure flows over the blades; pressure energy decreases continuously across the runner. |
| 2 | Runner operates in air at atmospheric pressure. | Runner is fully enclosed and submerged in flowing water. |
| 3 | Suitable for **high heads** (300 m to 1800 m). Example: **Pelton wheel**. | Suitable for **medium to low heads** (below 300 m). Example: **Francis, Kaplan**. |
| 4 | Water contacts the runner only once (single bucket wheel). | Water passes through guide vanes and the runner; may use draft tube. |
| 5 | No pressure variation across the blades. | Significant pressure variation across the blades. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model QP]**

**(a)** With the help of a velocity triangle, derive an expression for the work done per unit weight of water by a centrifugal pump impeller. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal pump delivers 0.05 m³/s of water against a manometric head of 25 m. The impeller diameter is 0.30 m and runs at 1500 rpm. If the manometric efficiency is 85%, find (i) the whirl velocity at the outlet, (ii) the power required to drive the pump, and (iii) the overall efficiency if the mechanical efficiency is 90%. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation** *(Refer to Section 3.1 for the full derivation)*

The velocity triangle at the impeller outlet has:
- $u_2$ = tangential blade velocity
- $V_2$ = absolute velocity of water
- $V_{r2}$ = relative velocity
- $V_{w2}$ = whirl (tangential) component
- $V_{f2}$ = flow (radial) component

By **Newton's law in angular momentum form**, the torque on the water is:
$$T = \dot{m} (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)$$

Assuming no pre-whirl, $V_{w1} = 0$:
$$T = \dot{m} \cdot V_{w2} \cdot r_2$$

The work done per second:
$$P = T \cdot \omega = \dot{m} \cdot V_{w2} \cdot u_2$$

**Work done per unit weight:**
$$W = \dfrac{P}{\dot{m} \cdot g} = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, uniform flow at inlet and outlet.
2. Infinite number of blades (no slip).
3. No pre-whirl at inlet.
4. Incompressible fluid.
5. No shock losses.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- $Q = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $H_m = 25 \text{ m}$
- $D_2 = 0.30 \text{ m}$
- $N = 1500 \text{ rpm}$
- $\eta_{man} = 85\% = 0.85$
- $\eta_{mech} = 90\% = 0.90$
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1: Calculate the blade (tangential) velocity at the outlet:**
$$u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.30 \times 1500}{60}$$

$$u_2 = \dfrac{1413.72}{60} = 23.56 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the whirl velocity $V_{w2}$ from the manometric efficiency formula:**

Theoretical head imparted by impeller:
$$H_{th} = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$

Relation: $H_m = \eta_{man} \times H_{th}$, so:
$$H_{th} = \dfrac{H_m}{\eta_{man}} = \dfrac{25}{0.85} = 29.41 \text{ m}$$

Therefore:
$$V_{w2} = \dfrac{H_{th} \cdot g}{u_2} = \dfrac{29.41 \times 9.81}{23.56}$$

$$V_{w2} = \dfrac{288.52}{23.56} \approx 12.25 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Water Power (output):**
$$P_{water} = \rho g Q H_m = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 25$$

$$P_{water} = 12262.5 \text{ W} \approx 12.26 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Shaft Power (= Water Power / Manometric Efficiency):**
$$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{water}}{\eta_{man}} = \dfrac{12.26}{0.85} \approx 14.42 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Power required to drive the pump (input from prime mover):**
$$P_{input} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{mech}} = \dfrac{14.42}{0.90}$$

$$\boxed{P_{input} \approx 16.02 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 6: Overall Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{mech} = 0.85 \times 0.90$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{overall} = 0.765 = 76.5\%}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**

**(a)** Classify hydraulic turbines in detail based on (i) action of water on the runner, (ii) head at the inlet, and (iii) direction of water flow. Give one example for each type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pair of spur gears with 24 teeth (pinion) and 64 teeth (gear) are in mesh. The module is 4 mm. If the pinion rotates at 1450 rpm, find: (i) pitch circle diameters of both gears, (ii) centre distance, (iii) speed of the gear, (iv) addendum and dedendum of teeth, and (v) circular pitch. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Classification of Hydraulic Turbines**

**(i) Based on Action of Water on Runner:**

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Impulse Turbine** | All pressure energy of water is converted into kinetic energy in a nozzle, which then strikes the runner buckets. The pressure at the runner is atmospheric. | Pelton Wheel |
| **Reaction Turbine** | Water flows over the runner blades under pressure. Both pressure and velocity change as water passes over the blades. The runner is enclosed in a casing. | Francis Turbine, Kaplan Turbine |

**(ii) Based on Head at Inlet:**

| Head Range | Suitable Turbine |
|---|---|
| **High head** (300 m – 1800 m) | Pelton wheel |
| **Medium head** (30 m – 300 m) | Francis turbine |
| **Low head** (below 30 m) | Kaplan / Propeller turbine |

**(iii) Based on Direction of Water Flow Through Runner:**

| Flow Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Tangential flow** | Water flows tangentially to the runner. | Pelton wheel |
| **Radial flow** | Water flows radially (inward or outward). | Old-style Francis turbine |
| **Axial flow** | Water flows parallel to the axis of rotation. | Kaplan / Propeller turbine |
| **Mixed flow** | Combination of radial and axial. | Modern Francis turbine |

---

**Part (b) — Numerical on Spur Gears**

**Given:**
- Pinion teeth $T_1 = 24$
- Gear teeth $T_2 = 64$
- Module $m = 4 \text{ mm}$
- Pinion speed $N_1 = 1450 \text{ rpm}$

**Step 1: Pitch Circle Diameters:**
$$D_1 = m \cdot T_1 = 4 \times 24 = 96 \text{ mm}$$

$$D_2 = m \cdot T_2 = 4 \times 64 = 256 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Centre Distance:**
$$C = \dfrac{D_1 + D_2}{2} = \dfrac{96 + 256}{2} = \dfrac{352}{2}$$

$$C = 176 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Speed of the Gear:**
Using velocity ratio:
$$N_2 = N_1 \times \dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = 1450 \times \dfrac{24}{64} = 1450 \times 0.375$$

$$N_2 = 543.75 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 4: Addendum and Dedendum:**
$$\text{Addendum } a = 1 \cdot m = 1 \times 4 = 4 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{Dedendum } b = 1.157 \cdot m = 1.157 \times 4 = 4.628 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Circular Pitch:**
$$p_c = \pi m = \pi \times 4 = 12.566 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit consistency:** Forgetting to convert mm to m when calculating torque or force (1 mark penalty).
> 2. **Velocity ratio direction:** Writing $N_2 = N_1 \times (T_2/T_1)$ instead of $(T_1/T_2)$ — pinion (smaller teeth) rotates faster.
> 3. **Centrifugal pump formula mix-up:** Using $H_m = V_{w2} u_2 / g$ directly as the manometric head, forgetting to multiply by $\eta_{man}$ for the actual head.
> 4. **Impulse vs Reaction:** Calling Francis a "low head" turbine (it is medium head 30–300 m).
> 5. **Addendum vs Dedendum confusion:** Addendum is the tooth *above* the pitch circle (always 1m); dedendum is the tooth *below* (1.157m).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 Centrifugal Pump — Rapid Revision
- ✅ Working principle: **forced vortex + pressure conversion in volute**.
- ✅ **Priming is mandatory** — pump cannot suck air.
- ✅ **Euler's head equation:** $H_{th} = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$
- ✅ **Manometric head:** $H_m = H_s + H_d + H_f$ (sum of static + friction losses).
- ✅ **Total head:** $H = H_m + \dfrac{V_d^2}{2g}$ (adds velocity head at outlet).
- ✅ **Overall efficiency:** $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{mech}$
- ✅ Blade velocity: $u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$

### 🎯 Hydraulic Turbines — Rapid Revision
- ✅ **Pelton = Impulse + High head + Tangential flow** (most asked).
- ✅ **Francis = Reaction + Medium head + Radial/Mixed flow**.
- ✅ **Kaplan = Reaction + Low head + Axial flow**.
- ✅ Specific speed: $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ (in **pump form**; in turbine form, use $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ as well, but with $P$ in kW and $H$ in m).
- ✅ **Runner (Pelton) is in air; Runner (Francis/Kaplan) is submerged.**
- ✅ Use **specific speed to select** the right turbine for a given site.

### 🎯 Gears — Rapid Revision
- ✅ **Module ($m$)** = Standard parameter for all gear sizing in KTU problems.
- ✅ **Standard pressure angle = 20°** (full-depth involute).
- ✅ **Addendum = $1m$; Dedendum = $1.157m$** (memorize!).
- ✅ **Centre distance** for spur gears: $C = \dfrac{m(T_1+T_2)}{2}$.
- ✅ **Velocity ratio** for any gear pair: $i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$.
- ✅ **Spur** = straight teeth, parallel shafts, **noisy at high speed**.
- ✅ **Helical** = angled teeth, **smooth & quiet**, thrust load on shaft.
- ✅ **Bevel** = intersecting shafts, used in **differentials**.
- ✅ **Worm & wheel** = **self-locking**, high reduction ratios, used in **lifts**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for Gears:** **"S**pur = **S**traight, **H**elical = **H**elping silence the noise, **B**evel = **B**ent shafts, **W**orm = **W**inding (screw-like)". This four-line rhyme has saved countless KTU students in the viva!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Clutches and Their Uses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO MECHANICAL ENGINEERING & CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Clutches and Their Uses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Clutch?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A **clutch** is a mechanical device used in power transmission systems to engage and disengage the transmission of power (torque) from the driving shaft to the driven shaft, either gradually or suddenly, without disconnecting the driving member from the source of power.

In simpler terms, a clutch is the "**on/off switch**" of a vehicle or machine that controls the flow of rotational power between an engine (driver) and the wheels or working component (driven member).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Plain-English Version

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Two Plates on a Table":**
> Imagine two flat plates lying on a table. The bottom plate is spinning (the engine), and the top plate is connected to a wheel that is stationary. When you press them together, friction causes the top plate to start spinning with the bottom one. When you lift the top plate away, the spinning stops transferring. **That pressed contact is exactly what a friction clutch does!**

- **Driver (Engine side)** → rotating input member
- **Driven (Wheel/Output side)** → member that receives torque
- **Engagement** = contact established → power flows
- **Disengagement** = contact removed → power stops

---

## 1.3 Why Do We Need a Clutch?

A clutch is essential for:

1. **Smooth starting** of a vehicle from rest.
2. **Changing gears** without damaging the gearbox.
3. **Preventing overloading** of the engine during sudden stops.
4. **Connecting/disconnecting** power to auxiliary drives (pumps, compressors).

---

## 1.4 Broad Classification of Clutches

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Classification Snapshot:**
> Clutches are broadly classified into two main families based on the **mode of power transmission**:

| S.No. | Type | Power Transfer Mechanism | Key Feature |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Friction Clutch** | By **frictional contact** between mating surfaces | Smooth engagement, gradual torque transfer |
| 2 | **Positive Clutch** | By **mechanical interlocking** (teeth/jaws) | Positive (no-slip) engagement, sudden action |

---

## 1.5 Friction Clutches — Sub-Types

Friction clutches are further divided based on the **shape of the contact surfaces** and the **actuation principle**:

### (a) Single Plate Clutch
- **Construction:** A single friction disc (driven plate) is sandwiched between a **flywheel** (driver) and a **pressure plate** (cover).
- **Working:** A spring pushes the pressure plate against the friction disc, which is pressed against the flywheel.
- **Use:** **Passenger cars, light commercial vehicles** (most common in automobiles).
- **Number of friction surfaces (n) = 2** (one on each side of the disc).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of a sandwich — the flywheel is one bread slice, the pressure plate is the other, and the friction disc is the filling. The spring is the hand squeezing them together.

### (b) Multi-Plate Clutch
- **Construction:** Uses **multiple friction discs** alternately mounted on the driving and driven shafts, enclosed in a housing.
- **Use:** **Motorcycles, racing cars, heavy machinery** where high torque must be transmitted in a compact space.
- **Number of friction surfaces (n) = (number of discs − 1) × 2** (each disc has two contact faces).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multi-plate?** More friction surfaces = more torque capacity in a smaller space. Like stacking many thin cloths together — each one adds grip.

### (c) Cone Clutch
- **Construction:** The contacting surfaces are **cone-shaped** (one male cone fits into a female cone).
- **Use:** **Synchro-mesh gearboxes, industrial machinery, older automobiles.**
- **Advantage:** Cone shape provides a **mechanical wedging effect**, multiplying the axial thrust force.
- **Disadvantage:** Slightly difficult to disengage; can grab/judder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Like a coffee cup lid fitting into the cup — the slanted side provides more grip and self-locking due to the wedge angle.

### (d) Centrifugal Clutch
- **Construction:** **Shoes or weights** mounted on the driving shaft, kept away from the drum by springs at low speeds.
- **Working:** At a preset **cut-in speed**, centrifugal force throws the shoes outward, pressing them against the inner drum surface (driven member), engaging the clutch automatically.
- **Use:** **Scooters, mopeds, small go-karts, lawn mowers, chain saws.**
- **Special feature:** **Self-engaging** — no manual lever needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine spinning a ball on a string — the faster you spin, the harder the string pulls outward. The centrifugal clutch uses this exact principle to "click in" automatically.

---

## 1.6 Positive Clutches — Sub-Types

### (a) Jaw (Dog) Clutch
- **Construction:** Two flanges with interlocking **teeth (jaws)** that slide axially to engage.
- **Use:** **Machine tools, industrial gearboxes, punch presses** where engagement/disengagement is infrequent.
- **Limitation:** **Cannot be engaged when shafts are rotating** at different speeds — teeth will chip. Used in **stationary or very low-speed** conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Like two LEGO bricks snapping together — once teeth are meshed, the connection is rigid and slip-free.

### (b) Spline Clutch
- **Construction:** Uses **longitudinal splines** (grooves) on the shaft and mating teeth on a sliding hub.
- **Use:** **Constant-mesh gearboxes** in automobiles, where gears are always rotating but only need to be connected occasionally.
- **Advantage:** Can transmit torque **continuously without full engagement** (unlike jaw clutch which must fully mesh).

---

## 1.7 Master Summary Table — At a Glance

| Clutch Type | Engagement Type | Typical Use | Engagement Speed |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Single Plate | Friction | Cars, LCVs | Gradual |
| Multi-Plate | Friction | Bikes, racing cars | Gradual |
| Cone | Friction (Wedging) | Industrial gearboxes | Gradual |
| Centrifugal | Friction (Auto) | Scooters, mowers | Self at cut-in RPM |
| Jaw / Dog | Positive (Mechanical) | Machine tools | Only at rest/synced |
| Spline | Positive (Sliding) | Constant-mesh gearboxes | Partial/Full |

---

## 1.8 KTU Examiner's First-Look Notes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For your 3-mark question answer — always remember these 3 things:**
> 1. Clutch = engages/disengages torque between two shafts.
> 2. Two main families: **Friction** (smooth) and **Positive** (rigid).
> 3. Torque transmitted **T = μ × W × R<sub>m</sub> × n** — formula universally tested.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Physics of a Friction Clutch

When two surfaces are pressed together with a **normal axial force (W)**, the **frictional force (F<sub>f</sub>)** at the contact resists relative motion.

$$
F_f \;=\; \mu \times W
$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = **coefficient of friction** between contact surfaces (dimensionless, typically **0.1 to 0.5**).
- $W$ = axial clamping force in **Newtons (N)**.

The torque transmitted is the frictional force multiplied by the **effective radius** at which it acts.

---

## 2.2 The Two Governing Theories of Pressure Distribution

When a friction plate is pressed against another plate, the **contact pressure (p)** may not be uniform across the contact area. Two classical theories are used in clutch design:

### 2.2.1 Uniform Pressure Theory (Newcomen's Assumption)
- **Assumption:** Pressure $p$ is **constant** across the entire contact surface from inner radius $r$ to outer radius $R$.
- **Used when:** Surfaces are **new** and **rigid**, with even wear not yet developed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Like pressing a flat eraser on a flat table — pressure is the same everywhere.

### 2.2.2 Uniform Wear Theory (Reye's Assumption)
- **Assumption:** The product $p \times r$ is **constant** (i.e., pressure decreases with radius).
- **Used when:** Surfaces have **worn-in** and wear is uniform across the contact area.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** After prolonged use, the outer edge wears more (higher linear velocity), so pressure must reduce there to equalize wear.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it is the most-tested content on clutches in KTU exams.**

### A. General Torque Equation for a Friction Clutch

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|
| Axial Clamping Force | $W$ | Given / from spring | N |
| Coefficient of Friction | $\mu$ | Material property | dimensionless |
| Inner Radius | $r$ | Geometry | m |
| Outer Radius | $R$ | Geometry | m |
| Number of Friction Surfaces | $n$ | Per clutch design | dimensionless |
| **Mean Friction Radius** | $R_m$ | $(R + r) / 2$ | m |

### B. Torque Transmission Equations

| Theory | Torque Transmitted ($T$) | Axial Force for Given Torque ($W$) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Uniform Pressure** | $T \;=\; \dfrac{2}{3} \, \mu \, W \, n \, \dfrac{R^{3} - r^{3}}{R^{2} - r^{2}}$ | $W \;=\; \dfrac{3T(R^{2} - r^{2})}{2 \mu \, n \, (R^{3} - r^{3})}$ |
| **Uniform Wear** | $T \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2} \, \mu \, W \, n \, (R + r)$ | $W \;=\; \dfrac{2T}{\mu \, n \, (R + r)}$ |

### C. Special Cases (Memo-Worthy)

| Special Case | Torque Formula | Note |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Cone Clutch** (Uniform Wear) | $T \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2} \, \mu \, W \, n \, (R + r) \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sin\alpha}$ | $\alpha$ = **half-cone angle** |
| **Cone Clutch** (Uniform Pressure) | $T \;=\; \dfrac{2}{3} \, \mu \, W \, n \, \dfrac{R^{3} - r^{3}}{R^{2} - r^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sin\alpha}$ | Same factor $1/\sin\alpha$ multiplies |
| **Axial Force on Cone** | $W_{axial} \;=\; W_{normal} \times \sin\alpha$ | Wedging effect |
| **Centrifugal Clutch** | $W \;=\; m \, \omega^{2} \, r$ | $m$ = shoe mass, $\omega$ = angular velocity |
| **Multi-Plate** | All formulas above multiplied by **$n$** | $n = (N_1 + N_2 - 1)$ where $N_1, N_2$ = discs on driver and driven |

---

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic — Why Two Theories?

**Step 1 — Pressure Distribution Begins:**
When a new clutch is engaged for the first time, the pressure at every point is equal because the surfaces are perfectly flat and rigid.

**Step 2 — Wear Starts Occurring:**
After thousands of engagements, the **outer edge** (which has higher linear velocity) wears out more, and the **inner edge** wears less.

**Step 3 — Equilibrium State:**
In the long-term, the clutch operates in a state where **wear is uniform**. Since wear rate $\propto p \times v = p \times \omega \times r$, for uniform wear:

$$
p \times r \;=\; \text{constant} \;=\; C
$$

**Step 4 — Why Uniform Wear is Used in Design:**
Designers use the **uniform wear theory** because the clutch is in this state for 95% of its working life. Uniform pressure is used only for the **initial design** or for very short-life clutches.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these formulas save money in industry:**
> 1. **Automobile Design** — selecting the right spring pressure (W) for a given engine torque.
> 2. **Heavy Earth-Moving Equipment** — multi-plate clutches handle huge torques in small spaces (e.g., bulldozers).
> 3. **Scooter/Moped Manufacturing** — centrifugal clutch "cut-in RPM" tuning (e.g., 2,000 RPM for Honda Activa).
> 4. **Industrial Press Machines** — jaw clutches handle high shock loads in metal stamping.

---

## 2.6 Design Constraints and Material Notes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favorite Material Property Questions:**

- **Friction Lining Materials:** Asbestos (older), Sintered bronze, Ceramics, Organic resin-bonded materials, Carbon composites.
- **Coefficient of friction $\mu$ values (typical):**
  - Asbestos-based: $\mu \approx 0.3 - 0.4$
  - Sintered bronze: $\mu \approx 0.2 - 0.3$
  - Organic (modern): $\mu \approx 0.25 - 0.35$
- **Permissible pressure $p_{max}$ (typical):** $\mathbf{0.7}$ to $\mathbf{2.0 \; MPa}$ depending on material.

---

## 2.7 Single-Plate vs. Multi-Plate — When to Use What?

| Parameter | Single Plate | Multi-Plate |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Torque Capacity | Lower | **Higher** (5–10×) |
| Space Required | More | **Less** |
| Heat Dissipation | Better | **Poorer** (compact = hot) |
| Cost | Lower | **Higher** |
| Number of surfaces (n) | 2 | 2×(N-1) |
| Example | Maruti Alto car clutch | Bajaj Pulsar bike clutch |

---

## 2.8 Centrifugal Clutch — Engineering Deep Dive

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Principle (Step by Step):**
> 1. Engine rotates the **driver spider** (which carries shoes).
> 2. Below **cut-in speed**, springs keep shoes retracted → **no contact with drum** → no power transfer.
> 3. As $\omega$ increases, **centrifugal force $F_c = m\omega^2 r$** grows.
> 4. When $F_c$ exceeds spring force $F_s$, shoes fly outward.
> 5. Shoes contact the drum (driven member) → friction → torque transfer begins.
> 6. Engine is now connected to the wheel.

**Design Equation:**

$$
m \, \omega_{cut-in}^{2} \, r \;\geq\; F_s
$$

Solving for the cut-in speed:

$$
\omega_{cut-in} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{F_s}{m \, r}} \quad \text{[rad/s]}
$$

Or in RPM:

$$
N_{cut-in} \;=\; \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{F_s}{m \, r}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Torque in Uniform Pressure Theory

### 3.1.1 Setup
Consider a single friction disc with:
- **Inner radius = $r$**
- **Outer radius = $R$**
- **Uniform pressure = $p$** (acting perpendicular to the surface)
- **Axial clamping force = $W$**

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Total Axial Force
The total axial force $W$ equals pressure $p$ times the **annular contact area**:

$$
W \;=\; p \times A \;=\; p \times \pi(R^{2} - r^{2})
$$

Therefore:

$$
p \;=\; \dfrac{W}{\pi(R^{2} - r^{2})}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Friction Force on an Elemental Ring
Consider a thin ring of radius $\rho$ and thickness $d\rho$ on the friction surface. Its area is:

$$
dA \;=\; 2\pi\rho \, d\rho
$$

The normal force on this ring (the axial reaction from clamping) is:

$$
dN \;=\; p \times dA \;=\; p \times 2\pi\rho \, d\rho
$$

The frictional force on this ring is:

$$
dF_f \;=\; \mu \, dN \;=\; \mu \, p \, 2\pi\rho \, d\rho
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Torque Contribution of the Elemental Ring
The torque transmitted by this elemental ring (force × radius) is:

$$
dT \;=\; dF_f \times \rho \;=\; \mu \, p \, 2\pi\rho^{2} \, d\rho
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Integrate Over the Full Annular Area
Integrate $\rho$ from $r$ to $R$:

$$
T \;=\; \int_{r}^{R} \mu \, p \, 2\pi\rho^{2} \, d\rho
$$

$$
T \;=\; 2\pi\mu p \int_{r}^{R} \rho^{2} \, d\rho
$$

The integral of $\rho^2$ is $\rho^3/3$:

$$
T \;=\; 2\pi\mu p \left[\dfrac{\rho^{3}}{3}\right]_{r}^{R}
$$

$$
T \;=\; 2\pi\mu p \left(\dfrac{R^{3} - r^{3}}{3}\right)
$$

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi\mu p}{3}(R^{3} - r^{3})
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Substitute $p$ from Step 1
From Step 1: $p = W / [\pi(R^2 - r^2)]$. Substituting:

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi\mu}{3}(R^{3} - r^{3}) \times \dfrac{W}{\pi(R^{2} - r^{2})}
$$

The $\pi$ cancels out:

$$
\boxed{\,T \;=\; \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot \dfrac{R^{3} - r^{3}}{R^{2} - r^{2}}\,}
$$

This is the **uniform pressure torque equation** for a single friction surface.

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Extend to $n$ Friction Surfaces

$$
\boxed{\,T_{\text{multi-surface}} \;=\; \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot n \cdot \dfrac{R^{3} - r^{3}}{R^{2} - r^{2}}\,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Torque in Uniform Wear Theory

### 3.2.1 The Wear Condition
Under uniform wear, the product $p \times \rho$ is constant:

$$
p \times \rho \;=\; C
$$

So pressure at any radius $\rho$:

$$
p(\rho) \;=\; \dfrac{C}{\rho}
$$

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Total Axial Force
Integrate the elemental normal force over the contact area:

$$
W \;=\; \int_{r}^{R} p(\rho) \times 2\pi\rho \, d\rho
$$

$$
W \;=\; \int_{r}^{R} \dfrac{C}{\rho} \times 2\pi\rho \, d\rho
$$

The $\rho$ cancels out (a key simplification):

$$
W \;=\; 2\pi C \int_{r}^{R} d\rho
$$

$$
W \;=\; 2\pi C (R - r)
$$

Therefore:

$$
C \;=\; \dfrac{W}{2\pi(R - r)}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Torque Contribution of Elemental Ring
The friction force on a ring of radius $\rho$:

$$
dF_f \;=\; \mu \times p(\rho) \times 2\pi\rho \, d\rho \;=\; \mu \times \dfrac{C}{\rho} \times 2\pi\rho \, d\rho
$$

$$
dF_f \;=\; 2\pi\mu C \, d\rho
$$

The torque of this ring:

$$
dT \;=\; dF_f \times \rho \;=\; 2\pi\mu C \rho \, d\rho
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Integrate

$$
T \;=\; \int_{r}^{R} 2\pi\mu C \rho \, d\rho
$$

$$
T \;=\; 2\pi\mu C \left[\dfrac{\rho^{2}}{2}\right]_{r}^{R}
$$

$$
T \;=\; \pi\mu C (R^{2} - r^{2})
$$

### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Substitute $C$ from Step 1

$$
T \;=\; \pi\mu \times \dfrac{W}{2\pi(R - r)} \times (R^{2} - r^{2})
$$

Since $R^2 - r^2 = (R-r)(R+r)$:

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{\mu W}{2(R - r)} \times (R - r)(R + r)
$$

The $(R-r)$ cancels:

$$
\boxed{\,T \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot (R + r)\,}
$$

This is the **uniform wear torque equation** for a single friction surface.

### 3.2.6 Step 5 — Extend to $n$ Friction Surfaces

$$
\boxed{\,T_{\text{multi-surface}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot n \cdot (R + r)\,}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Torque in Cone Clutch (Uniform Wear)

### 3.3.1 Geometry
A cone clutch has a contact surface inclined at **half-cone angle $\alpha$** to the axis. When the male cone is pushed axially into the female cone, the **normal force** on the contact surface is larger than the axial force due to the wedge effect.

### 3.3.2 Relation Between Axial and Normal Force
The axial component of the normal force $W_{normal}$ equals the applied axial thrust $W_{axial}$:

$$
W_{axial} \;=\; W_{normal} \times \sin\alpha
$$

So the normal force pressing surfaces together:

$$
W_{normal} \;=\; \dfrac{W_{axial}}{\sin\alpha}
$$

### 3.3.3 Step 1 — Replace W in the Uniform Wear Formula
In the uniform wear equation for a flat plate, the normal clamping force was $W$. In a cone clutch, the equivalent normal force is $W_{normal} = W_{axial} / \sin\alpha$.

Substitute this into the flat-plate uniform-wear torque equation:

$$
T_{cone} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \mu \cdot \left(\dfrac{W_{axial}}{\sin\alpha}\right) \cdot n \cdot (R + r)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_{cone} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot n \cdot \dfrac{R + r}{\sin\alpha}\,}
$$

### 3.3.4 KTU Numerical Insight
> [!NOTE]
> **Since $\sin\alpha < 1$, the term $1/\sin\alpha > 1$.** This means a cone clutch transmits **MORE torque** than a flat plate clutch for the **SAME axial force**. This is the **wedging advantage** and is the most-tested concept on cone clutches in KTU exams.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Centrifugal Clutch — Cut-in Speed

### 3.4.1 Force Balance at the Shoe
Each shoe experiences:
- **Centrifugal force (outward):** $F_c = m \, \omega^2 \, r_s$ where $r_s$ = radius from shaft center to shoe center of mass.
- **Spring force (inward):** $F_s$ (constant spring preload).

At the **cut-in speed**, these two are in equilibrium:

$$
m \, \omega_{c}^{2} \, r_s \;=\; F_s
$$

### 3.4.2 Solve for $\omega_c$

$$
\omega_{c} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{F_s}{m \, r_s}}
$$

In RPM:

$$
\boxed{\,N_{c} \;=\; \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{F_s}{m \, r_s}} \;=\; \dfrac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{F_s}{m \, r_s}}\,}
$$

### 3.4.3 Torque Transmitted After Engagement
Once shoes contact the drum with normal force $W = F_c = m\omega^2 r_s$, the torque transmitted follows the standard friction formula:

$$
T \;=\; \mu \, W \, n \, R_m \;=\; \mu \, m \, \omega^{2} \, r_s \, n \, \dfrac{(R + r)}{2}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Compare All Clutches

| Feature | Single Plate | Multi-Plate | Cone | Centrifugal | Jaw | Spline |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Torque formula basis** | Friction | Friction | Friction + wedge | Friction (auto) | Mechanical | Mechanical |
| **Smooth engagement?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Auto | ❌ Sudden | ⚠️ Partial |
| **Engage while running?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Auto | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Slip possible?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No (positive)** | **No (positive)** |
| **Space efficiency** | Low | **High** | Medium | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Wear** | Gradual | Gradual | Higher | Higher | Minimal | Minimal |
| **Heat dissipation** | Best | Poor | Medium | Medium | N/A | N/A |

---

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Model):** A single-plate clutch has an effective friction radius of 150 mm. The axial pressure is 20 kN, and $\mu = 0.3$. Calculate the torque transmitted assuming (a) uniform pressure theory and (b) uniform wear theory.

**Given:** $R_m = 0.15$ m, $W = 20{,}000$ N, $\mu = 0.3$, $n = 2$ (single plate has 2 surfaces).

**Note:** We are using $R_m$ as the effective radius (this is the mean radius for uniform pressure equivalent; in single-plate design problems KTU commonly uses $R_m$ directly). For uniform pressure with this approximation:

$$
T_{u.p.} \;=\; \mu \, W \, n \, R_m
$$

$$
T_{u.p.} \;=\; 0.3 \times 20{,}000 \times 2 \times 0.15
$$

$$
T_{u.p.} \;=\; 1800 \text{ N·m} \;=\; 1.8 \text{ kN·m}
$$

For uniform wear:

$$
T_{u.w.} \;=\; \mu \, W \, n \, R_m
$$

Same result under the $R_m$ approximation.

**For a full annulus problem (with $R$ and $r$ given):**

Let $R = 200$ mm, $r = 100$ mm, $W = 20$ kN, $\mu = 0.3$, $n = 2$.

**(a) Uniform Pressure:**

$$
T_{u.p.} \;=\; \dfrac{2}{3} \times 0.3 \times 20{,}000 \times 2 \times \dfrac{(0.2)^{3} - (0.1)^{3}}{(0.2)^{2} - (0.1)^{2}}
$$

Compute numerator: $0.008 - 0.001 = 0.007$ m³.
Compute denominator: $0.04 - 0.01 = 0.03$ m².
Ratio: $0.007 / 0.03 = 0.2333$ m.

$$
T_{u.p.} \;=\; \dfrac{2}{3} \times 0.3 \times 20{,}000 \times 2 \times 0.2333
$$

$$
T_{u.p.} \;=\; 0.6667 \times 0.3 \times 20{,}000 \times 2 \times 0.2333
$$

$$
T_{u.p.} \;=\; 0.6667 \times 0.3 \times 9333.33
$$

$$
T_{u.p.} \;\approx\; 1866.7 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(b) Uniform Wear:**

$$
T_{u.w.} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.3 \times 20{,}000 \times 2 \times (0.2 + 0.1)
$$

$$
T_{u.w.} \;=\; 0.5 \times 0.3 \times 20{,}000 \times 2 \times 0.3
$$

$$
T_{u.w.} \;=\; 0.5 \times 0.3 \times 12{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_{u.w.} \;=\; 1800 \text{ N·m}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway from Numerical:** In this case, uniform wear gives a **slightly lower torque** than uniform pressure. In design, the **uniform wear value is used as the safe, design torque** because it represents long-term operating conditions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Clutch Family Tree

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Block Diagram:** This flowchart shows the hierarchical classification of clutches as per KTU syllabus — useful for **2-mark or 3-mark** "classify clutches" questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CLUTCH] --> B[Friction Clutch]
    A --> C[Positive Clutch]

    B --> B1[Single Plate]
    B --> B2[Multi Plate]
    B --> B3[Cone]
    B --> B4[Centrifugal]

    C --> C1[Jaw or Dog]
    C --> C2[Spline]
    C --> C3[Friction and Positive Combined]

    B1 --> D1[Use: Cars and LCVs]
    B2 --> D2[Use: Bikes and Racing]
    B3 --> D3[Use: Industrial Gearbox]
    B4 --> D4[Use: Scooters and Mopeds]

    C1 --> E1[Use: Machine Tools at Rest]
    C2 --> E2[Use: Constant Mesh Gearbox]
    C3 --> E3[Use: Synchromesh Gearbox]
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Single Plate Clutch Operation

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Schematic:** Shows the engagement/disengagement logic of a single plate friction clutch (most-tested in KTU).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Crankshaft] --> B[Flywheel Driver]
    B --> C[Friction Disc Driven]
    D[Pressure Plate] --> C
    E[Clutch Spring] --> D
    F[Clutch Pedal] --> E

    C --> G{Gearbox Input Shaft}
    G --> H[Vehicle Wheels]

    F -->|Press Down| I[Disengage]
    F -->|Release| J[Engage]

    I --> K[Spring Compressed]
    K --> L[Pressure Plate Retracts]
    L --> M[Disc Free]
    M --> N[No Torque Transfer]

    J --> O[Spring Pushes]
    O --> P[Pressure Plate Clamps Disc]
    P --> Q[Friction Locks Disc to Flywheel]
    Q --> R[Torque Transmitted]
```

---

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Block Style) — Cone Clutch

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Block Cross-Section:** Represents the cone clutch geometry and force vectors in a block form (since true cross-sections are hard in mermaid, we use labeled components).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DRIVING_SHAFT [Driving Shaft Side]
        A1[Male Cone Driver]
    end

    subgraph CONTACT [Cone Contact Surface at Half Angle alpha]
        B1[Friction Lining on Cone Surface]
    end

    subgraph DRIVEN_SHAFT [Driven Shaft Side]
        C1[Female Cone Driven Hub]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> C1

    D1[Axial Thrust W_axial] --> A1

    E1[Normal Reaction W_normal = W_axial divided by sin alpha] -.-> B1

    F1[Friction Force mu times W_normal tangential to cone] --> C1
```

---

## 4.4 Centrifugal Clutch — Functional Block Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Schematic:** Maps the centrifugal clutch's self-engagement logic, including cut-in speed threshold.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Crankshaft Rotation] --> B[Driver Spider with Shoes]
    B --> C{Check RPM}

    C -->|RPM less than cut-in| D[Springs Keep Shoes Retracted]
    D --> E[No Contact with Drum]
    E --> F[Clutch Disengaged]

    C -->|RPM greater than or equal to cut-in| G[Centrifugal Force Exceeds Spring Force]
    G --> H[Shoes Fly Outward]
    H --> I[Shoes Press Against Drum Inner Surface]
    I --> J[Friction Locks Drum to Shoes]
    J --> K[Torque Transmitted to Driven Shaft]
```

---

## 4.5 Multi-Plate Clutch — Component Stack Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Block Diagram:** Shows the alternating disc arrangement that gives multi-plate clutches their high torque capacity.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Driving Shaft] --> B[Driving Discs mounted on Shaft]
    C[Driven Shaft Coaxial] --> D[Driven Discs mounted on Hub]
    B --> E[Alternating Stack in Housing]
    D --> E
    F[Springs at End Plate] --> E
    G[Operating Lever or Hydraulic Piston] --> F
    E --> H[Friction Surfaces multiply n]
    H --> I[High Torque in Compact Space]
```

---

## 4.6 Comparison Block Diagram — Friction vs Positive Clutch

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Block Schematic:** Side-by-side functional comparison of the two main clutch families.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FRICTION [Friction Clutch]
        F1[Contact by Friction]
        F2[Smooth and Gradual Engagement]
        F3[Slip Possible During Engagement]
        F4[Wear Occurs Over Time]
    end

    subgraph POSITIVE [Positive Clutch]
        P1[Contact by Teeth or Splines]
        P2[Instant and Rigid Engagement]
        P3[No Slip Once Engaged]
        P4[Minimal Wear]
    end

    FRICTION --> USE1[Used in Automobiles Bikes and Scooters]
    POSITIVE --> USE2[Used in Machine Tools and Constant Mesh Gearboxes]
```

---

## 4.7 Key Components — Labelled Block Diagram for a Typical Clutch

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Schematic:** Identifies all standard parts of an automotive clutch assembly for viva and descriptive questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Flywheel] --> B[Cover or Pressure Plate]
    B --> C[Clutch Springs]
    C --> D[Release Levers]
    D --> E[Release or Throw-out Bearing]
    E --> F[Clutch Pedal Linkage]
    G[Friction Disc with Hub Splines] --> A
    B --> G
    H[Input Shaft of Gearbox] --> G
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]**
**Define a clutch. List any four types of clutches used in automobiles.**

**Model Answer:**

A clutch is a mechanical device that engages and disengages the transmission of power (torque) from the driving shaft to the driven shaft. It allows smooth starting of a vehicle from rest and enables gear changes.

Four common types of clutches used in automobiles:

1. **Single-plate friction clutch** — used in cars.
2. **Multi-plate friction clutch** — used in motorcycles.
3. **Cone clutch** — used in older cars and industrial gearboxes.
4. **Centrifugal clutch** — used in scooters and mopeds.

*(Also acceptable: Jaw/dog clutch, Diaphragm spring clutch.)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always pair each type with **one use**. Marks are split: 1 for definition + 0.5 each for four types and uses.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]**
**Differentiate between a friction clutch and a positive clutch.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Friction Clutch | Positive Clutch |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Power Transfer** | By friction between contact surfaces | By mechanical interlocking of teeth/splines |
| **Engagement** | Gradual and smooth | Sudden and rigid |
| **Slip** | Slip possible during engagement | No slip once engaged |
| **Engage while running** | Yes (at speed difference) | No (must be at rest or synchronized) |
| **Wear** | Yes — surfaces wear out | Minimal — no relative motion after engagement |
| **Application** | Automobiles, scooters | Machine tools, constant-mesh gearboxes |
| **Examples** | Single plate, multi-plate, cone | Jaw clutch, spline clutch |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2023, Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a single-plate clutch**. State the conditions under which uniform pressure theory and uniform wear theory are applicable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A multi-plate clutch has **3 driving discs and 3 driven discs** with inner and outer radii of **75 mm and 150 mm** respectively. The coefficient of friction is **0.3** and the maximum axial intensity of pressure is **0.6 MPa**. Find the axial force and the torque transmitted, assuming **uniform pressure theory**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a)**

**Construction:**

A single-plate clutch consists of the following components (refer Section 4.7 block diagram):

1. **Flywheel** — bolted to the engine crankshaft; acts as one of the friction surfaces.
2. **Friction (Driven) Plate** — a flat circular disc with friction lining on both sides; connected to the gearbox input shaft via a splined hub.
3. **Pressure Plate (Cover)** — a heavy metal cover that presses the driven plate against the flywheel.
4. **Clutch Springs** — coil springs (or a diaphragm spring) that apply axial clamping force.
5. **Release Levers** — pivoted levers that, when the pedal is pressed, pull the pressure plate away from the disc.

**Working:**

- When the **clutch pedal is released**, the springs push the pressure plate against the friction disc, which is in turn pressed against the flywheel. Friction between the surfaces locks the disc to the flywheel, transmitting torque to the gearbox.
- When the **clutch pedal is pressed**, the release levers pull the pressure plate back, the disc is freed, and torque transmission stops.

**Conditions for Theories:**

- **Uniform pressure theory** — applicable when the contact surfaces are **new or unworn**, and the surfaces are **rigid**.
- **Uniform wear theory** — applicable when the contact surfaces have **worn-in** over time and the wear is uniform across the contact area. Used in **long-term design calculations**.

---

#### **Solution to Part (b)**

**Given Data:**
- Number of driving discs = 3
- Number of driven discs = 3
- Total number of friction surfaces: $n = 3 + 3 - 1 = 5$ (because adjacent discs contribute one surface each, and there are 5 interfaces in a 6-disc stack).
- Inner radius $r = 75$ mm $= 0.075$ m
- Outer radius $R = 150$ mm $= 0.150$ m
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.3$
- Maximum pressure intensity $p = 0.6$ MPa $= 0.6 \times 10^6$ N/m²

**Step 1 — Axial Force W**

The maximum pressure $p$ acts at the inner radius. From uniform pressure theory:

$$
W \;=\; p \times \pi(R^{2} - r^{2})
$$

$$
W \;=\; 0.6 \times 10^{6} \times \pi \times \left[(0.150)^{2} - (0.075)^{2}\right]
$$

Compute $R^2 = 0.0225$ and $r^2 = 0.005625$:

$$
W \;=\; 0.6 \times 10^{6} \times \pi \times 0.016875
$$

$$
W \;=\; 0.6 \times 10^{6} \times 0.05301
$$

$$
\boxed{\,W \;\approx\; 31{,}808 \text{ N} \;\approx\; 31.81 \text{ kN}\,}
$$

**Step 2 — Torque Transmitted**

Using the uniform pressure formula:

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{2}{3} \times \mu \times W \times n \times \dfrac{R^{3} - r^{3}}{R^{2} - r^{2}}
$$

Compute $R^3 = 0.150^3 = 0.003375$ m³ and $r^3 = 0.075^3 = 0.000421875$ m³:

$$
R^{3} - r^{3} \;=\; 0.002953 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

$$
R^{2} - r^{2} \;=\; 0.016875 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
\dfrac{R^{3} - r^{3}}{R^{2} - r^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.002953}{0.016875} \;=\; 0.175 \text{ m}
$$

Now:

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{2}{3} \times 0.3 \times 31{,}808 \times 5 \times 0.175
$$

$$
T \;=\; 0.6667 \times 0.3 \times 31{,}808 \times 0.875
$$

$$
T \;=\; 0.6667 \times 0.3 \times 27{,}832
$$

$$
T \;\approx\; 0.6667 \times 8349.6
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T \;\approx\; 5566.4 \text{ N·m} \;\approx\; 5.57 \text{ kN·m}\,}
$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] (INTERNAL CHOICE)**

**(a)** Describe the **construction, working and applications of a centrifugal clutch**. Derive the expression for the **cut-in speed** of the clutch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **cone clutch** has a mean cone radius of **125 mm**, a **half-cone angle** of **12.5°**, and a coefficient of friction of **0.28**. The maximum axial thrust applied is **5 kN**. Determine the torque transmitted by the clutch, assuming **uniform wear theory**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a)**

**Construction:**

A centrifugal clutch consists of:

1. **Driver spider (or driving member)** — keyed to the engine crankshaft, rotating with the engine.
2. **Shoes (or flyweights)** — typically 3 or 4, pivoted on the spider.
3. **Springs** — hold the shoes in retracted position at low speeds.
4. **Drum (driven member)** — a cylindrical drum attached to the gearbox input shaft, surrounding the shoes.
5. **Friction lining** — on the outer face of each shoe.

**Working:**

- At low engine speeds (below cut-in), spring force exceeds centrifugal force → shoes remain retracted → no contact with the drum → **clutch is disengaged**.
- As engine speed increases, centrifugal force grows as $F_c = m\omega^2 r$ and eventually exceeds spring force.
- Shoes fly outward and press against the inner surface of the drum.
- Friction between shoes and drum transmits torque from the engine to the driven shaft.
- Engagement is **automatic and progressive** — engagement force increases with speed.

**Applications:**

- Scooters, mopeds, auto-rickshaws
- Small go-karts
- Lawn mowers and chain saws
- Centrifugal pumps and small machinery

**Derivation of Cut-in Speed:**

At the cut-in speed $\omega_c$, the centrifugal force on each shoe equals the spring force:

$$
m \, \omega_{c}^{2} \, r_s \;=\; F_s
$$

Solving:

$$
\boxed{\,\omega_{c} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{F_s}{m \, r_s}} \quad \text{and} \quad N_{c} \;=\; \dfrac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{F_s}{m \, r_s}}\,}
$$

where:
- $m$ = mass of one shoe
- $r_s$ = radial distance of shoe's center of mass from the shaft axis
- $F_s$ = spring preload force

---

#### **Solution to Part (b)**

**Given Data:**
- Mean cone radius $R_m = 125$ mm $= 0.125$ m
- Half-cone angle $\alpha = 12.5°$
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.28$
- Axial thrust $W = 5$ kN $= 5000$ N
- Number of friction surfaces $n = 1$ (single cone clutch)

**Step 1 — Formula for Cone Clutch (Uniform Wear)**

$$
T_{cone} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2} \times \mu \times W \times n \times (R + r) \times \dfrac{1}{\sin\alpha}
$$

For uniform wear, the mean radius approach is used with $R + r = 2R_m$:

$$
R + r \;=\; 2 \times 0.125 \;=\; 0.25 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sin\alpha$**

$$
\sin(12.5°) \;\approx\; 0.2164
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute**

$$
T_{cone} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.28 \times 5000 \times 1 \times 0.25 \times \dfrac{1}{0.2164}
$$

$$
T_{cone} \;=\; 0.5 \times 0.28 \times 5000 \times 0.25 \times 4.621
$$

$$
T_{cone} \;=\; 0.5 \times 0.28 \times 1250 \times 4.621
$$

$$
T_{cone} \;=\; 0.5 \times 350 \times 4.621
$$

$$
T_{cone} \;=\; 175 \times 4.621
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_{cone} \;\approx\; 808.7 \text{ N·m} \;\approx\; 0.81 \text{ kN·m}\,}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Read Carefully):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by $n$:** In multi-plate problems, students often use the single-plate formula and lose 2–3 marks. Always count surfaces: $n = (N_1 + N_2 - 1)$ where $N_1$ = driver discs and $N_2$ = driven discs.
>
> 2. **Mixing up the two theories:** Uniform pressure has the $\frac{2}{3}$ factor and the complex $R^3 - r^3 / R^2 - r^2$ ratio. Uniform wear is simple: $\frac{1}{2}\mu W(R+r)$. Do NOT mix them in one problem.
>
> 3. **Cone clutch — missing the $\sin\alpha$ factor:** Many students forget that cone clutch torque is divided by $\sin\alpha$ (or multiplied by $1/\sin\alpha$). This is THE most-missed step.
>
> 4. **Unit errors:** Pressure must be in N/m² (Pa), forces in N, radii in m. KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for unit mistakes.
>
> 5. **Confusing centroid radius with mean radius:** For uniform pressure, sometimes the centroid radius $\frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{R^3 - r^3}{R^2 - r^2}$ is used. For uniform wear, $\frac{R + r}{2}$ is the equivalent radius.
>
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** KTU expects you to state the **assumptions of each theory** explicitly — 1 mark is reserved for this in design questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam):**

### ✅ **Core Classification**
- Friction vs Positive Clutch — the master classification
- Friction: Single-plate, Multi-plate, Cone, Centrifugal
- Positive: Jaw (Dog), Spline

### ✅ **The Two Design Theories**
- **Uniform Pressure** → new, rigid surfaces
- **Uniform Wear** → long-term, worn-in surfaces (used in actual design)

### ✅ **The Five Must-Know Formulas**

$$
T_{u.p.} \;=\; \dfrac{2}{3} \, \mu \, W \, n \, \dfrac{R^{3} - r^{3}}{R^{2} - r^{2}}
$$

$$
T_{u.w.} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2} \, \mu \, W \, n \, (R + r)
$$

$$
T_{cone} \;=\; T_{u.w.} \times \dfrac{1}{\sin\alpha}
$$

$$
N_{c,centrifugal} \;=\; \dfrac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{F_s}{m \, r_s}}
$$

$$
n_{multi-plate} \;=\; N_{driver} + N_{driven} - 1
$$

### ✅ **Applications Mapping (Memorize)**
- Car clutch → Single plate
- Bike clutch → Multi-plate
- Scooter/moped → Centrifugal
- Industrial gearbox → Cone or jaw
- Constant-mesh gearbox → Spline
- Punch press / machine tool → Jaw/Dog

### ✅ **Common Viva Questions**
- Why is uniform wear theory used in design? *(Because clutch runs in worn state for 95% of life.)*
- Why is cone clutch torque divided by $\sin\alpha$? *(Because only the normal component contributes to friction; the axial push creates less normal force due to the wedge.)*
- Why can't a jaw clutch be engaged at speed? *(Teeth will chip due to impact; only at rest or synced speeds.)*
- Why multi-plate in bikes? *(To transmit high engine torque in a small radial space.)*

### ✅ **KTU-2024 Cognitive Level Tags**
- **Remember (L1):** Define, list types — 3 mark questions
- **Understand (L2):** Differentiate, explain working — 7 mark part-(a)
- **Apply (L3):** Numerical torque calculation — 7 mark part-(b)
- **Analyze (L4):** Compare two theories, justify design choice — comes in long answers
- **Evaluate (L5):** Select clutch type for given application — design-oriented 14 mark

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bearings and Their Classification (Journal Bearing and Ball Bearing)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO MECHANICAL ENGINEERING & CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bearings and Their Classification (Journal Bearing and Ball Bearing)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Bearing? (The Engineering Definition)

A **bearing** is a machine element whose primary function is to **support a rotating shaft** (or oscillating component) and to **transmit loads** from one part of a machine to another, while **permitting relative motion** between the loaded surfaces with **minimum friction and wear**.

In simple, plain English, every rotating shaft in every machine must be held in place somehow — and it must be allowed to spin freely. The component that does this job, that "holds the spinning shaft in mid-air so it can rotate," is called a **bearing**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Bearings are classified into two major families — **Plain (Sliding) Bearings** and **Rolling Contact (Antifriction) Bearings**. This module is a high-weightage, frequently-asked concept.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are sitting on a **swivel chair** in your study room. The chair's central pin lets you spin — that pin is essentially acting like a **journal bearing**. Now imagine a **skateboard wheel** — the balls/cylinders inside the wheel hub let the wheel roll smoothly — those act as **rolling contact bearings**.

So:
- A **journal bearing** = two surfaces **sliding past each other** (like a door hinge).
- A **ball/roller bearing** = little balls/rollers **rolling between** the surfaces (like ball bearings in a fidget spinner).

> [!NOTE]
> The **journal bearing** is a *plain bearing* — no rolling elements. The shaft (journal) slides directly on a thin film of oil inside the bearing shell. The **ball bearing** is a *rolling contact* bearing — steel balls roll between the inner and outer races.

## 1.3 The Two Master Categories of Bearings

$$\text{Bearings} \; \Big\{ \begin{array}{l} \text{1. Plain / Sliding Bearings (Journal Bearing)} \\ \text{2. Rolling Contact Bearings (Ball, Roller, Needle)} \end{array}$$

| Feature | Journal (Plain) Bearing | Ball (Rolling) Bearing |
|---|---|---|
| **Type of Motion** | Sliding contact | Rolling contact |
| **Starting Friction** | High (≈ 2 to 5× running friction) | Very low |
| **Lubricant Need** | Continuous oil supply | Grease or oil (long interval) |
| **Load Capacity** | Very high (slow-speed, heavy machinery) | Moderate to high |
| **Speed Capability** | Moderate to high | Very high (10,000+ rpm) |
| **Radial Play** | Adjustable via shims | Fixed (manufactured) |
| **Typical Applications** | Steam turbines, IC engine crankshafts, large electric motors, sugar mill rolls | Electric motors, pumps, gearboxes, wheels, machine tool spindles |

## 1.4 Formal Definition of a Journal Bearing

A **journal bearing** is a **sliding-contact bearing** in which the **journal (shaft)** is enclosed by and rotates within a **bearing shell (sleeve)**. The two surfaces are completely separated by a **pressurized hydrodynamic film of lubricant** (in full-film operation) so that metal-to-metal contact is avoided.

The contact between the journal and the shell is along a **partial cylindrical surface** — typically spanning an arc of **120°** in a partial journal bearing, or the **full 360°** in a full journal bearing.

## 1.5 Formal Definition of a Ball Bearing

A **ball (or rolling-element) bearing** is a bearing in which the **load is transmitted through spherical rolling elements (balls)** that are interposed between an **inner race** (mounted on the shaft) and an **outer race** (mounted in the housing). A **cage (retainer)** keeps the balls evenly spaced around the circumference and prevents them from touching each other.

## 1.6 Key Terminology (Must Remember for KTU Exams)

- **Journal**: The part of the shaft that is supported by the bearing.
- **Bearing Shell / Liner / Bush**: The stationary cylindrical surface that supports the journal.
- **Housing / Pedestal / Saddle**: The structural block that holds the bearing shell.
- **Inner Race**: The ring mounted on the rotating shaft.
- **Outer Race**: The ring mounted in the stationary housing.
- **Cage / Retainer**: Holds the rolling elements at uniform spacing.
- **Radial Load (Fr)**: Load perpendicular to the shaft axis.
- **Thrust / Axial Load (Fa)**: Load parallel to the shaft axis.
- **Lubricant**: Oil or grease that separates the moving surfaces.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Journal Bearing

A typical **journal (plain) bearing** consists of the following parts:

1. **The Journal** — the rotating portion of the shaft itself.
2. **The Bearing Shell (Bush / Bushing)** — a replaceable sleeve (often made of babbitt, bronze, or PTFE) that forms the bearing surface.
3. **The Housing (Saddle / Pedestal)** — supports the shell and carries the load to the foundation.
4. **The Lubricant Supply System** — oil rings, splash, or pressurized feed.
5. **The End Thrust Collars** (if axial load exists) — control axial movement of the shaft.

> [!NOTE]
> The *babbitt* (an alloy of tin, copper, and antimony) is the classic soft bearing liner material because it has excellent **embeddability** (absorbs small dust particles without scoring the journal) and **conformability** (wears to match slight misalignment of the journal).

## 2.2 Constructional Details of a Journal Bearing

- The bearing shell is **split horizontally** (cap + base) for easy assembly and to permit **axial loading**.
- **Adjustable shims** are inserted between the cap and the housing to control the **radial clearance (c)**.
- The shell is **shorter than the journal** (typically L = 0.5 D to 2.0 D) so that oil can flow in axially through the sides, providing the **side-leakage flow** essential for hydrodynamic pressure generation.
- An **oil hole** is provided in the upper part of the shell to admit lubricant.
- The **load line** of a typical radial journal bearing passes through the **center of the unloaded bearing**, at an angle of about 60° to 90° from the line of centers.

## 2.3 Lubrication Regimes in Journal Bearings (Very High-Yield for KTU)

The mode of lubrication is decided by the **lambda ratio (λ)** = (actual film thickness) / (combined surface roughness):

1. **Boundary Lubrication** (λ < 1): Surfaces touch at asperities. Friction is high. Wear occurs.
2. **Mixed Lubrication** (1 ≤ λ < 3): Partial asperity contact + partial fluid film.
3. **Hydrodynamic / Full-Film Lubrication** (λ ≥ 3): Surfaces fully separated by a pressurized oil wedge. **This is the design goal of every journal bearing.**
4. **Hydrostatic Lubrication**: Oil is supplied under external pressure even when the shaft is stationary (used in heavy slow-speed machinery such as rolling mills and turbines).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Reynolds Equation** (1886) mathematically describes the pressure build-up in the oil film. It is the foundation of hydrodynamic bearing theory and is the theoretical basis for the Sommerfeld number.

## 2.4 Theory of Hydrodynamic Lubrication — The "Why" Behind the Oil Wedge

When the shaft (journal) starts to rotate inside a slightly larger bearing shell, **viscous drag** pulls the oil into the converging wedge-shaped clearance space between the journal and the shell. Because the oil cannot escape fast enough through the side leakage, a **pressure peak** is generated in the wedge. This pressure is sufficient to **lift the journal off the shell** and to **support the applied radial load**.

This is the **Tower's discovery (1883) and Reynolds' mathematical proof (1886)** — the cornerstone of fluid-film bearing theory.

The journal therefore does **not** sit in the geometric center of the shell. It is displaced from the center by a distance **e** (the **eccentricity**), and the line of centers rotates through an angle **φ (attitude angle)** relative to the load line.

## 2.5 Anatomy of a Ball Bearing (Deep-Dive Construction)

A deep-groove ball bearing has the following components:

1. **Outer Race** — fixed in the housing; has a curved (groove) track for the balls.
2. **Inner Race** — fitted on the shaft; also has a curved track.
3. **Balls** — precision-ground steel (or ceramic) spheres of high hardness.
4. **Cage / Retainer** — keeps the balls equally spaced and prevents ball-on-ball contact.
5. **Shields (Z)** or **Seals (2RS)** — optional; prevent entry of dust and leakage of grease.
6. **Radial play / Internal clearance** — small intentional gap that allows thermal expansion and slight misalignment.

> [!TIP]
> A common exam line: *"A deep-groove ball bearing can carry both radial and a small axial load in either direction, but for pure thrust loads, a dedicated **thrust ball bearing** is used."*

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units (SI) | Definition of Symbols |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Projected Bearing Area** | $A = L \times D$ | m² | L = bearing length, D = journal diameter |
| 2 | **Unit Bearing Pressure** | $P = \dfrac{W}{L \times D}$ | N/m² (Pa) | W = radial load on bearing |
| 3 | **Radial Clearance** | $c = \dfrac{D - d}{2}$ | m | D = bore (bearing ID), d = journal (shaft) dia. |
| 4 | **Clearance Ratio** | $\dfrac{c}{r} \; \text{or} \; \dfrac{C}{D}$ | dimensionless | r = D/2, typical 0.001 to 0.002 |
| 5 | **Eccentricity Ratio** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{e}{c}$ | dimensionless | e = eccentricity (offset of journal from bearing center) |
| 6 | **Petroff's Friction (f)** | $f = 2\pi^2 \, \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{\mu N}{P}\right)$ | dimensionless | μ = dynamic viscosity, N = speed in rev/s |
| 7 | **Sommerfeld Number (So)** | $S_o = \dfrac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$ | dimensionless | Also written as $S_o = \dfrac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \left(\dfrac{D}{2c}\right)^2$ |
| 8 | **Coefficient of Friction (360°)** | $f = 2\pi^2 \, \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right) \cdot \dfrac{S_o}{\sqrt{S_o^2 - 1}}$ | dimensionless | Approximate for full journal bearing |
| 9 | **Heat Generated (Hi)** | $H_g = f \, W \, V$ | W (J/s) | V = sliding velocity = πDN/60 |
| 10 | **Heat Dissipated (Ho)** | $H_d = C \, A_b \, (t_1 - t_2)$ | W | C = heat transfer coefficient, A_b = bearing housing surface area, t = temperature |
| 11 | **Heat Balance Equation** | $f \, W \, V = C \, A_b \, (t_1 - t_2)$ | W | Used to find the **equilibrium oil temperature** |
| 12 | **Minimum Film Thickness (h₀)** | $h_0 = c \, (1 - \varepsilon)$ | m | Must be > combined surface roughness |
| 13 | **Ball Bearing Life (L₁₀)** | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | million revs | p = 3 for ball, 10/3 for roller |
| 14 | **Equivalent Dynamic Load (P)** | $P = X \, F_r + Y \, F_a$ | N | X, Y from manufacturer tables |
| 15 | **Basic Dynamic Load Rating (C)** | from manufacturer catalogue | N | Load for 1 million rev life |
| 16 | **Viscosity (Newton's Law)** | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{(du/dy)}$ | N·s/m² | τ = shear stress, du/dy = velocity gradient |
| 17 | **Sliding Velocity** | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s | N in rpm |
| 18 | **Kinematic Viscosity** | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | m²/s | ρ = density of oil |
| 19 | **PV Product** | $PV = P \times V$ | Pa·m/s | Indicates bearing heating — used for plastic bushes |
| 20 | **Bearing Characteristic Number** | $\dfrac{\mu N}{P}$ | dimensionless | Often tabulated in design charts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MNTC (Must Know to Calculate)**: The **Sommerfeld Number** is the most important non-dimensional parameter in journal-bearing analysis. It links viscosity, speed, pressure, and clearance — the four physical quantities that govern bearing performance. **Practically every KTU numerical problem asks for the Sommerfeld number, coefficient of friction, or heat balance.**

## 2.7 Real-World Utility (Why KTU Tests This Concept)

The performance of **every rotating machine** on earth — from a ceiling fan motor, to the crankshaft of a Maruti car, to a 500 MW steam turbine, to a jet engine rotor, to a wind turbine gearbox — is fundamentally **limited by its bearings**. Selecting the right bearing, the right lubricant, and the right clearance determines:

- **Energy efficiency** (lower friction = lower electricity bill)
- **Machine life** (proper lubrication avoids wear and seizure)
- **Operating temperature** (oil temperature determines oil life)
- **Noise and vibration** (critical for EVs and aerospace)

Hence, KTU tests this topic across **every discipline** — Mechanical, Automobile, Aeronautical, Electrical, and even Civil (large rotating machinery in water-pumping stations).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Petroff's Equation for Friction Torque in a Full Journal Bearing (Lossless, Concentric Case)

### 3.1.1 Assumptions
- The journal is **concentric** with the bearing (no eccentricity → only true for very high speeds or no-load case).
- The clearance space is **very small** (c ≪ r).
- The flow is **laminar**.
- The lubricant is **Newtonian** with viscosity μ.
- The bearing is **infinitely long** (no side leakage).
- The bearing is **lightly loaded** (no pressure build-up).

### 3.1.2 Setup
Let:
- R = radius of bearing (m)
- r = radius of journal (m)
- c = radial clearance = R − r (m)
- L = axial length of bearing (m)
- N = rotational speed (rev/s)
- ω = angular velocity = 2πN (rad/s)
- U = surface velocity of journal = rω = 2πrN (m/s)

### 3.1.3 Velocity Distribution in the Clearance Gap

Choose a coordinate **y** measured **radially outward** from the journal surface (y = 0 at the journal, y = c at the bearing surface).

Because c ≪ r, the clearance gap is treated as a **straight channel of height c** — this is the classic **"unwrapped journal bearing"** approximation.

Apply **Newton's law of viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy}$$

For a concentric journal with no pressure gradient in the axial direction (and considering the journal as a rotating cylinder dragging the oil over a stationary cylinder), the velocity distribution **u(y)** is **linear**:

$$u(y) = U \cdot \left(\dfrac{y}{c}\right)$$

**Step 1: Velocity at the journal surface** (y = 0): $u = 0$ (relative to journal).
**Step 2: Velocity at the bearing surface** (y = c): $u = U$ (no slip on the stationary wall — but in the journal frame, this is the relative velocity of the wall).

### 3.1.4 Shear Stress on the Journal Surface

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy} = \mu \cdot \dfrac{U}{c}$$

### 3.1.5 Friction Force on the Journal

The journal has a surface area of $A = 2\pi r L$. Therefore, the total **frictional (viscous) force** opposing rotation is:

$$F_f = \tau \cdot A = \mu \cdot \dfrac{U}{c} \cdot 2 \pi r L$$

### 3.1.6 Friction Torque

The friction torque $T_f$ is the friction force multiplied by the journal radius r:

$$T_f = F_f \cdot r = \mu \cdot \dfrac{U}{c} \cdot 2 \pi r L \cdot r$$

Substitute $U = 2\pi r N$:

$$T_f = \mu \cdot \dfrac{2\pi r N}{c} \cdot 2 \pi r^2 L = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c}$$

### 3.1.7 Coefficient of Friction (f)

The **coefficient of friction** is defined as:

$$f = \dfrac{F_f}{W} = \dfrac{\text{Friction Force}}{\text{Load on Bearing}}$$

From Petroff's setup, $F_f = \mu \cdot \dfrac{U}{c} \cdot 2 \pi r L = \dfrac{2 \pi \mu N \cdot 2 \pi r^2 L}{c} = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu N r^2 L}{c}$

Using the unit pressure definition $P = \dfrac{W}{L D} = \dfrac{W}{2 r L} \;\Rightarrow\; W = 2 P r L$:

$$f = \dfrac{F_f}{W} = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu N r^2 L / c}{2 P r L} = \dfrac{2 \pi^2 \mu N r}{P c}$$

Replacing r/c with (r/c) and noting that 2πr/c = 2π(r/c):

$$\boxed{f = 2 \pi^2 \, \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{\mu N}{P}\right)}$$

This is the famous **Petroff's equation** (1883), giving the coefficient of friction in an **unloaded, concentric journal bearing**.

### 3.1.8 The Sommerfeld Number (Dimensionless Group)

Define the **Sommerfeld Number** as:

$$\boxed{S_o = \dfrac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \dfrac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \left(\dfrac{D}{2c}\right)^2}$$

So Petroff's equation can be rewritten as:

$$f = 2 \pi^2 \, \sqrt{S_o \cdot \left(\dfrac{c}{r}\right)^{-2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\mu N}{P}\right)} \quad\longrightarrow\quad \text{Useful in 360° Babbitt design charts.}$$

For a **lightly loaded full journal bearing**, the **actual coefficient of friction** is approximately:

$$f \approx 2 \pi^2 \, \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right) \cdot \dfrac{S_o}{\sqrt{S_o^2 - 1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Petroff's equation **underestimates** the actual friction at low Sommerfeld numbers because the assumption of concentricity breaks down. It is therefore valid only for **very high speeds or zero load**. Real bearings have eccentricity and develop a **pressure wedge**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Minimum Oil-Film Thickness h₀

From the geometry of an eccentric journal inside its bearing:

The minimum radial gap occurs at the location **opposite the loaded zone** (where the journal surface comes closest to the bearing wall).

$$\boxed{h_0 = c \, (1 - \varepsilon)}$$

where:
- $h_0$ = minimum film thickness (m)
- $c$ = radial clearance (m)
- $\varepsilon = e/c$ = eccentricity ratio (0 ≤ ε ≤ 1)
- $e$ = eccentricity = distance between bearing center $O_B$ and journal center $O_J$

**Interpretation:**
- ε = 0 → concentric journal, $h_0 = c$ (maximum possible film).
- ε = 1 → journal touches the bearing (metal-to-metal contact), $h_0 = 0$ (failure).

**Design rule (McKee's):** $h_0 \geq 0.00025$ inch (≈ 6 μm) for proper hydrodynamic operation.

## 3.3 Derivation of Heat-Generated and Heat-Dis sipated

### 3.3.1 Heat Generated (Frictional Power Loss)

Power lost to friction:

$$H_g = f \cdot W \cdot V$$

where $V = \pi D N / 60$ is the mean sliding velocity of the journal in m/s.

**Derivation step-by-step:**

The friction force is $F_f = fW$. The journal surface moves at velocity $V$. Work done per second = Force × Velocity.

$$H_g = F_f \cdot V = f \cdot W \cdot V \quad \text{(watts)}$$

### 3.3.2 Heat Dissipated (from Housing Surface to Ambient Air)

By Newton's law of cooling from the bearing housing surface to the surrounding air:

$$H_d = C \cdot A_b \cdot (t_1 - t_2)$$

where:
- $C$ = overall heat-transfer coefficient from housing to air (typical value **35 to 45 W/m²·°C** for still air, higher with fan cooling)
- $A_b$ = bare area of bearing housing (m²)
- $t_1$ = temperature of bearing housing (°C)
- $t_2$ = ambient air temperature (°C)

### 3.3.3 Heat Balance (Steady State)

At equilibrium (steady running temperature), the heat generated equals the heat dissipated:

$$\boxed{f \cdot W \cdot V = C \cdot A_b \cdot (t_1 - t_2)}$$

This equation is used to find the **operating oil temperature** $t_1$ for a given load W, speed N, and design parameters.

## 3.4 Derivation of Equivalent Dynamic Load on a Ball Bearing

For a ball bearing subjected to **both** a radial load $F_r$ and a thrust (axial) load $F_a$, the manufacturer publishes tables of factors $X$ (radial factor) and $Y$ (thrust factor) and $e$ (threshold ratio). The equivalent load is:

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

**Case 1: $F_a / F_r \leq e$** → Thrust is negligible.

$$P = F_r$$

**Case 2: $F_a / F_r > e$** → Thrust is significant.

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The values of X, Y, and e are read from the bearing manufacturer's catalogue (SKF, FAG, NTN) and depend on the **type of bearing** (deep-groove, angular-contact, etc.) and the **$F_a/C_0$** ratio. KTU expects you to use tabulated values for a standard deep-groove ball bearing.

## 3.5 Derivation of Bearing Life Equation (L₁₀)

The **basic dynamic load rating (C)** is defined as the constant radial load that a bearing can carry for **1 million revolutions** with a 90% reliability.

For a load P smaller than C, the bearing life increases as:

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p \quad \text{(million revolutions)}$$

where:
- $L_{10}$ = rated life (90% reliability)
- $p = 3$ for **ball bearings**
- $p = 10/3 \approx 3.33$ for **roller bearings**

**Conversion to hours** (if N is in rpm):

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \, N} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p = \dfrac{16666}{N} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p \quad \text{(hours)}$$

**Derivation logic:** Contact-stress theory (Hertz) shows that for point contact (balls) the fatigue life follows a **cubic law** (p = 3), and for line contact (rollers) it follows a **10/3 law**. This was experimentally verified by Lundberg and Palmgren (1947).

## 3.6 KTU Numerical-Style Worked Example (14-Mark Model Problem)

**Problem:** A full journal bearing has the following data:
- Journal diameter D = 100 mm
- Bearing length L = 100 mm  
- Radial load W = 10,000 N
- Speed N = 1440 rpm
- Viscosity of oil μ = 0.020 N·s/m²
- Clearance ratio c/r = 0.001
- Ambient temperature t₂ = 30 °C
- Bearing housing surface area A_b = 0.5 m²
- Heat transfer coefficient C = 40 W/m²·°C

**Find:** (a) Sommerfeld number, (b) Coefficient of friction, (c) Heat generated, (d) Steady-state oil temperature.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**(a) Sommerfeld Number:**

First, convert: N in rev/s = 1440/60 = 24 rev/s; r = 50 mm = 0.05 m; c = 0.001 × 0.05 = 0.00005 m; c/r = 0.001.

Unit pressure:

$$P = \dfrac{W}{L D} = \dfrac{10000}{(0.1)(0.1)} = 1{,}000{,}000 \; \text{Pa} = 1 \; \text{MPa}$$

Now compute $\mu N / P$:

$$\dfrac{\mu N}{P} = \dfrac{0.020 \times 24}{1{,}000{,}000} = 4.8 \times 10^{-7}$$

Sommerfeld number:

$$S_o = \left(\dfrac{\mu N}{P}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2 = 4.8 \times 10^{-7} \times (1000)^2 = 0.48$$

**(b) Coefficient of Friction (full bearing, 360°):**

$$f = 2\pi^2 \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right) \cdot \dfrac{S_o}{\sqrt{S_o^2 - 1}}$$

$$f = 2\pi^2 \times 1000 \times \dfrac{0.48}{\sqrt{0.48^2 - 1}}$$

$$\sqrt{0.2304 - 1} = \sqrt{-0.7696} = \text{imaginary!}$$

This means the **Sommerfeld number is too low** to support a 360° full bearing with hydrodynamic film. We would need a **partial journal bearing** (e.g., 120° arc) to handle this load. For the academic answer in KTU, we use Petroff's approximation:

$$f = 2\pi^2 \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right) \left(\dfrac{\mu N}{P}\right) = 2\pi^2 \times 1000 \times 4.8 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$f = 19.74 \times 1000 \times 4.8 \times 10^{-7} = 0.00948 \approx 0.0095$$

**(c) Heat Generated:**

Sliding velocity V = πDN/60 = π × 0.1 × 1440 / 60 = 7.54 m/s.

$$H_g = f \cdot W \cdot V = 0.00948 \times 10000 \times 7.54 = 714.8 \; \text{W}$$

**(d) Steady-State Oil Temperature (Heat Balance):**

$$f W V = C A_b (t_1 - t_2)$$

$$714.8 = 40 \times 0.5 \times (t_1 - 30)$$

$$t_1 - 30 = \dfrac{714.8}{20} = 35.74$$

$$\boxed{t_1 = 65.74 \; °C}$$

This is the **equilibrium oil temperature** — safe below the typical 90 °C limit for mineral oils.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's favourite twist**: If $S_o < 1$ for a 360° bearing, the bearing will be in the boundary/mixed lubrication regime — the answer should mention that the **bearing should be redesigned** (e.g., use a partial bearing, increase viscosity, or reduce clearance) to get $S_o \geq 1$.
