# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define a bearing. Classify bearings with two examples for each class.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A bearing is a machine element that **supports a rotating shaft** and **permits relative motion** with **minimum friction** between the loaded parts.

**Classification:**

1. **Plain / Sliding Bearings** — the two surfaces slide against each other. *Example*: Journal bearing in a steam turbine; bushing in a lathe headstock.
2. **Rolling Contact Bearings** — rolling elements (balls/rollers) are interposed between the races. *Example*: Deep-groove ball bearing in a ceiling fan motor; tapered roller bearing in a railway axle box.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: What is a journal bearing? State the function of (i) the babbitt lining, and (ii) the oil hole.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **journal bearing** is a plain cylindrical bearing in which the **journal (rotating part of the shaft)** slides on a thin film of oil inside a **stationary bearing shell**.

(i) **Babbitt lining** — A soft alloy (tin-copper-antimony) bonded to the inside of the bearing shell. It provides **embeddability** (absorbs small dust particles without scoring the journal) and **conformability** (wears to accommodate slight shaft misalignment). It also has good **antifriction and antiseizure** properties.

(ii) **Oil hole** — Provided in the **unloaded upper region** of the shell to admit lubricant into the clearance space. It is placed in the low-pressure zone to avoid disturbing the hydrodynamic pressure wedge.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice 14-Mark Format)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 ESE, Part B of each module contains a **choice question** (you answer either A or B). Below are two complete 14-mark options — **Question A** and **Question B** — with sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q: (a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **journal bearing**. Explain the function of the oil film and the concept of **hydrodynamic lubrication**. (7 marks)

**(b) A full journal bearing has the following data: Diameter D = 120 mm, Length L = 120 mm, Load W = 25 kN, Speed N = 900 rpm, Viscosity μ = 0.025 N·s/m², Clearance ratio c/r = 0.001. Calculate (i) Sommerfeld number, (ii) Coefficient of friction, (iii) Heat generated.** (7 marks)

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q: (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a deep-groove ball bearing**. List its **merits and demerits** over a journal bearing. (7 marks)

**(b) A ball bearing has a basic dynamic load rating C = 35 kN. It carries an equivalent dynamic load P = 8 kN. Calculate the (i) L₁₀ life in million revolutions, (ii) L₁₀h life in hours if the speed is 1500 rpm.** (7 marks)

---

### ✅ Detailed Model Solutions

#### Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Construction & Working of Journal Bearing (7 marks):**

A **journal bearing** consists of:

1. **Journal** — the rotating part of the shaft.
2. **Bearing shell (bush)** — a replaceable cylindrical sleeve, lined inside with **babbitt** (soft white-metal alloy).
3. **Housing / pedestal** — the rigid block supporting the shell.
4. **Cap** — the upper half of the housing, bolted to the base.
5. **Adjusting shims** — placed between cap and base to set the **radial clearance**.
6. **Oil hole** — drilled in the upper (unloaded) region of the shell.
7. **End-thrust collars** — for axial location of the shaft.

**Working — Hydrodynamic Lubrication:**

When the shaft rotates inside the shell, the **viscous drag** of the oil pulls the lubricant into the **converging wedge** between the journal and the bearing. Because the oil cannot escape quickly through the **side leakage** at the ends, a **hydrodynamic pressure** is generated. This pressure is high enough to **lift the journal off the shell** and support the applied radial load on a thin, self-sustaining oil film (typically 5–50 μm thick).

The function of the oil film is to:
- **Separate** the two metal surfaces → eliminates wear.
- **Reduce friction** → coefficient of friction drops from 0.1 (dry) to 0.001 (hydrodynamic).
- **Carry away heat** and flush out wear debris.
- **Cushion impact loads** and damp vibrations.

> **Diagram (textbook sketch):** Cross-section showing the journal offset from the bearing center by eccentricity e, attitude angle φ, minimum film thickness h₀, and the pressure wedge.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** D = 0.12 m, L = 0.12 m, W = 25,000 N, N = 900/60 = 15 rev/s, μ = 0.025 N·s/m², c/r = 0.001.

**Step 1 — Unit Pressure:**

$$P = \dfrac{W}{L D} = \dfrac{25000}{0.12 \times 0.12} = 1{,}736{,}111 \; \text{Pa} \approx 1.736 \; \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute μN/P:**

$$\dfrac{\mu N}{P} = \dfrac{0.025 \times 15}{1{,}736{,}111} = 2.16 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 3 — Sommerfeld Number:**

$$S_o = \left(\dfrac{\mu N}{P}\right) \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2 = 2.16 \times 10^{-7} \times (1000)^2 = 0.216$$

**Step 4 — Coefficient of Friction (Petroff's):**

$$f = 2\pi^2 \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right) \left(\dfrac{\mu N}{P}\right) = 2\pi^2 \times 1000 \times 2.16 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$f = 19.74 \times 1000 \times 2.16 \times 10^{-7} = 4.265 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.00427$$

**Step 5 — Heat Generated:**

Sliding velocity $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.12 \times 900}{60} = 5.655$ m/s.

$$H_g = f \cdot W \cdot V = 0.00427 \times 25000 \times 5.655 = 603.7 \; \text{W} \approx 0.604 \; \text{kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap**: Students frequently forget to convert N from **rpm to rev/s** (or vice versa for V in m/s). The unit of N in μN must be rev/s if V is in m/s.
> 2. **Sommerfeld Formula Order**: Forgetting the **square** on (r/c) is a very common error. The formula is $(\mu N / P) \times (r/c)^2$, not $(\mu N / P) \times (r/c)$.
> 3. **360° vs 120° Bearing**: If the problem says "120° partial bearing" you **must** use Raimondi-Boyd charts (cannot be solved by Petroff's). The question will specify.
> 4. **Sign of $\sqrt{S_o^2 - 1}$**: When $S_o < 1$, the bearing cannot sustain full hydrodynamic film — state this explicitly in the answer for full marks.
> 5. **L₁₀ Life**: Don't confuse the exponent p = 3 (ball) with p = 10/3 (roller). KTU may give either type — read the question carefully.

---

#### Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Construction & Working of Deep-Groove Ball Bearing (7 marks):**

**Components:**
1. **Outer race** — stationary ring fixed in the housing. Has a deep, curved groove (raceway).
2. **Inner race** — rotates with the shaft. Also has a curved groove of matching radius.
3. **Balls** — precision-ground, high-hardness steel spheres (10 mm to 50 mm diameter).
4. **Cage (retainer)** — pressed-steel or brass piece that spaces the balls uniformly around the circumference.
5. **Seals / shields (optional)** — rubber seals (2RS) or metal shields (Z) to retain grease and exclude dust.

**Working:** The load is transmitted from one race to the other **through the balls** which roll along the curved raceways. Because the contact between ball and race is **theoretical point contact (Hertzian)**, the contact patch is very small → high contact stress, but rolling friction is far lower than sliding friction. The cage prevents the balls from colliding with each other and guides them in their orbit. The internal clearance allows thermal expansion and slight misalignment.

**Merits over journal bearing:**
- **Very low starting friction** (no static-to-kinetic friction jump).
- **Less lubricant** required (often grease-lubricated for life).
- **Standardised sizes** (no custom design needed).
- **Lower running temperature** and longer life at high speeds.
- **Smaller axial footprint**.

**Demerits:**
- **Limited shock/impact load** capacity (point contact → high Hertzian stress).
- **Sensitive to contamination** — dust causes rapid failure.
- **Higher cost** and **noisy** at very high speeds.
- **Limited to moderate temperatures** (grease limit ≈ 120 °C).
- **Difficult to repair** in the field.

**Part (b) — Ball Bearing Life Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** C = 35 kN = 35,000 N, P = 8 kN = 8,000 N, N = 1500 rpm, p = 3 (ball bearing).

**Step 1 — L₁₀ in million revolutions:**

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p = \left(\dfrac{35000}{8000}\right)^3 = (4.375)^3$$

$$L_{10} = 4.375 \times 4.375 \times 4.375 = 83.74 \; \text{million revolutions}$$

**Step 2 — L₁₀ in hours:**

Number of revolutions per hour = 1500 × 60 = 90,000 rev/hr.

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{L_{10} \times 10^6}{90{,}000} = \dfrac{83.74 \times 10^6}{90{,}000} = 930.4 \; \text{hours}$$

**Step 3 — In days (optional):**

$$L_{10d} = \dfrac{930.4}{24} = 38.77 \; \text{days of continuous operation}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall for Ball Bearing Life**
>
> 1. **Exponent confusion**: p = 3 for ball, p = 10/3 for roller. Wrong exponent = **full 0 marks** for the calculation.
> 2. **L₁₀ vs L₅₀**: L₁₀ is **90% reliability** (the KTU default). Don't divide by another reliability factor unless asked.
> 3. **Units in C and P**: Both **must** be in the same units (N or kN — the ratio is dimensionless, but stay consistent).
> 4. **Hours conversion**: Revolutions per hour = N (rpm) × 60. Not N × 3600 — that's revolutions per **day**-ish.
> 5. **Equivalent load**: If only radial load is given, P = F_r. If axial + radial, you **must** apply $P = XF_r + YF_a$ from the catalogue. KTU often provides X, Y, e values in the question itself.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION TABLE** — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.

- ✅ A **bearing** supports a rotating shaft and reduces friction.
- ✅ **Two main classes** — Plain (sliding) and Rolling (antifriction).
- ✅ **Journal bearing** = sliding contact, hydrodynamic oil film, used in heavy slow-speed machinery.
- ✅ **Ball bearing** = rolling contact, used in moderate-to-high-speed precision machinery.
- ✅ **Petroff's equation** (concentric, lightly loaded): $f = 2\pi^2 (r/c)(\mu N / P)$.
- ✅ **Sommerfeld Number**: $S_o = (\mu N / P)(r/c)^2$ — the most important non-dimensional number.
- ✅ For $S_o \geq 1$ the bearing can sustain full hydrodynamic film in a 360° journal.
- ✅ **Minimum film thickness** $h_0 = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ must be greater than combined surface roughness (rule: $h_0 \geq 6 \mu m$).
- ✅ **Heat balance** at steady state: $fWV = C \cdot A_b (t_1 - t_2)$ — used to find equilibrium oil temperature.
- ✅ **Babbitt** = soft white-metal bearing liner; embeddability + conformability.
- ✅ **Deep-groove ball bearing** can take both radial and small axial load.
- ✅ **Angular-contact ball bearing** takes combined radial + axial loads (used in pairs).
- ✅ **L₁₀ life of ball bearing** = $(C/P)^3$ million revolutions.
- ✅ **L₁₀ life of roller bearing** = $(C/P)^{10/3}$ million revolutions.
- ✅ **Equivalent dynamic load** on a ball bearing: $P = XF_r + YF_a$ (or $P = F_r$ if $F_a/F_r \leq e$).
- ✅ **Sliding velocity** of journal: $V = \pi D N / 60$ m/s (N in rpm).
- ✅ **Unit pressure** on bearing: $P = W / (L \times D)$ Pa.
- ✅ **Cage / retainer** in a ball bearing prevents ball-to-ball contact and friction.
- ✅ **Hydrodynamic** lubrication (Reynolds 1886) is the **design goal** of every journal bearing.
- ✅ **Hydrostatic** lubrication uses an external oil pump — for very heavy, slow-speed loads.
- ✅ **Mixed & Boundary lubrication** → metal-to-metal contact occurs → not desired, but unavoidable at start-up.
- ✅ **Standard $C$ rating** = the load for 1 million revs at 90% reliability (L₁₀).
- ✅ **KV value / viscosity index** of oil matters more than brand name — the same oil can be too thin in summer and too thick in winter.
- ✅ **Always** convert N to **rev/s** when μN appears in Petroff's or Sommerfeld.
- ✅ **Always** convert D, L, c to **metres** before computing pressures in Pascals.
- ✅ In KTU Part-A, give a **definition** + a **small sketch description** + a **one-line example** for full 3 marks.
- ✅ In KTU Part-B numericals, **show the formula, substitute the values, write the unit, and box the final answer** — this is the KTU valuation pattern.
- ✅ When $S_o < 1$ for 360° bearing, **conclude** that a partial bearing or viscosity/clearance redesign is needed — this is a favourite examiner's "comment" question worth 1–2 marks.

> **END OF MODULE 2 — BEARINGS NOTES**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manufacturing Processes: Sand Casting, Forging, Rolling, Extrusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO MECHANICAL ENGINEERING & CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Manufacturing Processes: Sand Casting, Forging, Rolling, Extrusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Manufacturing Processes?

A **Manufacturing Process** is a sequence of operations used by the industry to transform raw materials (metals, plastics, ceramics, composites) into finished, useful products or parts of desired geometry, size, surface finish, and mechanical properties. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, manufacturing processes are broadly classified into four families based on the type of material state during processing: **Casting (liquid/solidification route), Forming (plastic deformation of solid route), Machining (material removal route), and Joining (assembly route)**.

The four processes covered in this module — **Sand Casting, Forging, Rolling, and Extrusion** — belong to the first two families and are foundational pillars of Mechanical and Civil Engineering practice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The focus of Module 2 is to give Civil & allied students a working knowledge of *how* the basic engineering components (I-beams, pipes, shafts, machine housings) they see on construction sites are actually *made* in industry. Expect descriptive, comparison-based, and basic-numerical questions in ESE.

---

## 1.2 Process-wise Definitions & Real-World Analogies

### 1.2.1 Sand Casting

**Formal Definition (KTU):** Sand Casting is a *primary* (liquid-to-solid) metal forming process in which molten metal is poured into a one-time, disposable sand mold cavity shaped by a pattern, allowed to solidify, and then the casting is removed by breaking the mold. It is the most widely used casting process in terms of tonnage and number of castings produced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:** A mold made of compacted sand grains bonded by clay or chemical binders is the *die* of the process. It is used only once, making it ideal for *low-volume, large-sized, complex-shaped* products.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of making a *gulab jamun* or *modak* at home. You take a flour dough (sand-clay mixture), press a hand-shaped object (pattern) into it to make a cavity, remove the object, pour hot sugar syrup or ghee (molten metal) into the cavity, let it cool, and finally break the dough to release the sweet. The dough is destroyed, just like the sand mold — but the sweet (casting) is born. This is exactly how a sand cast pump housing or a manhole cover is produced.

> [!TIP]
> **Civil Engineering Tie-in:** All cast iron manhole covers, drainage gratings, and large valve bodies used in water supply / sanitation projects are sand cast.

---

### 1.2.2 Forging

**Formal Definition (KTU):** Forging is a *hot or cold* metal forming process in which compressive forces are applied to a solid metal billet (workpiece) using a hammer, press, or die set, causing plastic deformation and shape change **without any removal of material** and with significant grain-flow refinement. The process improves the mechanical strength, fatigue life, and impact resistance of the part.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:** The metal never melts. It is *squeezed* into shape, which forces the internal grain structure to align with the part contour (fibrous flow lines), giving forged parts superior directional strength compared to cast parts.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a child playing with *wet clay or playdough*. The child presses a small ball of clay between the two palms and rolls it out into a long roti or a snake. The clay never leaves the palms, and no clay is scraped off. What you are doing with your hands is exactly what a forging hammer does at industrial scale — the only difference is that industrial forging happens at temperatures of **900 °C to 1250 °C** for steel so that the metal deforms easily.

> [!TIP]
> **Civil Engineering Tie-in:** The eye-bolts used in tower cranes, anchor rods, hooks, chain links, and railway couplings are *drop-forged* for safety-critical loading.

---

### 1.2.3 Rolling

**Formal Definition (KTU):** Rolling is a primary *bulk deformation* process in which a metallic workpiece (ingot, bloom, billet, or slab) is passed between two opposing rotating cylindrical rolls, which reduce the cross-sectional area and produce a continuous product of uniform thickness. The compressive force is applied through friction between the rolls and the workpiece.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:** The cross-sectional area decreases while the length increases. Volume is conserved. Rolling is the **largest tonnage metal forming process in the world** — virtually all steel structural sections (I-sections, channels, angles, T-bars, plates, sheets, rails) are produced by rolling.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Picture a kitchen *chapati/roti maker* — you place a small thick ball of dough between two counter-rotating rollers and a thin, wide, longer chapati comes out the other side. That is rolling! At an industrial steel plant, the rolls are huge (sometimes 2 m diameter), powered by electric motors of thousands of horsepower, and the dough is a red-hot slab of steel.

> [!TIP]
> **Civil Engineering Tie-in:** Every TMT rebar, structural I-beam, channel, angle iron, and corrugated roofing sheet used in Kerala construction is a *rolled product*.

---

### 1.2.4 Extrusion

**Formal Definition (KTU):** Extrusion is a *continuous* bulk deformation process in which a solid cylindrical billet is forced (pushed or pulled) through a shaped die orifice, producing a product of constant cross-section whose profile is determined by the die geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:** Like squeezing toothpaste out of a tube. The tube is the container (billet), your hand applies force, and the opening (die) decides the cross-section of the paste that comes out.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a *cake-decorating icing gun* or a *soap dispenser*. The cylindrical cake of soap inside the gun is the *billet*, the plunger pushing it is the *ram*, and the shaped nozzle is the *die*. What emerges is a continuous soap bar of any complex cross-section (star, flower, square) — without cutting, without melting. The same process is used in industry to make aluminium window frames, copper tubes, and lead pipes for plumbing.

> [!TIP]
> **Civil Engineering Tie-in:** Aluminium window/door sections (sliding, casement), anodised hand-rails, electrical conduits, and lead/copper pipes in old buildings are all extrusion products.

---

## 1.3 Comparative Snapshot at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **At-a-Glance Comparison — KTU Quick Revision**

| Process | Material State During Process | Type of Force | Continuous/ Batch | Die/Mold Reuse | Typical Product |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sand Casting** | Liquid (Molten) | Gravity/Low pressure pouring | Batch (one mold = one part) | **No** (Sand mold is broken) | Manhole covers, pump bodies |
| **Forging** | Solid (Hot or Cold) | Compressive impact / pressing | Batch (one part per die strike) | **Yes** (Die lasts thousands of parts) | Crankshafts, bolts, hooks |
| **Rolling** | Solid (Hot or Cold) | Compressive (via friction) | **Continuous** (long lengths) | **Yes** (Rolls last years) | I-sections, plates, rebars |
| **Extrusion** | Solid (Hot or Cold) | Compressive (ram pressure) | **Continuous / Semi-continuous** | **Yes** (Die lasts long) | Window frames, pipes, tubes |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sand Casting — Theory & Operational Steps

Sand casting follows a strict sequence. KTU expects students to draw the *flow chart* and label each step in 2- or 3-mark questions.

### Operational Steps (Bulleted Logic)

1. **Pattern Making** — A wooden / metal / plastic replica of the final casting, slightly oversized to account for **shrinkage allowance** (≈ **1–2 %** for steel, **1.5 %** for cast iron, **1.3 %** for aluminium) and **machining allowance** (≈ 2–5 mm).
2. **Mold Preparation** — Silica sand (SiO₂) is mixed with a **binder** (green sand uses bentonite clay + water; dry sand uses resin-hardener). The mixture is rammed around the pattern inside a **flask** (two-part box: cope on top, drag on bottom).
3. **Pattern Removal** — The pattern is carefully withdrawn, leaving a clean cavity. To allow withdrawal, pattern sides have a **draft angle** (typically **1°–3°**).
4. **Core Placement** (if hollow cavity needed) — Sand cores (bonded and baked) are placed in the mold to form internal features.
5. **Melting & Pouring** — Metal is melted in a furnace (cupola for cast iron, induction for steel) and poured into the mold through a **sprue**, which leads to a **runner** and finally a **gate** that enters the cavity.
6. **Solidification & Cooling** — Metal freezes, beginning at the mold walls and progressing inward. Risers feed liquid metal to compensate for volumetric shrinkage.
7. **Shakeout** — The sand mold is broken; the casting is removed, and the **sprue, runner, riser** are cut off (fettled).
8. **Cleaning & Inspection** — Shot blasting removes adhering sand; visual and NDT (radiography, ultrasonic) checks ensure soundness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why sand?** Sand is cheap, refractory (can withstand molten metal temperature ≈ 1600 °C), and can be re-conditioned and reused → economical for large castings up to **200 tonnes** (single piece).

### Key Terminology in Sand Casting (KTU Frequently Asked)

- **Flask** — The box that holds the sand.
- **Cope** — Upper half of the flask.
- **Drag** — Lower half of the flask.
- **Parting Line** — The interface between cope and drag.
- **Sprue** — Vertical channel through which molten metal enters.
- **Runner** — Horizontal channel that carries metal from sprue to gate.
- **Gate** — Final entry point into the mold cavity.
- **Riser** — Reservoir of molten metal that feeds shrinkage during solidification.
- **Vent** — Thin hole to release gases/air.
- **Core** — Sand insert to create internal cavities.
- **Draft** — Slight taper (1°–3°) on pattern vertical walls for easy withdrawal.
- **Chaplets** — Small metal supports that hold cores in place inside the mold.

---

## 2.2 Forging — Theory & Operational Logic

Forging uses compressive plastic deformation. The fundamental logic is:

> Apply force **F** greater than the material's **flow stress (σ_f)** over the contact area **A** to permanently deform the workpiece.

### 2.2.1 Types of Forging (KTU Syllabus Level)

1. **Open-Die Forging** — Flat/simple dies; used for large shafts, rings, discs. (Civil application: large industrial shafts).
2. **Closed-Die (Impression-Die) Forging** — Die has the exact cavity shape; metal is squeezed into it. (Civil application: hooks, eye-bolts, connecting rods).
3. **Drop Forging** — A heavy hammer is raised and dropped onto the workpiece. High impact rate, suitable for batch production.
4. **Press Forging** — Slow, steady hydraulic press force. Used for large, thick parts where gravity hammer would crack the metal.
5. **Upset Forging** — Increasing the cross-section by compressing along the axis. (Civil application: bolt-head forming).
6. **Roll Forging** — Continuous forging between shaped rolls; used for taper leaf springs and axles.

### 2.2.2 Hot vs Cold Forging

| Parameter | Hot Forging | Cold Forging |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Temperature | Above recrystallization temp (≈ 0.6 T_m) | Room temperature |
| Flow stress | Low (easy deformation) | High (more force needed) |
| Surface finish | Poor (oxidation/scale) | Excellent (bright) |
| Dimensional accuracy | Low | Very high |
| Strength of part | Good (refined grain) | Higher (work hardening) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** **Hot forging = High temperature, High ductility, Low force, Poor finish**. **Cold forging = Room temperature, High force, High accuracy, Good finish**.

---

## 2.3 Rolling — Theory & Operational Logic

Rolling works on the **friction hill principle**. The rolls pull the workpiece in only if the friction angle **β** is greater than the contact angle **α**.

$$\beta > \alpha \quad \text{where} \quad \beta = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$$

and

$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}\!\left(1 - \frac{\Delta h}{R}\right)$$

Here, **μ** is the coefficient of friction, **Δh = h₀ - h_f** is the draft (reduction in thickness per pass), and **R** is the roll radius.

### 2.3.1 Types of Rolling Mills (KTU Important)

- **Two-High Reversing Mill** — Rolls rotate in both directions; slab reduced back-and-forth.
- **Two-High Continuous Mill** — Multiple stands in series; ingot enters one end, finished product exits the other.
- **Three-High Mill** — Three rolls; middle is fixed; top, middle, bottom are used alternately. Allows return pass without reversing rotation.
- **Four-High Mill** — Small work rolls backed by larger backup rolls → used for thin sheet/foil rolling.
- **Cluster Mill (Sendzimir)** — Multiple backup rolls → for very thin stainless steel foil.
- **Universal Mill** — Has horizontal + vertical rolls → produces I-beams with perfectly vertical flanges.

### 2.3.2 Defects in Rolling (Frequently Asked in ESE)

- **Piping** — Central axial shrinkage cavity in the ingot that fails to weld during rolling.
- **Crocodile / Alligatoring** — Longitudinal splitting due to improper reduction.
- **Wavy edges** — Insufficient friction or excessive roll flattening.
- **Flattening of rolls** — Elastic deflection → plate is thicker at centre than edges → solved by **cambered rolls** or **roll bending** systems.
- **Folding / Laps** — Surface overlap of metal from previous passes.

---

## 2.4 Extrusion — Theory & Operational Logic

Two main variants:

1. **Direct (Forward) Extrusion** — Billet is pushed *in the same direction* as the ram moves. Friction at container walls wastes ≈ 30–40 % of the ram force.
2. **Indirect (Backward) Extrusion** — Die is mounted on a hollow ram. The product flows *opposite* to the ram motion. No container-wall friction → requires less force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Most aluminium window sections, copper tubes, PVC pipes, and lead sheets used in construction are made by **direct hot extrusion** of billets heated to **350–500 °C** (for aluminium) or cold extrusion for steels.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory formulas for KTU ESE 2024 — print and revise daily**

| # | Process | Formula | Variables | Units | KTU Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Sand Casting — Shrinkage | $L_{p} = L_{c}(1 + S)$ | $L_{p}$ = pattern length, $L_{c}$ = casting length, $S$ = shrinkage fraction (e.g. 0.015) | mm | Sizing the pattern |
| 2 | Sand Casting — Chvorinov's Rule | $t_{s} = B \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{n}$ | $t_{s}$ = solidification time, $V$ = volume, $A$ = surface area, $B$ = mold constant, $n \approx 2$ | s | Sizing the riser |
| 3 | Forging — Load | $F = K_{f} \cdot \sigma_{f} \cdot A$ | $K_{f}$ = forging shape factor (1 to >3), $\sigma_{f}$ = flow stress, $A$ = projected area | N | Hammers/Presses |
| 4 | Rolling — Contact Angle | $\alpha = \cos^{-1}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{R}\right)$ | $\alpha$ = bite angle, $\Delta h$ = draft, $R$ = roll radius | rad | Maximum draft per pass |
| 5 | Rolling — Max Draft (no-slip) | $\Delta h_{max} = \mu^{2} R$ | $\mu$ = coefficient of friction | mm | Theoretical limit |
| 6 | Rolling — True Strain | $\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{h_{0}}{h_{f}}\right)$ | $h_{0}$ = initial thickness, $h_{f}$ = final thickness | dimensionless | Stating deformation |
| 7 | Rolling — Avg Roll Pressure (simplified) | $\bar{p} = Y_{f} \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu L}{2\bar{h}}\right)$ | $Y_{f}$ = yield stress, $L$ = projected contact length, $\bar{h}$ = avg thickness | Pa | Rolling load |
| 8 | Rolling — Total Force | $F = \bar{p} \cdot L \cdot w$ | $w$ = width of strip | N | Power of motor |
| 9 | Extrusion — Direct Load | $F = A_{0} \cdot k \cdot \ln(R) \cdot (1 + \phi)$ | $A_{0}$ = billet area, $k$ = yield stress, $R = A_{0}/A_{f}$ = extrusion ratio, $\phi$ = friction factor (0.05–0.30) | N | Ram force selection |
| 10 | Extrusion — Pure Ideal Load | $F_{ideal} = A_{0} \cdot k \cdot \ln(R)$ | Same as above, no friction | N | Comparison baseline |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Formulas (for last-minute KTU revision):** **"C-F-R-E" → Casting–Forging–Rolling–Extrusion → "Sh-Ch-Fo-Bi-Ma-Pr-Lo-Id"**

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Map

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters to a Civil Engineer:** Every reinforced-cement-concrete (RCC) building you see in Kerala contains:
> - **Sand cast iron** — Manhole covers, drainage gratings.
> - **Forged steel** — Reinforcement couplers, anchor bolts, crane hooks.
> - **Hot-rolled steel** — TMT rebars (Fe-500, Fe-550), I-sections, channels, angles, plates, roofing sheets.
> - **Extruded aluminium / PVC** — Window frames, plumbing pipes, conduits, façade profiles.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Mandate:** Every algebraic step is shown. No skipping. Follow the conversions and logic in the text rows. This is the most KTU-lucrative section for 14-mark derivation problems.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Possible Draft in Rolling (No-Slip Condition)

### Problem Setup
A solid cylindrical roll of radius **R** is pressing on a workpiece of initial thickness **h₀**. The roll surface just touches the entry thickness at point A. As the roll rotates, the workpiece passes through and emerges with thickness **h_f** at point B. The arc AB subtends an angle **2α** at the roll centre, called the **contact (or bite) angle**.

Let the centre of the roll be **O**. Drop a perpendicular from **O** to the line of the strip. The vertical distance from the strip surface to the top of the roll is **R cos α**, and the vertical distance from the strip top to the centre is **R**. So the original thickness **h₀** equals **R + R cos α**. The final thickness **h_f** equals **R − R cos α**.

### Algebraic Derivation

**Step 1 — Express h₀ and h_f in terms of R and α.**

$$h_{0} = R + R\cos\alpha = R(1 + \cos\alpha)$$

$$h_{f} = R - R\cos\alpha = R(1 - \cos\alpha)$$

**Step 2 — Subtract to find the draft Δh = h₀ − h_f.**

$$\Delta h = h_{0} - h_{f} = R(1 + \cos\alpha) - R(1 - \cos\alpha)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the bracket.**

$$\Delta h = R + R\cos\alpha - R + R\cos\alpha$$

**Step 4 — Combine like terms.**

$$\Delta h = 2R\cos\alpha$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to solve for cos α in terms of Δh and R.**

$$\cos\alpha = \frac{\Delta h}{2R}$$

**Step 6 — Take inverse cosine to obtain the bite angle α.**

$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta h}{2R}\right) \quad \text{— (this is the contact angle)}$$

*Note: Some textbooks write the term as $(1 - \frac{\Delta h}{R})$ because they measure α from the vertical (line of centres). Both forms are equivalent by a simple trig identity.*

**Step 7 — Apply the no-slip (friction) condition.**
For the roll to *pull* the workpiece in (without slip), the **friction angle β = tan⁻¹(μ)** must be greater than the contact angle **α**. Setting the limiting case **α = β**:

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$$

**Step 8 — Combine with Step 6.**

$$\cos\!\left(\tan^{-1}\mu\right) = \frac{\Delta h_{max}}{2R}$$

**Step 9 — Use the trigonometric identity cos(tan⁻¹ x) = 1 / √(1 + x²).**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \mu^{2}}} = \frac{\Delta h_{max}}{2R}$$

**Step 10 — Solve for Δh_max.**

$$\Delta h_{max} = \frac{2R}{\sqrt{1 + \mu^{2}}}$$

**Step 11 — Apply the small-μ approximation (μ < 0.3 typical in hot rolling).** For small μ, √(1 + μ²) ≈ 1 + μ²/2 ≈ 1, hence the classic KTU formula:

$$\boxed{\Delta h_{max} \approx \mu^{2} R}$$

This is the formula that appears in KTU numerical questions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Forging Load for an Upsetting Cylinder

### Problem Setup
A solid cylindrical billet of initial height **h₀** and diameter **d₀** is compressed axially in a forging press. The instantaneous height is **h** and the instantaneous diameter is **d**. The material has flow stress **σ_f** (which may increase as strain hardens). The forging load is **F**.

### Algebraic Derivation

**Step 1 — Volume constancy assumption (incompressibility of plastic deformation).**

$$V = \text{constant} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\pi}{4}d_{0}^{2} h_{0} = \frac{\pi}{4}d^{2} h$$

**Step 2 — Cancel π/4 on both sides.**

$$d_{0}^{2} h_{0} = d^{2} h$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the instantaneous diameter d in terms of h.**

$$d^{2} = \frac{d_{0}^{2} h_{0}}{h} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = d_{0} \sqrt{\frac{h_{0}}{h}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the instantaneous cross-sectional area A.**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{d_{0}^{2} h_{0}}{h}$$

**Step 5 — Forging force is the flow stress times the projected area, multiplied by a shape factor K_f (to account for friction at die-workpiece interface, barrel formation, and geometry).**

$$F = K_{f} \cdot \sigma_{f} \cdot A$$

**Step 6 — Substitute A.**

$$\boxed{F = K_{f} \cdot \sigma_{f} \cdot \frac{\pi d_{0}^{2} h_{0}}{4 h}}$$

**Step 7 — Special case: Cold forging with no friction (K_f = 1) and constant σ_f.**

For a strain-hardening material, the **true strain ε** is:

$$\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{h_{0}}{h}\right)$$

The average flow stress is the area under the σ-ε curve:

$$\bar{\sigma}_{f} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon} \int_{0}^{\varepsilon} \sigma_{f}(\varepsilon')\,d\varepsilon'$$

For a linear strain-hardening material, $\sigma_f = K \varepsilon^{n}$, which gives:

$$\bar{\sigma}_{f} = \frac{K \varepsilon^{n}}{n+1}$$

And the final compact form:

$$\boxed{F = K_{f} \cdot \frac{\pi d_{0}^{2} h_{0}}{4 h} \cdot \frac{K \varepsilon^{n}}{n+1}}$$

This is the KTU final-answer form for forging problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Ideal Extrusion Force (Direct Extrusion, No Friction)

### Problem Setup
A cylindrical billet of initial cross-section **A₀** is forced through a die to a final cross-section **A_f**. The extrusion ratio is defined as $R = A_0 / A_f$. The material is rigid-perfectly plastic with constant yield strength **k = σ_y** (no work hardening for ideal case).

### Algebraic Derivation

**Step 1 — True strain in extrusion is the log of the area ratio.**

$$\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{A_{0}}{A_{f}}\right) = \ln(R)$$

**Step 2 — For a perfectly plastic material, the homogeneous work per unit volume (work of deformation) is:**

$$w = \int_{0}^{\varepsilon} \sigma_{y}\,d\varepsilon' = k \cdot \varepsilon = k \cdot \ln(R)$$

**Step 3 — Total work = w × initial volume of billet being deformed. For simplicity, take a unit length of billet, so volume = A₀ × 1 = A₀:**

$$W = k \cdot A_{0} \cdot \ln(R)$$

**Step 4 — Assuming 100 % ideal work conversion (no friction, no redundant work), the extrusion force times the ram stroke equals the work:**

$$F_{ideal} \cdot s = W$$

**Step 5 — For a unit ram stroke, s = 1:**

$$\boxed{F_{ideal} = A_{0} \cdot k \cdot \ln(R)}$$

**Step 6 — Real extrusion (with friction and redundant work) is modified by an efficiency factor η (typically 0.5 to 0.8) or equivalently by an empirical factor (1 + φ):**

$$\boxed{F_{real} = A_{0} \cdot k \cdot \ln(R) \cdot (1 + \phi)}$$

where φ is the combined friction + redundant-work factor (≈ 0.3 to 1.0 for hot extrusion).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Numerical Setup

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix as a template for any 7-mark numerical that appears in KTU ESE Module-2.**

| Given Quantity | What to Find | Governing Formula | Substitution Strategy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Initial / final thickness, roll radius | Max bite angle, max draft | $\alpha = \cos^{-1}(1 - \Delta h/R)$ | Substitute numerical values carefully |
| Coefficient of friction, roll radius | Max possible draft | $\Delta h_{max} = \mu^{2} R$ | Direct substitution |
| Initial & final dimensions | True strain | $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$ | Calculator required; convert to decimal |
| Forging shape factor, area, flow stress | Forging load | $F = K_f \sigma_f A$ | Identify K_f from chart for the part shape |
| Billet area, die area, yield strength | Extrusion force | $F = A_0 k \ln(R) (1+\phi)$ | Compute R first, then ln(R) |
| Casting length, shrinkage fraction | Pattern size | $L_p = L_c(1+S)$ | Linear dimensions, not area |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Sample)

> **Question:** A 200 mm diameter cylindrical billet of aluminium (yield strength = 90 MPa) is to be extruded to a square section of side 30 mm. Assuming an ideal direct extrusion, calculate the extrusion force. Take $A_0 = \pi d^2/4$.

**Step 1 — Calculate billet area A₀.**

$$A_{0} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.200)^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.04 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate final area A_f (square of 30 mm).**

$$A_{f} = (0.030)^{2} = 0.0009 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Extrusion ratio R.**

$$R = \frac{A_{0}}{A_{f}} = \frac{0.03142}{0.0009} = 34.91$$

**Step 4 — Compute ln(R).**

$$\ln(34.91) = 3.552$$

**Step 5 — Ideal extrusion force (k = 90 MPa = 90 × 10⁶ N/m²).**

$$F_{ideal} = A_{0} \cdot k \cdot \ln(R) = 0.03142 \times 90 \times 10^{6} \times 3.552$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate.**

$$F_{ideal} = 0.03142 \times 90 \times 10^{6} \times 3.552 = 1.004 \times 10^{7} \text{ N} = 10.04 \text{ MN}$$

**Final Answer:** $F_{ideal} \approx 10.04$ MN (≈ 1000 tonnes-force).

If a friction factor φ = 0.4 is specified, then:
$$F_{real} = 1.004 \times 10^{7} \times 1.4 = 1.406 \times 10^{7} \text{ N} = 14.06 \text{ MN}$$

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answers Provided. KTU expects 3-4 sentences plus a labelled sketch wherever possible.**

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "draft" in sand casting. Why is it provided on the pattern? State the typical values used in practice.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Draft is the **slight taper** provided on the vertical (or inclined) surfaces of a pattern so that it can be withdrawn from the sand mold **without damaging the mold cavity walls**.

- **Why provided:** Without draft, friction between pattern and rammed sand would tear away sand grains, ruining the cavity surface, leading to defective castings.
- **Typical values:** **1° to 3°** for wooden patterns, and **0.5° to 1°** for metal patterns.
- A labelled sketch (2 marks) showing pattern taper, parting line, and mold wall earns full credit.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse "draft" with "shrinkage allowance." Draft is for *pattern withdrawal*; shrinkage is for *pattern sizing*. Do not interchange them.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Compare hot working and cold working of metals. State any two advantages of hot working.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Hot Working | Cold Working |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Temperature | Above recrystallization temperature | Below recrystallization temperature |
| Flow stress | Low (easy deformation, less force) | High |
| Surface finish | Poor (oxide scale) | Excellent (bright surface) |
| Dimensional accuracy | Lower | Very high |
| Mechanical strength | Improved (refined grain) | Higher (work hardening) |
| Examples | Hot rolling, hot forging, hot extrusion | Cold rolling, cold forging, wire drawing |

**Two advantages of hot working:**
1. **Lower deformation force** — because the metal is soft at high temperature, smaller/cheaper equipment suffices.
2. **Refined grain structure** — recrystallization breaks the as-cast coarse grains, giving better toughness and ductility.
3. *(Bonus third)* Defects like porosity, pipes, and cracks in the ingot get welded up during hot working, improving soundness.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write "hot working = high accuracy" — this is **wrong**. Hot working has *lower* accuracy because of scale, uneven cooling, and shrinkage. Cold working gives higher accuracy.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (1 × 14 Marks = 14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Choice Provided. KTU allows you to attempt EITHER Question A OR Question B. Both are full 14-mark questions with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.**

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled sketch, describe the **sand casting process** step-by-step. State the function of (i) sprue, (ii) riser, and (iii) vent. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cast iron casting of nominal length **600 mm** is required. The shrinkage fraction for cast iron is **1.5 %**. Calculate the **pattern length**. If the same pattern is used to cast **steel** (shrinkage = 2 %), what would be the resulting casting length? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q A (a)

A clean labelled diagram of a sand mold in section showing **flask (cope + drag), sprue, runner, gate, riser, vent, pattern cavity, parting line** is mandatory. (3 marks for diagram + labels)

**Step-by-step process description (3 marks):**

1. **Pattern making** — A slightly oversized wooden/metal pattern (oversize accounts for shrinkage and machining allowance) is made.
2. **Mold preparation** — Sand mixed with binder (clay or resin) is rammed around the pattern inside a two-part flask. The top half is called the **cope**, the bottom is the **drag**.
3. **Pattern withdrawal** — Pattern is carefully lifted; a draft angle of 1°–3° on the pattern prevents mold wall damage.
4. **Core placement** (if hollow) — Sand cores (baked and bonded) are placed in the cavity to form internal features.
5. **Pouring** — Molten metal is poured into the **sprue**, which channels it to the runner, then through the gate into the cavity.
6. **Solidification** — Metal freezes from mold walls inward. The **riser** supplies fresh liquid metal to compensate for shrinkage.
7. **Cooling & shakeout** — Mold is broken and casting is removed.
8. **Fettling & inspection** — Sprue/runner/riser are cut off; surface is cleaned; visual and NDT checks are done.

**Functions (1 mark each):**
- **Sprue** — Vertical passage that conducts molten metal from the pouring basin to the runner/gate.
- **Riser** — Acts as a reservoir of molten metal to feed the casting as it shrinks during solidification, preventing shrinkage cavities.
- **Vent** — Thin passage that allows trapped air and gases to escape, preventing blowholes and incomplete filling.

---

#### Model Solution — Q A (b)

**Given:**
- Nominal casting length $L_c = 600$ mm
- Shrinkage fraction for cast iron, $S_{CI} = 1.5\% = 0.015$
- Shrinkage fraction for steel, $S_{st} = 2\% = 0.02$

**Step 1 — Calculate pattern length for cast iron.**

$$L_{p,\,CI} = L_{c}\,(1 + S_{CI}) = 600 \times (1 + 0.015)$$

$$L_{p,\,CI} = 600 \times 1.015 = 609 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate casting length if the same 609 mm pattern is used for steel.**

$$L_{c,\,steel} = \frac{L_{p}}{1 + S_{st}} = \frac{609}{1 + 0.02} = \frac{609}{1.02}$$

$$L_{c,\,steel} = 597.06 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compare.**

The same 609 mm pattern produces a steel casting of only ~597 mm, which is **3 mm shorter** than the cast iron casting of 600 mm — illustrating that patterns are *material-specific*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Most Common Mistake in this Question:**
> 1. Students write $L_p = L_c \times S$ instead of $L_p = L_c (1 + S)$. **This loses 1–2 marks.**
> 2. Students forget to convert percentage to decimal (1.5% → 0.015, not 1.5).
> 3. Students do not state units in the final answer.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **rolling process** with a neat sketch. Derive the relationship between **maximum possible draft, coefficient of friction, and roll radius**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 200 mm thick aluminium slab is hot-rolled using rolls of radius **400 mm**. The coefficient of friction between roll and slab is **0.30**. Calculate (i) the **contact angle**, (ii) the **maximum possible draft**, and (iii) the **true strain** if the slab is reduced to 160 mm in the first pass. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q B (a)

**Sketch (3 marks):** Show two rolls rotating in opposite directions, incoming slab of thickness h₀, outgoing strip of thickness h_f, contact angle α, roll radius R, and direction of friction force. Label all forces.

**Explanation (1 mark):** Rolling is a bulk deformation process where the workpiece is passed between two counter-rotating rolls. The rolls compress the workpiece, reducing its cross-section while increasing its length. Friction between the rolls and the workpiece draws the material into the roll gap.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Starting from the geometry (refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for full derivation):

$$h_{0} = R(1 + \cos\alpha), \qquad h_{f} = R(1 - \cos\alpha)$$

$$\Delta h = h_{0} - h_{f} = 2R\cos\alpha$$

$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta h}{2R}\right) = \cos^{-1}\!\left(1 - \frac{\Delta h}{R}\right)$$

Applying the no-slip condition $\alpha = \beta = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$, and using $\cos(\tan^{-1}\mu) = 1/\sqrt{1+\mu^2}$:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\mu^{2}}} = \frac{\Delta h_{max}}{2R}$$

For small μ, $\sqrt{1+\mu^2} \approx 1$, giving:

$$\boxed{\Delta h_{max} \approx \mu^{2}\,R}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Q B (b)

**Given:**
- $h_0 = 200$ mm (slab initial thickness)
- $h_f = 160$ mm (slab final thickness)
- $R = 400$ mm (roll radius)
- $\mu = 0.30$ (coefficient of friction)

**Part (i) — Contact angle α.**

**Step 1 — Compute draft Δh.**

$$\Delta h = h_0 - h_f = 200 - 160 = 40 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the contact angle formula.**

$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}\!\left(1 - \frac{\Delta h}{R}\right) = \cos^{-1}\!\left(1 - \frac{40}{400}\right) = \cos^{-1}(1 - 0.10) = \cos^{-1}(0.90)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the inverse cosine.**

$$\alpha = 25.84° = 0.4510 \text{ rad}$$

**Part (ii) — Maximum possible draft.**

$$\Delta h_{max} = \mu^{2} R = (0.30)^{2} \times 400 = 0.09 \times 400 = 36 \text{ mm}$$

**Observation:** The actual draft of 40 mm is **greater than** the maximum allowed (36 mm) — meaning the roll **cannot grip** the slab in this configuration. The student must conclude that the parameters need to be revised (e.g., increase roll radius or μ, or take two smaller passes).

**Part (iii) — True strain.**

$$\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{200}{160}\right) = \ln(1.25) = 0.2231$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Q B(b):**
> 1. The **inverse cosine must be evaluated in degrees or radians consistently**. Many students leave it as $\cos^{-1}(0.9)$ and don't compute a numerical value → loses 1 mark.
> 2. **The "observation" in part (ii) is what earns full marks** — the question implicitly tests whether the student understands that $\Delta h_{actual} \le \Delta h_{max}$ is a *physical constraint*. Just plugging into the formula is worth only 2 of 3 marks; the engineering comment is worth the remaining 1 mark.
> 3. **Units** — Always state angles in degrees (or rad) and length in mm.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist. Revise the night before ESE 2024.**

### A. Core Concepts
- [x] **Sand casting** = liquid-to-solid + disposable mold + sand + binder (clay or resin).
- [x] **Forging** = solid-state plastic deformation + compressive force + no melting + grain flow improvement.
- [x] **Rolling** = solid-state + two rotating rolls + friction-driven bite + continuous long product.
- [x] **Extrusion** = solid-state + billet pushed/pulled through die + continuous constant cross-section.

### B. Numerical Readiness
- [x] Pattern length: $L_p = L_c (1 + S)$.
- [x] Rolling contact angle: $\alpha = \cos^{-1}\!\left(1 - \frac{\Delta h}{R}\right)$.
- [x] Maximum draft: $\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R$.
- [x] Forging load: $F = K_f \sigma_f A$.
- [x] Extrusion force: $F = A_0 k \ln(R) (1 + \phi)$.
- [x] True strain: $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f) = \ln(A_0/A_f)$.

### C. Definitions to Memorize (3-mark favorites)
- [x] **Draft** (pattern taper for withdrawal).
- [x] **Shrinkage allowance** (pattern oversize to compensate metal contraction).
- [x] **Riser** (feeds liquid metal during solidification shrinkage).
- [x] **Sprue / Runner / Gate** (channels for molten metal entry).
- [x] **Cope & Drag** (upper and lower halves of the flask).
- [x] **Flow stress** (instantaneous stress required to continue plastic deformation).
- [x] **Neutral point / no-slip line** (point in rolling where roll surface and strip move at the same velocity).
- [x] **Extrusion ratio** ($R = A_0 / A_f$).
- [x] **Draft in rolling** (reduction per pass: $\Delta h = h_0 - h_f$).
- [x] **Camber** (slightly larger diameter at roll centre to compensate for elastic roll flattening).

### D. Comparison Hot-Spots
- [x] **Hot working vs Cold working** — 6 differences.
- [x] **Forging vs Casting** — strength, grain flow, wastage, cost.
- [x] **Direct vs Indirect extrusion** — friction, force, complexity.
- [x] **Two-high vs Three-high vs Four-high rolling mill** — geometry, application.
- [x] **Green sand vs Dry sand vs Shell mold** — binder, surface finish, cost.

### E. Civil Engineering Applications (Examiner loves these)
- [x] Sand casting → manhole covers, drainage gratings, large valve bodies.
- [x] Forging → crane hooks, anchor bolts, chain links, eye-bolts.
- [x] Rolling → TMT rebars, I-beams, channels, angles, plates, rails, roofing sheets.
- [x] Extrusion → aluminium window/door profiles, PVC pipes, electrical conduits, handrails.

### F. Last-Day Watch-Outs
- [x] **Percentage to decimal** — never substitute 1.5 for 0.015.
- [x] **Units** — mm, m, N, MN, Pa, MPa — declare them.
- [x] **Sketch before solving** — even a rough one in 14-mark questions earns 2–3 marks.
- [x] **Read the question fully** — "compare" needs a table; "explain" needs prose + sketch; "derive" needs step-by-step math.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metal Joining Processes: Types of Welding, Arc Welding (SMAW), Soldering and Brazing and Their Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GCEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO MECHANICAL ENGINEERING & CIVIL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Metal Joining Processes: Types of Welding, Arc Welding (SMAW), Soldering and Brazing and Their Applications # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Introduction to Metal Joining Processes Metal joining is a fundamental manufacturing process used to permanently bond two or more metal components together to form a single, unified assembly. These processes are the backbone of modern fabrication industries, ranging from the construction of skyscrapers and bridges to the assembly of microelectronic circuits. > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** > A *Metal Joining Process* is defined as a manufacturing operation that involves the coalescence (permanent fusion) of two or more metal parts, achieved through the application of heat, pressure, or both, often supplemented with a filler material, to produce a strong metallurgical bond between the workpieces. Metal joining is broadly classified into two categories: - **Welding** (cohesion at the atomic level, with or without melting) - **Brazing and Soldering** (adhesion via a melted filler metal that wets the base metal surfaces without melting them) ## 1.2 Concept of Welding > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition of Welding:** > Welding is a fabrication process whereby two or more parts are fused together by means of heat, pressure, or both, to form a permanent joint with strength equivalent to or greater than that of the base metal. The base metals themselves are melted at the joint interface, often along with a filler material, to create a metallurgical bond upon solidification. **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine two ice cubes pressed against each other. If you apply slight heat to melt a thin film on their surfaces, they fuse into a single block as they re-freeze. Welding works on the same principle, but with metals and at much higher temperatures (often above **1500 °C**). ## 1.3 Concept of Soldering and Brazing **Soldering** and **Brazing** are similar processes in that they use a **filler metal** with a melting point **lower** than the base metal. The base metals are *not* melted — only the filler is melted, which flows into the joint by capillary action. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction (KTU Board Standard):** > The internationally accepted demarcation line between Soldering and Brazing is **450 °C** (sometimes cited as 840 °F). > - **Soldering:** Filler metal melts **below 450 °C** (soft soldering, typically 90 °C–450 °C). > - **Brazing:** Filler metal melts **above 450 °C** but **below the melting point of the base metal** (typically 450 °C–1200 °C). **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of soldering/brazing like gluing two pieces of paper together, where the glue is molten metal instead of adhesive. The paper (base metal) is not destroyed; only the glue (filler) melts and hardens to hold them together. The only difference between soldering and brazing is the *temperature* of the "glue" used. ## 1.4 Classification of Welding Processes Welding is broadly classified into **Fusion Welding** and **Solid-State Welding**. | Category | Sub-Category | Description | |----------|--------------|-------------| | **Fusion Welding** | Arc Welding | Uses an electric arc as the heat source (e.g., SMAW, GTAW, GMAW) | | | Gas Welding | Uses a combustible gas flame (e.g., Oxy-acetylene welding) | | | Resistance Welding | Uses resistance to electric current (e.g., Spot, Seam, Projection) | | | Thermit Welding | Uses thermit reaction for heat (e.g., rail joint welding) | | **Solid-State Welding** | Forge Welding | Uses heat + pressure (e.g., hammer welding) | | | Friction Welding | Uses frictional heat (e.g., Friction Stir Welding) | | | Ultrasonic Welding | Uses ultrasonic vibrations under pressure | | **Soldering/Brazing** | Soft / Hard Joining | Uses filler metal below the base metal melting point | > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Arc welding — specifically **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** — is the most extensively tested topic under this module. Master its working, equipment, and electrode classification. ## 1.5 Standard Units and Metrics The following physical quantities are central to this topic and must be memorised: - **Arc Temperature:** **6000 °C – 7000 °C** (in the core of the arc column) - **Welding Pool Temperature:** approximately **2500 °C** (for steel) - **Soldering Temperature Range:** **< 450 °C** - **Brazing Temperature Range:** **450 °C – 1200 °C** - **Heat Input Unit:** **kJ/mm** (kilojoules per millimetre) or **J/mm** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) — The Detailed Working Principle Shielded Metal Arc Welding is the most widely used manual arc welding process. It is also popularly known as **"Stick Welding"** or **Manual Metal Arc Welding (MMAW)**. ### 2.1.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Power Source Activation:** An AC or DC welding power source (typically 20–40 V open-circuit voltage) is connected to the workpiece (via a ground clamp) and to an **electrode holder**. 2. **Electrode Selection:** A consumable metal rod — the *electrode* — is clamped into the electrode holder. The electrode consists of a **core wire** (filler metal) coated with a **flux coating**. 3. **Arc Initiation (Scratch/Tap Strike):** The welder drags or taps the electrode tip against the workpiece. The low-resistance contact generates high current density, instantly vaporising a small portion of metal and ionizing the air gap. This ionised gas becomes electrically conductive, and an **arc** is struck. 4. **Arc Sustenance:** The arc melts both the **electrode tip** and the **base metal**, forming a molten pool called the **weld pool** or **weld puddle**. 5. **Filler Metal Deposition:** Droplets of molten electrode metal transfer across the arc and are deposited into the weld pool. This is the *filler material* of the joint. 6. **Flux Decomposition:** The heat of the arc decomposes the flux coating, releasing a **shielding gas** (typically CO₂, H₂) and forming a **molten slag** layer on top of the weld pool. 7. **Shielding Function:** The shielding gas displaces atmospheric nitrogen and oxygen, preventing **porosity**, **oxidation**, and **embrittlement** of the weld. 8. **Slag Solidification:** The molten slag floats on the weld pool, dissolves impurities, and solidifies on top of the weld bead, protecting it from rapid cooling and atmospheric contamination. 9. **Solidification:** As the arc moves along, the weld pool cools and solidifies into a **weld bead** (the visible rippled line of the joint). 10. **Slag Removal:** After cooling, the brittle solidified slag is chipped off with a wire brush or hammer, revealing the finished weld. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is the flux coating critical?** > Without flux: Atmospheric O₂ forms iron oxide inclusions, N₂ causes porosity and brittleness. Flux acts as a *triple shield* — gas shield + slag shield + deoxidiser. ### 2.1.2 SMAW Equipment | Component | Function | |-----------|----------| | **Welding Power Source** | Provides controlled V/I (Constant Current — CC type preferred) | | **Electrode Holder** | Holds the consumable electrode; conducts current | | **Ground Clamp** | Completes the electrical circuit via the workpiece | | **Welding Cables** | Heavy-duty copper cables (cable size per current rating) | | **Electrode (Filler Rod)** | Consumable rod — core wire + flux coating | | **Chipping Hammer & Wire Brush** | Slag removal tools | | **Safety Helmet (with Auto-Darkening Filter)** | Eye protection from UV/IR radiation | ### 2.1.3 AWS Electrode Classification (E-XXYZ) Every SMAW electrode has a standardised classification. For example, **E 6013** or **E 7018**. | Digit Position | Meaning | |----------------|---------| | **E** | Designates an **E**lectrode for arc welding | | **First 2 digits (XX)** | Minimum **Tensile Strength** of deposited weld metal, in **ksi** (kilo-pounds per square inch). E60 → 60,000 psi, E70 → 70,000 psi | | **Third digit (Y)** | **Welding position** capability:
1 = All positions (flat, horizontal, vertical, overhead)
2 = Flat & horizontal only | | **Fourth digit (Z)** | **Coating type, current type, and penetration characteristics**
0 = Cellulosic (DC, deep penetration)
1 = Cellulosic (AC/DC)
3 = Rutile (AC/DC, medium penetration)
8 = Low-hydrogen (AC/DC, mild penetration, X-ray quality) | > [!EXAMPLE] > **E 7018:** Electrode, 70,000 psi tensile strength, all positions, low-hydrogen iron powder coating. Used for critical welds like pressure vessels, bridges, and ship hulls. ## 2.2 Theory of Soldering ### 2.2.1 Working Principle 1. The base metal surfaces are cleaned (mechanically or chemically) to remove oxides, grease, and dirt. 2. A **flux** (rosin, zinc chloride, organic acid) is applied to the joint to dissolve residual oxides and prevent re-oxidation during heating. 3. Heat is supplied by a **soldering iron**, **soldering torch**, or **hot plate** to a temperature below **450 °C**. 4. The **solder** (filler metal — typically a tin-lead alloy, e.g., 60/40 Sn-Pb with m.p. **190 °C**) is melted and drawn into the joint by **capillary action**. 5. Upon cooling, the solder solidifies and forms a strong mechanical and electrical bond. ### 2.2.2 Common Solders - **Tin-Lead (Sn-Pb):** Traditional solder; eutectic at 63/37 ratio, melts at **183 °C**. - **Lead-Free (Sn-Ag-Cu):** Modern RoHS-compliant solder, melts around **217 °C**. - **Cadmium-Silver, Bismuth-Tin:** Specialty solders. ## 2.3 Theory of Brazing ### 2.3.1 Working Principle 1. Joint surfaces are cleaned thoroughly. 2. The parts are assembled with a small **clearance gap** (typically **0.03 mm – 0.13 mm**) to allow capillary flow. 3. A **flux** (borax-based for high-temperature brazing) is applied to prevent oxidation. 4. Heat is supplied via **torch, furnace, induction coil, or dip bath** to a temperature above **450 °C** but below the base metal's melting point. 5. The **filler metal** (brass, bronze, copper-phosphorus, aluminum-silicon) melts and flows into the joint via **capillary action**. 6. Upon cooling, the filler forms a strong metallurgical bond. ### 2.3.2 Common Brazing Filler Metals | Filler | Melting Point | Application | |--------|---------------|-------------| | **Copper (Cu)** | 1083 °C | Steel pipe joining, carbide tools | | **Brass (Cu-Zn)** | 870–900 °C | General-purpose brazing | | **Bronze (Cu-Sn)** | 800–950 °C | Sheet metal, plumbing | | **Cu-P (Copper-Phosphorus)** | 710–800 °C | Copper-to-copper refrigeration joints (self-fluxing) | | **Al-Si (Aluminum-Silicon)** | 580–620 °C | Aluminum components | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula | Description | Units | |---------|-------------|-------| | $HI = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S}$ | **Heat Input** = Voltage × Current × 60 / Travel Speed | kJ/mm | | $P = V \times I$ | **Arc Power** | Watts (W) | | $E_{weld} = V \times I \times t$ | **Total Heat Energy** supplied during welding | Joules (J) | | $E_{arc} = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \times L$ | Approx. electrode volume consumed (where d = core dia, L = length consumed) | mm³ | | $F_{d} = 0.01 \times A \times 60$ | **Deposition Rate** ≈ 0.01 × Amps × 60 | kg/h (approx) | | $\text{Voltage Range (SMAW)}$ | $V = 20 + 0.04 \times I$ | Empirical, V | > [!TIP] > **Note for KTU:** The Heat Input formula is the **single most important equation** in welding metallurgy. High heat input → coarse grains → reduced strength. Low heat input → fast cooling → risk of cracking. > **CRITICAL FORMATTING CHECK FOR ABSOLUTE VALUES:** > All absolute values (e.g., $V \times I$ magnitudes) are to be enclosed with proper LaTeX delimiters like $\vert V \cdot I \vert$ if needed, but never using bare vertical pipes inside the markdown tables. All necessary unit symbols use standard SI notation, no Markdown pipes are used as separators within formulas. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Construction & Infrastructure:** SMAW is the dominant process for site welding of structural steel (I-beams, girders, rebar cages). - **Shipbuilding:** SMAW with low-hydrogen electrodes (E7018) is used for hull plates where X-ray-quality welds are mandatory. - **Electronics Manufacturing:** Soldering is the universal process for PCB component assembly and wire termination. - **HVAC and Refrigeration:** Brazing with Cu-P filler is the industry standard for joining copper refrigerant lines. - **Tool Manufacturing:** Brazing is used to attach carbide cutting tips to steel tool shanks. - **Jewellery and Cutlery:** Soldering and brazing are used for decorative and antique restoration work. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Heat Input in Arc Welding The **Heat Input (HI)** is defined as the total energy delivered by the welding arc per unit length of weld. It is a critical parameter because it directly controls the **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)**, **grain structure**, **distortion**, and **mechanical properties** of the joint. ### 3.1.1 Conceptual Foundation Electrical power delivered by the arc is given by the standard DC circuit relation: $$P = V \times I$$ Where: - $P$ = Electrical power (Watts = J/s) - $V$ = Arc voltage (Volts) - $I$ = Welding current (Amperes) The **total energy** delivered to the workpiece over a time interval $t$ (seconds) is: $$E = P \times t = V \times I \times t$$ The **travel speed** $S$ is the linear speed at which the electrode moves along the joint, measured in **mm/min**: $$S = \dfrac{L}{t} \implies t = \dfrac{L}{S} \times 60$$ > *Note: The factor of 60 is required to convert minutes into seconds so that the time unit aligns with the J/s definition of Watts.* Substituting the time $t$ in the energy equation, the total energy per unit length of weld (in **J/mm**) is: $$HI = \dfrac{E}{L} = \dfrac{V \times I \times t}{L}$$ Replacing $t = \dfrac{L}{S} \times 60$: $$HI = \dfrac{V \times I \times \dfrac{L}{S} \times 60}{L}$$ Cancel $L$ from numerator and denominator: $$HI = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S}$$ The result is in **J/mm**. To convert to **kJ/mm**, divide by 1000: $$HI \text{ (kJ/mm)} = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Standard Formula (for KTU Board Use):** > $$\boxed{HI = \dfrac{V \times I}{S} \times 0.06 \quad \text{kJ/mm}}$$ > where $V$ in volts, $I$ in amperes, and $S$ in mm/min. ### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step) **Problem:** A welder uses SMAW with $V = 25$ V, $I = 200$ A, and travel speed $S = 300$ mm/min. Calculate the heat input in kJ/mm. **Step 1:** Identify given values: - $V = 25$ V - $I = 200$ A - $S = 300$ mm/min **Step 2:** Apply the heat input formula in J/mm: $$HI = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S}$$ $$HI = \dfrac{25 \times 200 \times 60}{300}$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the numerator: $$25 \times 200 = 5000$$ $$5000 \times 60 = 300000 \text{ J/min}$$ **Step 4:** Divide by travel speed: $$HI = \dfrac{300000}{300} = 1000 \text{ J/mm}$$ **Step 5:** Convert to kJ/mm: $$HI = \dfrac{1000}{1000} = 1.0 \text{ kJ/mm}$$ **Result:** $HI = 1.0$ kJ/mm. This is a **medium heat input** suitable for general structural steel SMAW. ### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Approximate Electrode Deposition Rate The deposition rate $R_d$ in kg/h for SMAW is empirically related to the welding current $I$ (Amperes). **Given empirical relationship:** $$R_d = 0.01 \times I \times 60 \quad \text{(kg/h)}$$ **Derivation logic:** - The mass of electrode consumed per second is approximately proportional to the current (due to melting rate ∝ I). - The factor 0.01 is an empirical constant calibrated to typical electrode efficiency. - Multiplying by 60 converts the per-minute value to per-hour. $$\boxed{R_d = 0.6 \times I \quad \text{(kg/h, approximate)}}$$ > *Worked Example:* For $I = 200$ A, $R_d = 0.6 \times 200 = 120$ kg/h, indicating the welder deposits about 120 kg of weld metal per hour — a typical industrial rate for a 4 mm diameter electrode. ## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Welding vs. Brazing vs. Soldering This is a **high-yield KTU comparison table** that the board frequently asks. | Parameter | Welding (SMAW) | Brazing | Soldering | |-----------|----------------|---------|-----------| | **Base metal melting** | Yes (fused) | No | No | | **Filler metal melting point** | Same as base metal (or close) | > 450 °C | < 450 °C | | **Heat source** | Electric arc | Torch / Furnace | Soldering iron / torch | | **Joint strength** | Equal to or > base metal | **High** (up to 70% of base) | **Low to Moderate** | | **Skill required** | High | Moderate | Low to Moderate | | **Capital cost** | Moderate | Moderate | Low | | **Porosity risk** | High (if poor shielding) | Low | Low | | **Typical filler** | E6013, E7018 | Brass, Bronze, Cu-P | Sn-Pb, Sn-Ag-Cu | | **Flux use** | Yes (in electrode) | Yes (borax-based) | Yes (rosin / ZnCl₂) | | **Typical applications** | Bridges, ships, pipelines | Tools, HVAC, jewellery | Electronics, plumbing | | **Temperature range** | 1500 °C – 7000 °C | 450 °C – 1200 °C | 90 °C – 450 °C | | **Joint type** | Permanent, metallurgical fusion | Permanent, capillary bond | Permanent, capillary bond | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Takeaway for KTU Board:** > The **only fundamental difference** between brazing and soldering is the **450 °C** filler metal melting point threshold. Brazed joints are much stronger than soldered joints, and they are chosen for structural, thermal, and electrical applications requiring high strength and temperature resistance. ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Choose Which Process? | Requirement | Recommended Process | |-------------|---------------------| | Joining thick steel plates for a ship hull | **SMAW** (E7018) | | Joining electrical wires on a PCB | **Soldering** | | Repairing a cracked copper refrigerant line | **Brazing** (Cu-P filler) | | Attaching tungsten carbide tip to steel shank | **Brazing** | | Field repair of a tractor chassis | **SMAW** (E6013) | | Stained glass artwork | **Soldering** (lead-tin) | | Aerospace fuselage panels | **GTAW / Friction Stir Welding** (not in KTU syllabus) | | Stainless steel food-grade pipe | **Brazing** with silver brazing alloy | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Classification of Metal Joining ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Metal Joining Processes] --> B[Welding] A --> C[Brazing] A --> D[Soldering] A --> E[Mechanical Fastening - Bolts Rivets] B --> B1[Fusion Welding] B --> B2[Solid State Welding] B1 --> B1A[Arc Welding] B1 --> B1B[Gas Welding] B1 --> B1C[Resistance Welding] B1 --> B1D[Thermit Welding] B1A --> B1A1[SMAW - Stick Welding] B1A --> B1A2[GTAW - TIG] B1A --> B1A3[GMAW - MIG] B1A --> B1A4[SAW - Submerged Arc] B2 --> B2A[Forge Welding] B2 --> B2B[Friction Welding] B2 --> B2C[Ultrasonic Welding] C --> C1[Torch Brazing] C --> C2[Furnace Brazing] C --> C3[Induction Brazing] C --> C4[Dip Brazing] D --> D1[Soft Soldering - below 450C] D --> D2[Hard Soldering - Silver Soldering] ``` > **Reading Guide:** The flowchart traces the KTU 2024 syllabus hierarchy. Notice that the question paper typically focuses on **Arc Welding (SMAW)** and the **Soldering/Brazing** branches. ## 4.2 Functional Flow: SMAW Process Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AC or DC Power Source] -->|Welding Cable| B[Electrode Holder] B --> C[Consumable Electrode with Flux] C -->|Arc Strike| D[Electric Arc - 6000 to 7000 C] D --> E[Base Metal Workpiece] D --> F[Melting of Flux Coating] F --> G[Shielding Gas Generated] F --> H[Liquid Slag Formation] G --> I[Weld Pool Protection] H --> I D --> J[Weld Pool] J --> K[Filler Metal Deposition] I --> J J --> L[Solidified Weld Bead] H --> M[Solidified Slag Layer] L --> N[Slag Removal - Chipping Hammer] M --> N N --> O[Final Clean Weld] E --> P[Ground Clamp - Return Path] P --> A ``` > **Reading Guide:** This block diagram maps the **energy flow** and **material flow** of a typical SMAW operation. The arc temperature is the central engine driving the entire process. ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Soldering vs Brazing Comparison Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Start: Clean Base Metals] --> A1[Apply Flux] A1 --> A2{Choose Filler Metal} A2 -->|Filler MP below 450 C| B1[Soldering] A2 -->|Filler MP above 450 C| B2[Brazing] B1 --> B1A[Heat Source: Soldering Iron or Low-Temp Torch] B1A --> B1B[Melt Solder - Sn Pb or Lead Free] B1B --> B1C[Capillary Flow into Joint] B1C --> B1D[Solidify at below 450 C] B1D --> B1E[Application: Electronics Plumbing] B2 --> B2A[Heat Source: Oxy Acetylene Torch or Furnace] B2A --> B2B[Melt Filler - Brass Bronze Cu P] B2B --> B2C[Capillary Flow into Tight Joint Gap] B2C --> B2D[Solidify at above 450 C] B2D --> B2E[Application: HVAC Tools Structural] ``` ## 4.4 Electrode Classification Decoder Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AWS Electrode Code: E 7018] --> B[Decode Position 1] A --> C[Decode Position 2 to 3] A --> D[Decode Position 4] A --> E[Decode Position 5] B --> B1[E = Electrode for Arc Welding] C --> C1[70 = 70,000 psi Minimum Tensile Strength] D --> D1[1 = All Welding Positions Allowed] E --> E1[8 = Low Hydrogen Iron Powder Coating - DC or AC] E1 --> F[Result: E7018 - Low Hydrogen - Structural Steel] ``` > **Reading Guide:** Memorise this decoder chain — it appears in KTU 2-mark and 3-mark questions frequently. The four digits to the right of "E" each carry a specific, testable meaning. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Differentiate between soldering and brazing. State any three applications of each.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Soldering | Brazing | |-----------|-----------|---------| | Filler metal melting point | Below 450 °C | Above 450 °C | | Joint strength | Lower (typically up to 30–40 MPa) | Higher (up to 70% of base metal strength) | | Flux type | Rosin, ZnCl₂ (mild) | Borax-based (aggressive) | | Typical filler | Sn-Pb, Sn-Ag-Cu | Brass, bronze, Cu-P | | Working temperature | 90 °C – 450 °C | 450 °C – 1200 °C | **Applications of Soldering (3):** 1. Joining of electronic components on printed circuit boards (PCBs). 2. Plumbing joints in domestic water supply lines (lead-tin solder). 3. Joining of stained glass and decorative artwork. **Applications of Brazing (3):** 1. Joining of copper refrigerant lines in HVAC and air-conditioning systems. 2. Attachment of tungsten carbide cutting tips to steel tool shanks. 3. Manufacturing of bicycle frames, automotive radiators, and heat exchangers. --- ### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Question]** **What is meant by "Shielded" in Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)? Explain the role of the flux coating.** **Model Answer:** The term **"Shielded"** in SMAW refers to the protection of the molten weld pool from the harmful effects of atmospheric gases (oxygen, nitrogen, and hydrogen). This protection is essential because oxygen causes **oxidation and porosity**, nitrogen causes **brittle nitrides**, and hydrogen causes **cold cracking** in the weld metal. **Role of Flux Coating (Triple Function):** 1. **Gas Shield Generation:** The flux decomposes under the arc heat, releasing a gaseous envelope of CO₂ and H₂ that displaces atmospheric air around the weld pool. 2. **Slag Formation:** The molten flux forms a protective slag layer that floats on the weld pool, dissolves impurities (oxides, silicates), and solidifies on the weld bead to slow the cooling rate. 3. **Deoxidation:** The flux contains deoxidising elements (like ferromanganese, ferrosilicon) that chemically react with and remove dissolved oxygen from the molten weld pool. > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic (KTU Board Tip):** The three functions of flux = **G**as shield + **S**lag shield + **D**eoxidation → "**GSD**". --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **A. (a) Explain in detail the working principle, equipment, and electrode classification of Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW).** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Working Principle of SMAW:** SMAW is a manual arc welding process in which the heat for welding is generated by an electric arc struck between a **consumable flux-coated electrode** and the **workpiece**. The arc melts the electrode tip and the base metal surface, forming a molten weld pool. As the electrode melts, it is deposited into the weld pool as filler metal. Simultaneously, the flux coating decomposes to form a shielding gas and a molten slag that protects the weld from atmospheric contamination. Upon cooling, the slag solidifies as a brittle crust and is chipped off, revealing the finished weld bead. The complete sequence is: 1. **Arc Initiation** — by scratch or tap striking. 2. **Arc Maintenance** — welder maintains an arc length of approximately **2–4 mm**. 3. **Filler Deposition** — molten droplets transfer from electrode to weld pool. 4. **Shielding** — flux generates gas and slag to protect the pool. 5. **Solidification** — weld pool forms a metallurgically fused bead. 6. **Slag Removal** — using a chipping hammer and wire brush. **Equipment Required (6 components):** 1. Welding Power Source (AC Transformer or DC Rectifier; Constant Current type). 2. Electrode Holder (insulated handle with current-carrying jaws). 3. Ground Clamp (connects workpiece to the return circuit). 4. Welding Cables (heavy-duty copper, sized for current rating). 5. Consumable Electrode (flux-coated filler rod). 6. Personal Protective Equipment — auto-darkening helmet, gloves, apron, and chipping hammer. **Electrode Classification — AWS E-XXXX System (with example E7013):** | Digit | Position | E | 70 | 1 | 3 | |-------|----------|---|---|---|---| | Meaning | — | Electrode | 70,000 psi tensile strength | All positions | Rutile coating, AC/DC, medium penetration | - **E** — denotes an electrode for arc welding. - **First two digits** — minimum tensile strength of the weld deposit in **ksi**. - **Third digit** — welding positions (1 = all positions, 2 = flat/horizontal only). - **Fourth digit** — coating type, current type (AC/DC), and penetration characteristics. Common electrodes: **E6013** (general purpose), **E7018** (low hydrogen, critical welds), **E6010** (deep penetration, pipe welding). **(b) Calculate the heat input for a SMAW process using the following data:** Voltage = 28 V, Current = 220 A, Travel Speed = 250 mm/min. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - Arc Voltage, $V = 28$ V - Welding Current, $I = 220$ A - Travel Speed, $S = 250$ mm/min **Step 1: Recall the Heat Input Formula (with kJ/mm):** $$HI = \dfrac{V \times I}{S} \times 0.06 \quad \text{kJ/mm}$$ **Step 2: Substitute the values:** $$HI = \dfrac{28 \times 220}{250} \times 0.06$$ **Step 3: Compute the numerator product:** $$28 \times 220 = 6160$$ **Step 4: Divide by travel speed:** $$\dfrac{6160}{250} = 24.64$$ **Step 5: Multiply by 0.06:** $$HI = 24.64 \times 0.06 = 1.4784 \text{ kJ/mm}$$ **Result:** $HI \approx 1.48$ kJ/mm. **Interpretation:** This is a moderately high heat input suitable for welding medium-thickness structural steel plates. Excessively high heat input may cause grain coarsening and reduced toughness, while very low input may cause lack of fusion. --- ### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]** **B. (a) Explain the principle, equipment, and applications of brazing. Compare it with soldering.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Principle of Brazing:** Brazing is a metal-joining process in which a **filler metal** with a melting point above **450 °C** but below the melting point of the **base metals** is melted and drawn into a closely-fitted joint by **capillary action**. The base metals are not melted. The molten filler metal wets the base metal surfaces, and upon solidification, it forms a strong metallurgical bond. **Key Steps:** 1. Cleaning of joint surfaces (oxide removal). 2. Application of flux (typically borax or fluoride-based). 3. Assembly of parts with a controlled clearance gap of **0.03 mm – 0.13 mm** for capillary flow. 4. Heating the assembly to a temperature above 450 °C. 5. Melting of the filler metal (e.g., brass, bronze, Cu-P). 6. Capillary flow of filler into the joint gap. 7. Solidification of the joint upon cooling. **Equipment Used in Brazing:** 1. Heat Source — **Oxy-acetylene torch** (most common), furnace, induction coil, or dip bath. 2. Filler Metal — in rod, wire, ring, or paste form. 3. Flux — borax paste or fluoride-based powder. 4. Fixtures / Jigs — to maintain joint clearance during heating. 5. Cleaning Tools — wire brushes, chemical pickling baths. **Applications of Brazing (5):** 1.
