# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1. Define "BIS" and state the IS codes for water absorption test and compressive strength test of burnt clay bricks. Mention the standard brick size as per IS 1077.
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]`**

**Model Answer:**
**BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** is the national standards body of India, responsible for the formulation and implementation of standards for products, including construction materials.

- **IS 3495 (Part 2):** Water absorption test of burnt clay bricks.
- **IS 3495 (Part 1):** Compressive strength test of burnt clay bricks.
- **Standard Modular Brick Size as per IS 1077:** $190 \text{ mm} \times 90 \text{ mm} \times 90 \text{ mm}$ (with 10 mm mortar joint, the nominal size becomes $200 \text{ mm} \times 100 \text{ mm} \times 100 \text{ mm}$).

---

### Question 2. What is the "bulking of sand"? Why is it considered a critical phenomenon in concrete mix design?
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar Pattern]`**

**Model Answer:**
**Bulking of sand** is defined as the **increase in the volume of sand** (with no change in mass) due to the formation of a thin moisture film around the sand particles, which causes them to push apart and increase the void volume.

- The maximum bulking occurs at a moisture content of **5% to 8%** and can be as high as **30%**.
- **Why it is critical:** When a site engineer measures sand by **volume** (using a box or head-pan) for concrete batching, the volume shown includes the bulking effect. If this bulking is ignored, the actual mass of sand used will be less than designed, leading to a **weaker mix** (less fine aggregate, more water/cement paste), compromising the **strength and durability** of the concrete. Hence, sand should be measured by **weight (kg)** on a batching plant to avoid this issue.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each 14-mark question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The model answer below follows the **valuation key format** used by KTU examiners.

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / May 2023, Similar Pattern]`**

**Q. (a)** List the **classifications of cement**. Explain the properties and uses of **Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC)** and **Portland Pozzolana Cement (PPC)** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **standard consistency test** of cement using **Vicat's apparatus**. State the standard initial and final setting times as per **IS 269**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a):
The classifications of cement are:
1. Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) — 33, 43, 53 Grade.
2. Portland Pozzolana Cement (PPC).
3. Rapid Hardening Portland Cement (RHPC).
4. Sulphate Resisting Cement (SRC).
5. Low Heat Portland Cement.
6. White Portland Cement.
7. Blast Furnace Slag Cement.

**OPC:**
- **Properties:** Composed primarily of Calcium Silicates ($C_3S, C_2S$), Calcium Aluminates ($C_3A, C_4AF$), and gypsum. Sets and hardens by hydration. Available in 33, 43, and 53 grades (defined by 28-day compressive strength in N/mm²). Sound, durable, and uniform.
- **Uses:** General construction, RCC structures, bridges, roads, pre-stressed concrete, and high-rise buildings.

**PPC:**
- **Properties:** Manufactured by grinding OPC clinker with 15% to 35% pozzolanic material (fly ash, calcined clay, or silica fumes). Produces less heat of hydration, denser, more durable, and offers better resistance to sulphates and chemical attack. Gains strength slowly but ultimate strength is higher.
- **Uses:** Marine construction, mass concrete for dams, foundations in sulphate-rich soils, plastering, and structures exposed to harsh chemical environments.

#### Model Answer for (b):
The **Standard Consistency Test** determines the **percentage of water** required to produce a cement paste of a definite plastic state (standard depth of penetration of the Vicat plunger).

**Procedure:**
1. Take 400 g of cement and mix with a measured quantity of water (e.g., starting with 25% by weight of cement).
2. Fill the Vicat mould resting on a non-porous plate with the cement paste.
3. Release the Vicat plunger (10 mm diameter) and measure the depth of penetration.
4. Repeat with varying water percentages until the plunger penetrates to a depth of **33 to 35 mm** from the top of the mould. This is the **Standard Consistency (P)**.

**Standard Setting Times (as per IS 269):**
- **Initial Setting Time:** $\geq 30 \text{ minutes}$ (Time when the Vicat needle penetrates $33$ to $35$ mm).
- **Final Setting Time:** $\leq 10 \text{ hours}$ (Time when the annular collar leaves no mark on the paste).
- The reason for these limits: To give sufficient **working time** for mixing, transporting, and placing the concrete before it begins to set.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]`**

**Q. (a)** Classify **building stones** based on their geological origin. Explain the properties, uses, and identification of **granite, marble, and slate**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What are the **qualities of good timber**? List the differences between **softwood and hardwood** with at least two examples each. Explain the **moisture content test** on timber as per IS 1121. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a):
**Geological Classification of Stones:**
1. **Igneous Rocks:** Formed by cooling of molten magma. E.g., Granite, Basalt.
2. **Sedimentary Rocks:** Formed by deposition and compaction of sediments. E.g., Limestone, Sandstone.
3. **Metamorphic Rocks:** Formed by transformation of existing rocks under heat and pressure. E.g., Marble (from Limestone), Slate (from Shale).

**Granite (Igneous):**
- **Properties:** Very hard, dense, high compressive strength (100–250 N/mm²), low water absorption (<0.5%), takes fine polish, available in grey, pink, green colors.
- **Identification:** Crystalline texture, visible grains of quartz, feldspar, and mica.
- **Uses:** Columns, flooring, bridge piers, railway ballast, retaining walls, kitchen countertops.

**Marble (Metamorphic):**
- **Properties:** Calcareous, takes excellent mirror polish, available in white, pink, green, black colors, hard and durable but less resistant to acids (especially HCl), uniform crystalline structure.
- **Identification:** Reacts with dilute hydrochloric acid (effervescence due to CaCO₃), shows a sugary crystalline appearance when broken.
- **Uses:** Flooring, decorative cladding, ornamental work, statues, bathroom walls, table tops.

**Slate (Metamorphic):**
- **Properties:** Argillaceous, very fine-grained, can be split into thin, smooth sheets, low water absorption, hard, and electrically non-conductive.
- **Identification:** Splits into thin laminations; dull sound on strike; dark grey/black color.
- **Uses:** Roofing tiles, paving slabs, blackboards, billiard tables, electrical switchboards, flagstones.

#### Model Answer for (b):
**Qualities of Good Timber:**
1. **Heartwood vs. Sapwood:** Should be made of *heartwood* (denser, more durable) rather than *sapwood*.
2. **Color & Grain:** Uniform color, close, straight, and regular grain. Free from knots.
3. **Density & Specific Gravity:** Higher density indicates higher strength.
4. **Moisture Content:** Should be properly seasoned (10–12% MC).
5. **Soundness:** Should produce a clear ringing sound on striking (no dull sound, which indicates decay).
6. **Smell:** Fresh, sweet, resinous smell (no musty smell).
7. **Durability:** Resistance to fungi, insects, and weathering.
8. **Workability:** Should be easy to saw, plane, and nail without splitting.

**Softwood vs. Hardwood:**

| Feature | Softwood | Hardwood |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Source** | Coniferous (gymnosperm) trees | Deciduous (angiosperm) trees |
| **Density** | Low | High |
| **Strength** | Low to medium | High |
| **Durability** | Low | High |
| **Workability** | Easy to cut and work | Difficult, requires more effort |
| **Examples** | Deodar, Chir, Pine, Spruce | Teak, Sal, Shisham, Rosewood, Jackwood |
| **Cost** | Generally cheaper | Generally expensive |
| **Uses** | Light frames, packing cases, ceiling planks, poles, interior works | Heavy construction, doors, windows, flooring, furniture, railway sleepers |

**Moisture Content Test (IS 1121 / IS 1708):**
- **Procedure:** Take a representative sample of timber and weigh it immediately to get the **wet mass ($W_{wet}$)**. Then place the sample in a hot air oven maintained at $103^\circ\text{C} \pm 2^\circ\text{C}$. Dry the sample for 24 hours, then weigh it. Repeat the process of drying and weighing (typically at 2-hour intervals) until the difference between two consecutive weights is negligible (i.e., the mass becomes constant). This constant mass is the **oven-dry mass ($W_{dry}$)**.
- **Formula:**
$$
MC (\%) = \left[ \frac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \right] \times 100
$$
- **Acceptance Criterion:** For structural timber used in construction, the moisture content should be between **10% and 12%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not write just "10-12%"** for moisture content. Students often lose 1 mark by not specifying "by oven-dry weight".
> 2. **OPC vs PPC confusion:** PPC has *slow* strength gain but *higher ultimate* strength — not slower strength forever. Do not mix this up.
> 3. **Bulking of sand is not a "property" of dry sand.** It only occurs in the *moist* state. Writing the definition without the "moisture film" concept will lose you 1 mark.
> 4. **Brick strength is applied on the bed face (190 × 90 mm), NOT on the side (90 × 90).** Using the wrong area in numericals is the most common KTU error.
> 5. **Do not confuse Fineness Modulus with Specific Surface.** FM is a single number indicating the average particle size; specific surface is the surface area per unit mass.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this checklist the night before your KTU exam.**

- [ ] **Brick Standard Size:** $190 \times 90 \times 90$ mm; Frog filled with 1:1 mortar before testing.
- [ ] **Brick Water Absorption Limit:** $\leq 20\%$ (IS 3495 Part 2).
- [ ] **Brick Compressive Strength:** First Class $\geq 10 \text{ N/mm}^2$, Second $\geq 7.5$, Third $\geq 5$.
- [ ] **Ringing Sound Test** indicates a sound, well-burnt brick.
- [ ] **Granite is igneous, Marble is metamorphic (from Limestone), Slate is metamorphic (from Shale), Sandstone and Limestone are sedimentary.**
- [ ] **Crushing strength of good building stone:** $\geq 100 \text{ N/mm}^2$.
- [ ] **Sea sand is unfit for RCC** because chlorides corrode the steel reinforcement.
- [ ] **M-Sand** (Manufactured Sand) is the most eco-friendly alternative to river sand.
- [ ] **Fineness Modulus (FM) of sand:** Ideal range is 2.2 to 3.2 for concrete fine aggregate.
- [ ] **Bulking of Sand:** Maximum is ~30% at 5-8% moisture content.
- [ ] **Silt content in sand:** $\leq 3\%$ for plastering, $\leq 5\%$ for masonry.
- [ ] **Cement Gypsum addition:** 3% to 5% by weight, to control flash setting.
- [ ] **Vicat Apparatus:** Used for standard consistency and setting time tests of cement.
- [ ] **Standard Cement Cube Size:** $70.6 \text{ mm} \times 70.6 \text{ mm} \times 70.6 \text{ mm}$ (area $\approx 5000 \text{ mm}^2$).
- [ ] **OPC Grades:** 33, 43, 53 — based on 28-day compressive strength in MPa.
- [ ] **PPC:** Fly ash/pozzolana added (15-35%); used for marine/mass concrete.
- [ ] **Timber Softwood examples:** Pine, Deodar, Chir. **Hardwood examples:** Teak, Sal, Shisham.
- [ ] **Timber Moisture Content Limit for construction:** 10% to 12% (oven-dry basis).
- [ ] **Heartwood** is preferred over sapwood for structural timber.
- [ ] **Seasoning** of timber is the controlled drying to reduce moisture content.
- [ ] **IS Codes to remember:** IS 1077 (Bricks), IS 3495 (Brick Tests), IS 383 (Aggregates), IS 269 (OPC 33), IS 8112 (OPC 43), IS 12269 (OPC 53), IS 1489 (PPC), IS 1708 (Timber), IS 1121 (Stone).
- [ ] **Bulking of sand does NOT occur in dry sand** — only with moisture content.
- [ ] **Coarse aggregate is stone/gravel; Fine aggregate is sand.**
- [ ] **First-class bricks should be used for permanent, load-bearing structures.**

**Final Exam Wisdom (KTU Board Examiner's Voice):**
*"In the KTU 2024 scheme, Module 4 is the most scoring area if you are conceptually clear. Focus on the two equations — Water Absorption and Compressive Strength. A single numerical on these carries 5–7 marks, and most students drop marks only by misreading the bed face of the brick. For the 14-mark question, the examiner's eye always goes to whether you have written the **IS Code numbers**. Writing the IS code alongside the answer is the single highest-value, lowest-effort trick to gain 1–2 bonus marks."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cement Concrete: Constituent Materials, Properties, and Types; Tests on Fresh and Hardened Concrete (Slump Test, Cube Compressive Strength as per IS Codes)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO MECHANICAL ENGINEERING & CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cement Concrete: Constituent Materials, Properties, and Types; Tests on Fresh and Hardened Concrete (Slump Test, Cube Compressive Strength as per IS Codes)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Cement Concrete

**Cement Concrete** is a composite construction material composed essentially of a **binding medium (Portland cement)**, **fine aggregate (sand)**, **coarse aggregate (gravel/crushed stone)**, and **water**, which hardens into a rock-like mass through a chemical reaction known as **hydration**. The resulting artificial stone is highly versatile, strong in compression, and forms the backbone of modern civil engineering infrastructure.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Concrete is NOT a simple mixture — it is a *heterogeneous, multi-phase, engineered composite* whose strength depends on the proportion, quality, and interaction of all four primary constituents plus optional chemical/mineral **admixtures**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Concrete Identity (Concrete Octave):** For every **1 m³** of structural concrete, approximately:
> - **Cement:** 350 – 450 kg
> - **Water:** 140 – 180 litres
> - **Fine Aggregate (Sand):** 600 – 750 kg
> - **Coarse Aggregate (Gravel):** 1100 – 1300 kg

---

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of Concrete — Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are baking a **cake** in your kitchen. The cake requires four ingredients, just like concrete:

| Cake Ingredient | Concrete Equivalent | Engineering Function |
|---|---|---|
| Flour (binder) | **Portland Cement** | Glues everything together; chemical glue |
| Butter / Oil (workability) | **Water** | Activates cement; controls flow |
| Sugar / Cocoa (filler) | **Fine Aggregate (Sand)** | Fills small gaps; smooth texture |
| Nuts / Chunks (bulk) | **Coarse Aggregate (Gravel)** | Gives bulk volume; load-bearing skeleton |
| Vanilla essence (modifier) | **Admixtures** | Optional performance enhancer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If you put **too much water** in the cake, it becomes soggy and weak. If you put **too little**, it crumbles. The same applies to concrete — the **water-cement ratio (w/c)** is the single most critical parameter governing the strength, durability, and workability of hardened concrete.

---

## 1.3 Engineering Significance

Concrete is the **second most consumed material on Earth** after water. The global consumption exceeds **30 billion tonnes per year**. Its dominance in construction arises from:

- **High compressive strength** (typically **20 – 60 N/mm²** for structural grades)
- **Mouldability** — can be cast into any shape while plastic
- **Economy** — locally available aggregates reduce cost
- **Durability** — excellent resistance to weathering, fire, and water
- **Compatibility with steel** — forms **Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC)**, overcoming concrete's weakness in tension

> [!NOTE]
> **Fascinating Fact for Examiners:** The **Pantheon Dome in Rome (AD 126)** — still the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome — used a sophisticated Roman concrete mix with *pozzolanic ash* and *lightweight pumice aggregate*. Modern concrete chemistry stands on the shoulders of this 1900-year-old innovation.

---

## 1.4 Constituent Materials — Quick Reference

### (a) Portland Cement
The most common binder. As per **IS 269, IS 8112, IS 12269** (BIS, Bureau of Indian Standards), common grades in India are:
- **OPC 33 Grade** (deprecated; was 33 N/mm² at 28 days)
- **OPC 43 Grade** — 43 N/mm² at 28 days
- **OPC 53 Grade** — 53 N/mm² at 28 days
- **PPC (Pozzolana Portland Cement)** — IS 1489
- **PSC (Portland Slag Cement)** — IS 455

### (b) Water
Potable water free from oils, acids, alkalis, salts, sugar, and organic matter. The **pH** must lie between **6 and 8**, and **suspended solids** must be below **2000 mg/litre**.

### (c) Fine Aggregate (Sand)
Particles passing through **4.75 mm IS Sieve** and retained on **75 micron sieve**. Natural river sand or **M-Sand (Manufactured Sand)** per **IS 383**.

### (d) Coarse Aggregate
Particles retained on **4.75 mm IS Sieve**, typically **10 mm, 20 mm, or 40 mm** nominal size. Crushed stone or natural gravel per **IS 383**.

### (e) Admixtures
Chemical or mineral additives per **IS 9103** (chemical) and **IS 3812** (fly ash) used to modify specific properties.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of Cement

Portland cement's engineering behaviour is governed by **fineness, soundness, setting time, compressive strength**, and **heat of hydration**.

| Property | Test Method | Standard | Typical Value (OPC 43) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fineness (specific surface) | Blaine Air Permeability | **IS 4031 (Part 2)** | **≥ 225 m²/kg** |
| Fineness (residue) | Sieve Test on 90 μm | IS 4031 | **≤ 10% residue** |
| Consistency | Vicat Apparatus | IS 4031 (Part 4) | **30 – 35% water by weight of cement** |
| Initial Setting Time | Vicat Needle | IS 4031 (Part 5) | **≥ 30 minutes** |
| Final Setting Time | Vicat Needle | IS 4031 (Part 5) | **≤ 600 minutes (10 hours)** |
| Soundness | Le-Chatelier / Autoclave | IS 4031 (Part 3) | **≤ 10 mm (Le-Chatelier)** |
| Compressive Strength (28 d) | Cube Test | IS 4031 (Part 6) | **43 N/mm² minimum** |
| Soundness (Autoclave) | Autoclave Expansion | IS 4031 | **≤ 0.8%** |

---

## 2.2 Properties of Fine Aggregate (Sand)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four Grading Zones of Sand (IS 383):** Sand is classified into **Grading Zones I, II, III, IV** from coarsest to finest. The **Fineness Modulus (FM)** is the cumulative percentage retained on standard sieves (150 μm to 4.75 mm) divided by 100. Standard structural concrete uses sand with **FM between 2.6 and 3.2**.

**Critical Fine Aggregate Tests:**
- **Sieve Analysis** — IS 2386 (Part 1)
- **Specific Gravity** — 2.6 to 2.7 (typical)
- **Bulking of Sand** — Moisture in sand causes volume increase; maximum bulking (~**30%**) occurs at **5–8% moisture content**. This is why **concrete is mixed by weight, not volume**.
- **Silt Content** — should not exceed **8%** (by volume)
- **Organic Impurities** — colour comparison test

---

## 2.3 Properties of Coarse Aggregate

| Property | Typical Range | Test Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Specific Gravity | **2.6 – 2.8** | IS 2386 (Part 3) |
| Water Absorption | **≤ 1%** (for 20 mm aggregate) | IS 2386 (Part 3) |
| Abrasion Value (Los Angeles) | **≤ 30%** for wearing surfaces | IS 2386 (Part 4) |
| Impact Value | **≤ 45%** (wearing) / **≤ 30%** (non-wearing) | IS 2386 (Part 4) |
| Crushing Value | **≤ 45%** (wearing) | IS 2386 (Part 4) |
| Flakiness Index | **≤ 25%** | IS 2386 (Part 1) |
| Elongation Index | **≤ 25%** | IS 2386 (Part 1) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Maximum Nominal Size Rule:** Per **IS 456:2000**, the nominal maximum size of coarse aggregate should not exceed **1/4 of the minimum thickness of the member**. For heavily reinforced members, it should be **5 mm less** than the minimum clear spacing between reinforcement bars.

---

## 2.4 Properties of Fresh Concrete

Fresh concrete must be **workable, cohesive, and free from segregation and bleeding**. Key properties:

### 2.4.1 Workability
Defined as the **ease with which fresh concrete can be mixed, transported, placed, compacted, and finished without segregation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Workability is NOT the same as Consistency.** Consistency refers only to the *wetness* or *flow* of the mix. Workability is the broader property encompassing consistency PLUS cohesiveness PLUS finishability.

**Factors Affecting Workability:**
- **Water Content** — increase → increase workability (but reduce strength)
- **Aggregate Properties** — shape, size, texture, grading
- **Cement Content** — more cement paste → more workable
- **Admixtures** — plasticizers and superplasticizers
- **Temperature** — high temperature reduces workability rapidly
- **Time** — workability decreases with time due to hydration

### 2.4.2 Segregation
**Separation of coarse aggregate from the mortar** due to improper handling, over-vibration, or excessive drop in height. Maximum free fall height of concrete = **1.5 m**.

### 2.4.3 Bleeding
**Rise of water to the top surface** of fresh concrete after placement, due to settlement of solids. Excessive bleeding causes weak top layers and laitance formation.

### 2.4.4 Setting
Fresh concrete gradually loses plasticity. **Initial set** is the stage when concrete begins to stiffen; **final set** is when it becomes rigid.

---

## 2.5 Properties of Hardened Concrete

| Property | Definition | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Compressive Strength (f_ck)** | Maximum compressive stress at failure | **20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 60 N/mm²** (IS 456) |
| **Tensile Strength** | Resistance to direct tension (~**10%** of f_ck) | **2 – 5 N/mm²** |
| **Flexural Strength** | Modulus of rupture (~**15–20%** of f_ck) | **3 – 6 N/mm²** |
| **Modulus of Elasticity (E_c)** | Stiffness under short-term loading | See formula below |
| **Durability** | Resistance to weathering, chemical attack, abrasion | Depends on w/c, cover, curing |
| **Shrinkage** | Volume reduction on drying | **0.0003 – 0.0008** strain |
| **Creep** | Time-dependent deformation under sustained load | Increases with age of loading |
| **Permeability** | Resistance to water/air penetration | Lower w/c → lower permeability |
| **Density** | Mass per unit volume | **24 – 25 kN/m³** (NCC); **18 – 22 kN/m³** (LWC) |

### Modulus of Elasticity (IS 456)

$$E_c = 5000 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$$

Where **f_ck** is the characteristic compressive strength in **N/mm²** and **E_c** is in **N/mm²**.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Concrete Engineering (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **w/c = (Mass of Water) ÷ (Mass of Cement)** | Water-cement ratio (Abrams' Law denominator) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | **f_c ∝ 1 / (w/c ratio)** | **Abrams' Law** — compressive strength inversely proportional to w/c | N/mm² |
| 3 | **E_c = 5000 √f_ck** | IS 456 — Modulus of Elasticity | N/mm² |
| 4 | **f_cr = 0.7 f_ck** | Target mean strength for mix design (IS 10262) | N/mm² |
| 5 | **f_cr = f_ck + 1.65 σ** | General target strength formula | N/mm² |
| 6 | **Density = Mass ÷ Volume** | For 150 mm cube: ~8.5 kg (M20) | kg/m³ |
| 7 | **f_t = 0.7 √f_ck** | Split tensile strength (IS 456) | N/mm² |
| 8 | **f_r = 0.7 √f_ck** | Flexural strength estimate (IS 456) | N/mm² |
| 9 | **Slump = H_initial − H_subsided** | Slump cone test measurement | mm |
| 10 | **Compressive Strength = Load / Area** | Cube/cylinder test | N/mm² |
| 11 | **FM = Σ Cumulative % Retained / 100** | Fineness Modulus calculation | Dimensionless |
| 12 | **Water Absorption % = ((W_ssd − W_dry) / W_dry) × 100** | Aggregate water absorption test | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MNEMONIC FOR KTU:** *"**W**ater **C**ement **S**trength **C**ube **S**lump" — **WCSS CS** — covers all major formula families tested in KTU papers.

---

## 2.7 Types of Concrete — Engineering Classification

| Category | Type | Key Feature | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **By Density** | **Normal Weight Concrete (NWC)** | Density 24 – 25 kN/m³ | General RCC structures |
| | **Lightweight Concrete (LWC)** | Density < 18 kN/m³ | Insulation, partition walls |
| | **Heavyweight Concrete (HWC)** | Density > 26 kN/m³ | Radiation shielding (nuclear) |
| **By Strength** | **Ordinary Concrete** (M10 – M20) | 10 – 20 N/mm² | Non-structural, PCC |
| | **Standard Concrete** (M25 – M40) | 25 – 40 N/mm² | Buildings, bridges |
| | **High-Strength Concrete (HSC)** | M50 – M80 | High-rise columns |
| | **Ultra-High-Performance Concrete (UHPC)** | > 150 N/mm² | Special structures |
| **By Application** | **Plain Cement Concrete (PCC)** | No reinforcement | Pavements, beds |
| | **Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC)** | With steel rebars | Beams, columns, slabs |
| | **Prestressed Concrete (PSC)** | Pre-tensioned/post-tensioned steel | Long-span bridges |
| **By Special Property** | **Ready-Mix Concrete (RMC)** | Mixed at plant, transported | Urban construction |
| | **Self-Compacting Concrete (SCC)** | Flows under own weight; no vibration | Congested reinforcement |
| | **High-Performance Concrete (HPC)** | Engineered for durability + strength | Marine, chemical plants |
| | **Fibre-Reinforced Concrete (FRC)** | Steel/polypropylene fibres added | Industrial floors, tunnel linings |
| | **Polymer Concrete** | Polymer binder (no cement) | Repairs, overlays |
| | **Geopolymer Concrete** | No Portland cement; fly ash + alkali | Sustainable construction |
| | **Pervious Concrete** | High porosity; allows water passage | Parking lots, footpaths |
| | **Pumped Concrete** | Pushed through pipelines | High-rise construction |
| | **Shotcrete / Sprayed Concrete** | Pneumatically projected | Tunnel linings, pools |
| | **Roller-Compacted Concrete (RCC)** | Zero-slump; compacted by rollers | Dams, heavy pavements |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** "Discuss the different types of concrete" (often a 7-mark sub-question in Part B). Memorise at least **8 types with one-line applications** to secure full marks.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Utility

- **RCC** forms the skeleton of every building, bridge, dam, and metro station.
- **SCC** allows construction in zones with dense reinforcement (e.g., beam-column joints) where vibration is impossible.
- **UHPC** is used in iconic footbridges (e.g., **Seonyu Footbridge, Seoul**) and façade panels.
- **Pervious concrete** is now mandated in many cities for stormwater management.
- **Geopolymer concrete** offers **up to 80% reduction in CO₂** versus Portland cement, addressing climate goals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Problems & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Abrams' Law — The Foundation of Strength

**Derivation of the Conceptual Law:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Abrams' Law (1918):** Duff A. Abrams, an American concrete researcher, established through experiments that for a given set of materials, the compressive strength of concrete is **inversely proportional to the water-cement ratio** for plastic mixes, and **exponentially related** to it for a wide range of w/c.

**Mathematical Form (for plastic mixes, w/c ≥ 0.40):**

$$f_c = \frac{K_1}{K_2^{w/c}}$$

Where:
- **f_c** = Compressive strength of concrete (N/mm²)
- **w/c** = Water-cement ratio (by mass)
- **K_1, K_2** = Empirical constants depending on cement type, age, and curing conditions

**Simplified Working Form (most common in design):**

$$f_c \approx \frac{A}{B^{w/c}}$$

For **28-day strength** with **OPC**, typical values: **A ≈ 100**, **B ≈ 0.85 – 0.95** (varies with aggregate type and cement).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Reasoning Chain:** Lower w/c → less water → less capillary pores after hydration → denser microstructure → **higher compressive strength**. But w/c cannot be too low (typically **≥ 0.40** for workability); this is where **superplasticizers** become essential — they reduce water demand without sacrificing workability.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Problem 1 — Slump Cone Test Analysis

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2023 Style]**
> 
> In a slump cone test, the **initial height of the mould is 300 mm**. After the cone is slowly lifted, the **subsided concrete mass measures a height of 215 mm**.
> 
> **(a)** Calculate the slump value.
> **(b)** Identify the type of slump and its implication on concrete workability.
> **(c)** State the IS code reference.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Identify given data**

$$H_{initial} = 300 \text{ mm}, \quad H_{subsided} = 215 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Calculate slump**

$$\text{Slump} = H_{initial} - H_{subsided}$$

$$\text{Slump} = 300 - 215$$

$$\boxed{\text{Slump} = 85 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 3: Identify slump type and interpretation**

Looking at the Mermaid diagram in Section 4, the subsided concrete in this case forms a **truncated cone with the top settling uniformly and no lateral collapse or shear** — this is the **TRUE SLUMP** pattern.

**Step 4: IS Code Reference**

As per **IS 1199:1959** (Reaffirmed 2018) — *"Methods of Sampling and Analysis of Concrete."*

**Step 5: Workability classification**

For a **medium-strength RCC work** (beams, slabs, columns with moderate reinforcement), a slump of **85 mm** corresponds to a **medium workability** range (**50 – 100 mm** per IS 456) — **acceptable for most structural applications**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse the *direction* of measurement. The slump is the **vertical difference** between the mould top and the subsided concrete centre, NOT the difference between the original cone height and the subsided height. The mould is 300 mm; the cone shape remains but subsides by **85 mm**.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Problem 2 — Cube Compressive Strength

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style]**
> 
> Three **150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm** concrete cubes were cast and tested after **28 days** of curing. The failure loads recorded are **510 kN, 540 kN, and 525 kN**.
> 
> **(a)** Calculate the compressive strength of each cube.
> **(b)** Determine the **average compressive strength** and the **characteristic compressive strength (f_ck)** of the concrete.
> **(c)** Comment on whether the mix achieves **M25 grade** as per IS 456.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area of cube**

$$A = 150 \times 150 = 22500 \text{ mm}^2 = 225 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 2: Compressive strength of each cube**

$$f_1 = \frac{F_1}{A} = \frac{510 \times 10^3}{22500}$$

$$f_1 = \frac{510000}{22500} = 22.67 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$f_2 = \frac{F_2}{A} = \frac{540 \times 10^3}{22500} = 24.00 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$f_3 = \frac{F_3}{A} = \frac{525 \times 10^3}{22500} = 23.33 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Average compressive strength**

$$f_{avg} = \frac{f_1 + f_2 + f_3}{3} = \frac{22.67 + 24.00 + 23.33}{3}$$

$$f_{avg} = \frac{70.00}{3} = 23.33 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Characteristic compressive strength (f_ck)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (IS 456:2000, Clause 2.1):** *Characteristic strength is the value of the cube compressive strength below which **not more than 5% of the test results** are expected to fall.* In design, only **minimum of 3 specimens** is averaged, and the result is compared with f_ck.

For a **single batch** of three cubes, IS 456 specifies that the **average of 3 specimens** shall be taken as the test result.

$$f_{ck, observed} = 23.33 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 5: Grade assessment**

Required **f_ck for M25** = **25 N/mm²** at 28 days.

Since **23.33 N/mm² < 25 N/mm²**, the mix **DOES NOT achieve M25 grade**. It actually qualifies closer to **M20 grade** (where f_ck ≥ 20 N/mm², and 23.33 > 20, so it lies in **M20 range**).

> [!NOTE]
> **More rigorous assessment:** In statistical quality control, **f_ck = f_mean − 1.65 σ**, where σ is standard deviation. For first-time production, IS 456 assumes σ ≈ 4 N/mm² for "Very Good" control, giving **target mean strength f_target = 25 + 1.65 × 4 = 31.6 N/mm²**. Hence the **target** is actually 31.6 N/mm² for the trial mix, but the **acceptance criterion** is f_ck ≥ 25 N/mm².

---

## 3.4 Numerical Problem 3 — Mix Proportioning (IS 10262 Concept)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
> 
> For an **M25 grade** concrete mix, the target mean strength is to be calculated. Given:
> - f_ck = 25 N/mm²
> - Standard deviation σ = 5 N/mm² (assumed "Good" control)
> - Risk factor t = 1.65 (for 5% defectives)
> 
> **(a)** Calculate target mean strength f_cr.
> **(b)** If the actual test result gives 26 N/mm², comment on acceptance.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Target mean strength formula (IS 10262:2019)**

$$f_{cr} = f_{ck} + 1.65 \times \sigma$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$f_{cr} = 25 + 1.65 \times 5$$

$$f_{cr} = 25 + 8.25$$

$$\boxed{f_{cr} = 33.25 \text{ N/mm}^2}$$

**Step 3: Acceptance check**

The trial mix must achieve **f_cr = 33.25 N/mm²** to provide 95% confidence that no more than 5% of cubes will fall below 25 N/mm².

The actual observed strength of **26 N/mm² < 33.25 N/mm²** → the mix is **NOT acceptable** for M25 grade with the assumed control level.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Problem 4 — Bulk Density & Mix Volume Check

> **[Model Problem]**
> 
> A concrete mix has the following proportions by weight for **1 m³**:
> - Cement = 380 kg
> - Water = 152 kg
> - Fine aggregate = 620 kg
> - Coarse aggregate = 1240 kg
> 
> **(a)** Calculate the w/c ratio.
> **(b)** Calculate the total mass of concrete per m³.
> **(c)** If the fresh density is 2400 kg/m³, find the **yield** (actual volume produced).

### Solution:

**Step 1: w/c ratio**

$$w/c = \frac{152}{380} = 0.40$$

**Step 2: Total mass**

$$M_{total} = 380 + 152 + 620 + 1240 = 2392 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3: Actual volume produced (yield)**

$$V_{actual} = \frac{M_{total}}{\rho_{fresh}} = \frac{2392}{2400}$$

$$V_{actual} = 0.9967 \text{ m}^3 \approx 1.00 \text{ m}^3}$$

The mix is correctly proportioned (yield ≈ 1 m³).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Slump Test vs. Cube Compressive Strength Test

| Parameter | **Slump Test** | **Cube Compressive Strength Test** |
|---|---|---|
| **Property Evaluated** | Workability / Consistency | Strength |
| **Stage of Concrete** | Fresh | Hardened |
| **IS Code Reference** | **IS 1199:1959** | **IS 516:1959** |
| **Apparatus** | Slump cone (100 mm top, 200 mm base, 300 mm height) | Compression Testing Machine (CTM) of capacity **2000 kN** |
| **Mould Size** | Standard truncated cone | **150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm** cube |
| **Compaction Method** | 25 strokes with 16 mm dia rod, in 3 layers | Table vibration or hand tamping (35 blows in 4 layers for 150 mm cube) |
| **Curing Required** | None (immediate test) | **28 days** standard curing in water at **27 ± 2°C** |
| **Output Quantity** | Slump value in **mm** | Load in **kN** → Strength in **N/mm²** |
| **Acceptance Range** | **25 – 175 mm** depending on workability need | **≥ f_ck** as per IS 456 |
| **Number of Specimens** | Single test per batch | Minimum **3 cubes** per sample |
| **Loading Rate (CTM)** | N/A | **140 kg/cm²/min** (≈ **13.7 N/mm²/min**) — **STRICTLY CONTROLLED** |
| **Failure Pattern Expected** | True / Shear / Collapse slump | Cone-and-shear (or hourglass) failure |
| **Loading Direction** | N/A (self-weight) | **Perpendicular to casting direction** |
| **Major Source of Error** | Operator variability in rod strokes | Bearing surface unevenness; non-parallel loading faces |

---

## 3.7 Calculation of Water for Concrete (Step-by-Step IS 10262 Approach)

> **[KTU Numerical Style]**
> 
> An **M20 concrete mix** requires a w/c ratio of 0.50. If **380 kg of cement** is used per m³, calculate:
> - **(a)** Water content
> - **(b)** Volume of cement paste
> - **(c)** If 30% of the cement volume is filled with water + gel, find voids in aggregate.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Water content**

$$W = (w/c) \times C = 0.50 \times 380 = 190 \text{ litres}$$

**Step 2: Volume of cement (specific gravity G_c = 3.15)**

$$V_{cement} = \frac{380}{3.15 \times 1000} = 0.1206 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3: Volume of water**

$$V_{water} = \frac{190}{1000} = 0.190 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4: Total cement paste volume**

$$V_{paste} = 0.1206 + 0.190 = 0.3106 \text{ m}^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Step-by-step derivations with explicit substitution and unit conversion (kg → m³ using specific gravity) are mandatory for full marks in Part B numericals. Do not skip any intermediate step.

---

## 3.8 Derivation of Mix Design Target Strength (Theoretical)

The **target mean strength** derivation is based on the **statistical normal distribution** of concrete strength test results:

$$f_{cr} = f_{ck} + t \cdot \sigma$$

Where:
- **f_cr** = Target mean strength (N/mm²)
- **f_ck** = Characteristic compressive strength (N/mm²)
- **t** = Risk factor (statistical parameter) = **1.65** for 5% defectives (95% confidence)
- **σ** = Standard deviation (N/mm²) — depends on quality control level

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality Control Levels (IS 10262:2019):**
> - "Excellent" control: σ ≈ 3 N/mm²
> - "Very Good" control: σ ≈ 4 N/mm²
> - "Good" control: σ ≈ 5 N/mm²
> - "Fair" control: σ ≈ 6 N/mm²

**Why 1.65?** In a standard normal distribution, the area to the right of **μ + 1.65σ** is exactly **5%**, meaning only 5% of the population falls below the characteristic value — matching IS 456's definition of characteristic strength.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concrete Manufacturing & Curing Flow Architecture

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the entire concrete production and testing workflow, from raw material intake to quality acceptance. This is the **standard reference chart** for KTU 14-mark and 7-mark questions on the "concrete manufacturing process."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[Raw Material Intake] --> cemIn[Portland Cement IS 269 8112 12269]
    start1 --> wIn[Potable Water pH 6 to 8]
    start1 --> faIn[Fine Aggregate Sand IS 383]
    start1 --> caIn[Coarse Aggregate 20 mm 10 mm IS 383]
    start1 --> admIn[Chemical Admixtures IS 9103]

    cemIn --> batch1[Batching by Weight]
    wIn --> batch1
    faIn --> batch1
    caIn --> batch1
    admIn --> batch1

    batch1 --> mix1[Mixing in Drum or Pan Mixer 2 to 3 min]
    mix1 --> trans1[Transportation Trolley or Pump]
    trans1 --> place1[Placement Height less than 1.5 m]
    place1 --> comp1[Compaction Needle or Table Vibrator]

    comp1 --> ftest{Fresh Concrete}
    ftest --> slump[Slump Test IS 1199]
    ftest --> compA[Compaction Factor Test]
    ftest --> vebe[Vee Bee Consistometer]

    slump --> okS{Slump within Limit}
    okS -- Yes --> cure1
    okS -- No --> rej1[Reject and Redesign Mix]

    comp1 --> cure1[Curing 28 days at 27 plus minus 2 degree C]
    cure1 --> htest{Hardened Concrete}
    htest --> cube[Cube Compression Test IS 516]
    htest --> cyl[Cylinder Split Tensile Test]
    htest --> flex[Flexural Test on Beam]

    cube --> res{Result Comparison}
    cyl --> res
    flex --> res

    res -- Greater than fck --> accept[Acceptance Confirmed M Grade Verified]
    res -- Less than fck --> rej2[Non Conformance IS 456 Clause 17]
    rej2 --> ndt[Non Destructive Testing Rebound Hammer UPV Test]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** Note the **forking decision points** (diamond shapes). KTU examiners reward the ability to draw such **flowcharts with conditional branches** showing acceptance/rejection logic.

---

## 4.2 Slump Cone Test — Apparatus & Procedure Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Slump Cone Mold 100 mm top, 200 mm base, 300 mm height] --> A2[Non Absorbent Base Plate]
    A2 --> A3[Tamping Rod 16 mm dia, 600 mm long, bullet nosed]
    A3 --> A4[Filling in 4 Equal Layers]
    A4 --> A5[25 Rodding Strokes per Layer]
    A5 --> A6[Strike Off Top Surface Level]
    A6 --> A7[Wait 30 sec, Lift Cone Vertically in 5 to 10 sec]
    A7 --> A8[Measure Vertical Settlement Slump mm]
    A8 --> A9{Visual Pattern}
    A9 --> P1[True Slump Uniform Settlement]
    A9 --> P2[Shear Slump One Half Slides]
    A9 --> P3[Collapse Slump Mass Collapses]

    P2 --> W1[Repeat Test with Fresh Sample]
    P3 --> W2[Indicates High Water Content]
    P1 --> A10[Compare with IS 456 Workability Slump Table]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Visual Aid:** In exams, always draw the **truncated cone in 3D**, label all **three diameters (top 100, base 200, height 300 mm)**, and indicate the **rodding pattern (25 strokes in 4 layers)**. This is worth **2 free marks** in the procedure section.

---

## 4.3 Cube Compression Test — Stepwise Procedure Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Casting Cube 150 mm size IS 516] --> s2[Compaction Table Vibrator for 35 sec]
    s2 --> s3[Surface Smoothed and Covered]
    s3 --> s4[Demould after 24 plus minus 0.5 hours]
    s4 --> s5[Curing Tank at 27 plus minus 2 degree C for 28 days]
    s5 --> s6[Surface Drying Before Testing]
    s6 --> s7[Place Cube in CTM Centrally]
    s7 --> s8[Apply Load at 140 kg per cm square per min]
    s8 --> s9[Record Failure Load in kN]
    s9 --> s10[Compute Strength Load divided by Area]
    s10 --> s11[Average of 3 Cubes is Test Result]
    s11 --> s12[Compare with f_ck as per IS 456]
```

---

## 4.4 Concrete Constituents Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph binders[BINDER PHASE]
        c1[Portland Cement OPC 43 53]
        c2[Supplementary Cementitious Materials]
        c3[Fly Ash IS 3812]
        c4[GGBS IS 16714]
        c5[Silica Fume]
    end

    subgraph aggregates[AGGREGATE PHASE 60 to 75 percent Volume]
        a1[Fine Aggregate Sand Zone II to III]
        a2[Coarse Aggregate 20 mm 12.5 mm]
    end

    subgraph fluid[FLUID PHASE]
        f1[Mixing Water w/c 0.40 to 0.60]
    end

    subgraph chemical[CHEMICAL MODIFIERS]
        m1[Plasticizer IS 9103]
        m2[Superplasticizer]
        m3[Accelerator]
        m4[Retarder]
        m5[Air Entraining Agent]
    end

    binders -->|Hydration| matrix[Hardened Cement Matrix]
    aggregates -->|Mechanical Interlock| matrix
    fluid -->|Activates| binders
    chemical -->|Modify Properties| matrix

    matrix --> finalProp[Engineering Properties Strength Durability Workability]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Decoding the Block Diagram:** This is the *Five-Phase Model* of concrete. The **binder phase** provides cohesion, the **aggregate phase** provides bulk, the **fluid phase** activates hydration, and **chemical modifiers** fine-tune performance. The resulting **matrix** gives concrete its engineering properties.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram of Stress-Strain Behaviour of Concrete

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    st0[Stage 0 Initial Loading] --> st1[Stage 1 Elastic Region Up to 30 percent f_c]
    st1 --> st2[Stage 2 Microcracking Stage 30 to 50 percent f_c]
    st2 --> st3[Stage 3 Stable Crack Propagation 50 to 75 percent f_c]
    st3 --> st4[Stage 4 Unstable Crack Growth 75 to 100 percent f_c]
    st4 --> st5[Stage 5 Failure Cone and Shear Pattern]

    st0 -.-> lbl1[Stress Strain Curve Approximately Linear]
    st1 -.-> lbl2[Modulus E_c equals 5000 sqrt f_ck]
    st2 -.-> lbl3[Cracks Initiate at Paste Aggregate Interface]
    st3 -.-> lbl4[Visible Surface Cracks]
    st4 -.-> lbl5[Strain Softening Begins]
    st5 -.-> lbl6[Residual Strength about 20 percent f_c]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The **descending branch** of the stress-strain curve (Stages 4–5) is critical for **earthquake-resistant design** and **ductility analysis**. Plain concrete is **brittle**; adding reinforcement transforms it into a **ductile composite**.

---

## 4.6 Application Selection Matrix — Choosing the Right Concrete

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    appQ{Application Type} -->|High Rise| hrc[High Strength Concrete M50 plus]
    appQ -->|Bridge Deck| hpcSol[High Performance HPC plus Low w/c]
    appQ -->|Marine| dur[Low Permeability Concrete plus Mineral Admixtures]
    appQ -->|Densely Reinforced| sccFlow[Self Compacting Concrete]
    appQ -->|Pavement| rccMix[Roller Compacted Concrete]
    appQ -->|Radiation Shield| hwcMix[Heavy Weight Concrete plus Barytes]
    appQ -->|Insulation| lwcMix[Lightweight Concrete plus Pumice]
    appQ -->|Quick Repair| accMix[Accelerated Cure Concrete]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This decision-support flowchart is **frequently asked as a "Discuss the suitability of different concrete types for varying field conditions"** question in KTU Part B.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> **"Define the term 'workability of concrete'. List any four factors affecting workability."**

### Model Answer:

**Definition:** Workability is defined as the **ease with which fresh concrete can be mixed, transported, placed, compacted, and finished without segregation and bleeding**, while maintaining homogeneity.

**Four Factors Affecting Workability:**
1. **Water Content** — Higher water → better flow → more workable (but lower strength)
2. **Aggregate Shape, Size, and Grading** — Rounded aggregates → better workability; finer grading → better cohesion
3. **Cement Content and Fineness** — More cement paste → improved workability
4. **Admixtures** — Plasticizers and superplasticizers increase workability
5. *(Bonus point)* Temperature — higher temperature reduces workability
6. *(Bonus point)* Time — workability decreases with elapsed time

> **Valuation Tip:** A clean, 2-3 sentence definition + 4 factors (with brief 1-line explanations) is enough for **full 3 marks**. Avoid writing irrelevant detail.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

> **"What is meant by 'bulking of sand'? Why is it important in concrete mix design?"**

### Model Answer:

**Definition:** *Bulking of sand* is the **phenomenon of increase in volume of sand** due to the **presence of moisture content** in it. A thin film of water around sand particles causes them to push apart, increasing bulk volume.

**Key Numerical Detail:** Maximum bulking (~**30%**) occurs at **5–8% moisture content** by weight. Beyond this, water fills the voids and bulking decreases.

**Importance in Concrete Mix Design:**
- Sand measured **by volume** can lead to **deficient sand content** due to bulking, causing weak, porous concrete.
- Hence **IS 456:2000** recommends **mixing concrete by weight, not volume**, to eliminate the bulking error.
- Field engineers must account for bulking if volume batching is unavoidable.

> **Valuation Tip:** Mention **"5–8% moisture"** and **"30% maximum bulking"** — both are KTU-favourite numerical anchors.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[Per KTU 2024 Scheme:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** divided into two **7-mark sub-parts**. Students must answer with internal choice.**]

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper Style]**

> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **constituent materials of cement concrete** along with their desirable properties. (7 Marks)
> 
> **(b)** Describe the **procedure for conducting the slump cone test** as per **IS 1199:1959**, with a neat sketch. Classify the different types of slump with engineering interpretation. (7 Marks)

### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]:

The four essential constituents of cement concrete are:

**1. Cement (Binding Material)**
- **Desirable Properties:**
  - High fineness (≥ **225 m²/kg** for OPC 43)
  - Adequate setting time (**Initial ≥ 30 min, Final ≤ 600 min**)
  - Soundness (Le-Chatelier expansion ≤ **10 mm**)
  - Compressive strength per grade (33/43/53 N/mm² at 28 days)
- **Types:** OPC, PPC, PSC, low-heat cement, sulphate-resistant cement
- **Standard:** IS 269, IS 8112, IS 12269

**2. Water (Mixing & Curing)**
- **Desirable Properties:** Potable, pH **6–8**, free from oils, salts, sugar, organics
- **Role:** Activates cement hydration; controls w/c ratio
- **Curing water** should be the same quality as mixing water

**3. Fine Aggregate (Sand)**
- **Desirable Properties:** Clean, hard, durable, well-graded (Zone II preferred)
- **Grading Zones:** I, II, III, IV per IS 383
- **Limit on Silt Content:** ≤ **8%** by volume
- **Fineness Modulus Range:** **2.6 – 3.2** for structural concrete
- **Bulking awareness** is critical

**4. Coarse Aggregate (Gravel)**
- **Desirable Properties:** Hard, dense, durable, angular (for high strength)
- **Nominal Sizes:** 10 mm, 20 mm, 40 mm (most common: **20 mm**)
- **Limit Values:** Los Angeles Abrasion ≤ 30% (wearing) / 45% (non-wearing)
- **Maximum Size Rule:** ≤ 1/4 of member thickness per IS 456

**5. Admixtures (Optional, but Critical)**
- **Plasticizers** reduce water demand by 5–12%
- **Superplasticizers** reduce water by 12–30% (used in HPC, SCC)
- **Accelerators** speed up strength gain
- **Retarders** delay setting in hot weather
- **Air-entraining agents** improve freeze-thaw resistance
- **Standards:** IS 9103, IS 3812 (fly ash)

> **Valuation Tip:** Each constituent's *desirable property* earns **0.5–1 mark**; standards/IS codes earn bonus marks.

### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]:

**Apparatus (per IS 1199:1959):**
- Slump cone mould — **top diameter 100 mm, base diameter 200 mm, height 300 mm**
- Non-absorbent base plate
- Tamping rod — **16 mm diameter, 600 mm long, bullet-nosed**
- Measuring scale

**Procedure:**
1. Clean the mould and place on a smooth, level, non-absorbent plate
2. Fill the mould in **4 equal layers**
3. Compact each layer with **25 strokes** of the tamping rod, distributed uniformly
4. Strike off the top surface flush with the mould
5. Hold the cone firmly and **lift vertically** in **5–10 seconds** without twisting
6. Allow the concrete to subside under its own weight
7. Measure the **vertical difference** between the top of the mould and the **highest point of the subsided concrete**
8. Record the **slump value in millimetres**

**Three Types of Slump (with Engineering Interpretation):**

| Type | Visual Description | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **True Slump** | Uniform vertical settlement, retains cone shape | Ideal mix — good cohesion and workability |
| **Shear Slump** | One half of the cone slides down laterally | Indicates lack of cohesion; **test must be repeated** |
| **Collapse Slump** | Concrete collapses completely, spreading widely | Excessive water content; **not acceptable** — high w/c ratio |

**IS Code Reference:** **IS 1199:1959** (Reaffirmed 2018) — *Methods of Sampling and Analysis of Concrete*

> **Valuation Tip:** A neat sketch of the **3D cone with measurements** earns **1–2 free marks**. Labelling rodding strokes (25 in 4 layers) earns another mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

> **(a)** Explain the **procedure for determining the compressive strength of concrete** using **150 mm cubes** as per **IS 516:1959**. Discuss the preparation, curing, testing, and computation of results. (7 Marks)
> 
> **(b)** Define **characteristic compressive strength (f_ck)**. A concrete mix design has a target mean strength of **33.25 N/mm²** and a standard deviation of **5 N/mm²**. Determine the grade of concrete. If three cubes tested at 28 days gave strengths of **24.5, 25.5, and 26.0 N/mm²**, assess whether the mix conforms to IS 456. (7 Marks)

### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]:

**Apparatus:**
- Standard **150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm** cube moulds (IS 10086)
- Compression Testing Machine (CTM) of capacity **2000 kN**
- Tamping rod (16 mm, 600 mm)
- Vibrating table or needle vibrator
- Curing tank (water at **27 ± 2°C**)

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

**1. Preparation of Moulds:**
- Clean moulds, apply releasing agent
- Assemble tightly to prevent leakage

**2. Casting:**
- Fill mould in **4 equal layers**
- Compact each layer by **35 manual strokes** of tamping rod OR
- Vibrate on table vibrator for **6–15 seconds** per layer
- Strike off top surface smoothly

**3. Curing:**
- Cover moulds to prevent moisture loss
- Demould after **24 ± 0.5 hours**
- Immerse cubes in **clean, fresh water at 27 ± 2°C** for the entire curing period
- Test ages: **3, 7, 14, 28, 56, 90 days** (28-day is standard acceptance age)

**4. Testing:**
- Remove cube from water, wipe off surface moisture
- **Test in saturated surface dry (SSD) condition**
- Place cube centrally between the CTM platens
- Apply load **uniformly and continuously** at rate of **140 kg/cm²/min** (≈ **13.7 N/mm²/min**)
- Record the **maximum failure load** in kN

**5. Computation:**
- Area A = **150 × 150 = 22,500 mm²**
- Compressive strength **f = Load / Area** in N/mm²
- **Average of 3 cubes** = test result for the sample

**6. Acceptance Criterion (IS 456, Clause 16):**
- Average of 3 cubes ≥ f_ck
- Individual cube strength ≥ f_ck − 3 N/mm²

**IS Code:** **IS 516:1959** (Reaffirmed 2018) — *Methods of Tests for Strength of Concrete*

> **Valuation Tip:** Each of the 5 numbered steps (Preparation, Casting, Curing, Testing, Computation) is worth ~1 mark. A labelled diagram of the cube in CTM is worth 1 mark bonus.

### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]:

**Definition of Characteristic Strength:**

> **As per IS 456:2000, Clause 2.1:** *"The characteristic strength of concrete is the value of the cube compressive strength below which **not more than 5%** of the test results are expected to fall."*

**Statistical Basis:** In a normal distribution of test results, the value at **μ − 1.65σ** corresponds to the 5th percentile.

**Part (b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Given Data**
- Target mean strength **f_cr = 33.25 N/mm²**
- Standard deviation **σ = 5 N/mm²**
- Risk factor **t = 1.65**

**Step 2: Calculate f_ck**

$$f_{ck} = f_{cr} - 1.65 \sigma$$

$$f_{ck} = 33.25 - 1.65 \times 5$$

$$f_{ck} = 33.25 - 8.25$$

$$f_{ck} = 25.00 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Identify Concrete Grade**

Since **f_ck = 25 N/mm²**, the concrete mix is of **M25 grade** as per **IS 456:2000** standard grading table.

**Step 4: Evaluate Test Results**

- Cube 1: **24.5 N/mm²**
- Cube 2: **25.5 N/mm²**
- Cube 3: **26.0 N/mm²**

**Step 5: Calculate Average Strength**

$$f_{avg} = \frac{24.5 + 25.5 + 26.0}{3} = \frac{76.0}{3} = 25.33 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 6: Acceptance Criterion Check (IS 456:2000, Clause 16.1)**

| Criterion | Requirement | Observed | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Average of 3 cubes | ≥ f_ck (25 N/mm²) | **25.33 N/mm²** | **PASS** ✓ |
| Individual minimum | ≥ f_ck − 3 (22 N/mm²) | **24.5 N/mm²** | **PASS** ✓ |

**Conclusion:** The mix **conforms to IS 456** for **M25 grade** acceptance.

**Step 7: Standard Deviation Verification (if needed):**
- Mean = 25.33
- Variance = [(24.5−25.33)² + (25.5−25.33)² + (26.0−25.33)²] / 3
- = [0.6889 + 0.0289 + 0.4489] / 3 = 1.1667 / 3 = 0.3889
- σ = √0.3889 = **0.62 N/mm²** (excellent control!)

> **Valuation Tip:** A common student error is to **forget the individual cube criterion (≥ f_ck − 3)**. Both criteria must be checked for full marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Top 5 Pitfalls on This Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing g/cm³ and kN/m³:** Density of concrete is **2400–2500 kg/m³** OR **24–25 kN/m³** — these are NOT the same number with different units. Students who write "2400 kN/m³" lose 1 mark.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up the Slump Test Mould Direction:** The **100 mm opening is at the TOP**, not the bottom. Many students draw the cone inverted and lose marks in the sketch.
> 
> 3. **Curing Temperature Omission:** IS 516 specifies **27 ± 2°C**. Writing only "27°C" or "room temperature" is incomplete and may lose 0.5 mark.
> 
> 4. **Wrong Application of Abrams' Law:** Students write "strength is proportional to w/c" — it is **INVERSELY** proportional. The exact form is exponential. This is a critical KTU error.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting IS Code in the Answer:** Every test answer in KTU MUST cite the relevant **IS code reference** (IS 516, IS 1199, IS 456, IS 383, IS 10262, IS 9103). Forgetting IS codes is a guaranteed mark deduction.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

### 🏗️ Constituent Materials (Recall in 4 Lines)
- **Cement** — Portland cement grade (33/43/53) is the binder; reacts with water
- **Water** — pH 6–8, clean, no organic impurities
- **Fine Aggregate** — Sand < 4.75 mm; zones I to IV; FM 2.6–3.2
- **Coarse Aggregate** — Gravel > 4.75 mm; nominal sizes 10/20/40 mm

### 🧪 Key IS Codes (Must-Memorize for KTU)
- **IS 269 / 8112 / 12269** — Cement specifications
- **IS 383** — Aggregates grading
- **IS 456:2000** — Plain & Reinforced Concrete Code
- **IS 516:1959** — Strength tests on hardened concrete
- **IS 1199:1959** — Fresh concrete tests (slump)
- **IS 10262:2019** — Concrete mix proportioning
- **IS 9103** — Chemical admixtures
- **IS 2386** — Aggregate tests (all parts)

### 📐 Critical Formulas (Lock These in Memory)
- **Abrams' Law:** f_c ∝ 1 / (w/c) (conceptually), exponential form in practice
- **E_c = 5000 √f_ck** (IS 456 modulus of elasticity)
- **f_cr = f_ck + 1.65 σ** (Target mean strength, IS 10262)
- **Compressive Strength = Failure Load / Cross-sectional Area**
- **Slump = Initial Mould Height − Subsided Concrete Height**

### 🔬 Tests on Fresh Concrete
- **Slump Test (IS 1199):** Most common; 4 layers × 25 rodding strokes
- **Compaction Factor Test:** More sensitive than slump for low-workability mixes
- **Vee-Bee Test:** Time-based, suitable for stiff mixes

### 🏛️ Tests on Hardened Concrete
- **Cube Compression Test (IS 516):** 150 mm cubes, 28-day cure, load rate 140 kg/cm²/min
- **Cylinder Split Tensile Test:** f_t = 2P / (πDL)
- **Flexural Strength Test:** Beam 100×100×500 mm, third-point loading
- **Non-Destructive Tests:** Rebound Hammer (Schmidt), Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity (UPV)

### 📊 Hardened Concrete Properties (5+5)
- **Strength Properties:** Compressive, tensile, flexural, shear, bond
- **Deformation Properties:** Shrinkage, creep, modulus of elasticity, Poisson's ratio (~0.2)

### 🎯 KTU Mnemonics
- **Cement Grades:** "**4-3-5-2**" — **OPC 43, OPC 53, PPC, PSC** (do not confuse with year '52)
- **Slump Cone Sizes:** "**1-2-3 × 10²**" — **100 mm top, 200 mm base, 300 mm height**
- **Slump Strokes:** "**25-4-5"** — **25 strokes, 4 layers, 5 to 10 sec lift**
- **Curing Rule:** "**28-27-2**" — **28 days, 27°C, ±2°C tolerance**
- **CTM Load Rate:** "**140 kg/cm²/min"** — **NOT N/mm²/min in KTU convention; convert mentally**

### ⚠️ Critical Engineering Distinctions
- **Workability ≠ Consistency** — Workability is broader
- **Strength of Cement ≠ Strength of Concrete** — Cement strength is binder; concrete strength is composite
- **Nominal size = Aggregate's smallest sieve through which it passes 90–100%**
- **Maximum size = Smallest sieve above which all particles are retained**
- **RCC strength is governed by weakest link** — paste-aggregate interface

### 🛠️ Mix Design Highlights
- For M20: w/c ≈ 0.50–0.55
- For M25: w/c ≈ 0.45–0.50
- For M30: w/c ≈ 0.40–0.45
- For M40 and above: Superplasticizer mandatory
- **Nominal mix ratios** (volumetric, IS 456):
  - M5 → 1:5:10
  - M7.5 → 1:4:8
  - M10 → 1:3:6
  - M15 → 1:2:4
  - M20 → 1:1.5:3

### ✅ One-Line Last-Minute Rules
- More water → more workability → LESS strength
- More cement → more strength → more heat of hydration
- More compaction → fewer voids → more strength
- Better curing → better hydration → more strength & durability
- Lower w/c → less permeability → more durability
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steel: Structural Steel Sections and Steel Reinforcements - Types and Uses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO MECHANICAL ENGINEERING & CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Steel: Structural Steel Sections and Steel Reinforcements - Types and Uses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Structural Steel - Formal Definition

**Structural steel** is a category of carbon steel used as a construction material, shaped into standardized cross-sectional profiles (called **structural sections**) to efficiently carry loads in buildings, bridges, towers, and industrial structures. These sections are manufactured by hot rolling steel billets through grooved rollers, which give them their characteristic shapes (I, C, L, T, hollow, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> Structural steel sections are standardized shapes that transfer applied loads (tension, compression, bending, shear) to the foundations. They are designed such that material is placed **as far away from the neutral axis as possible**, maximizing the second moment of area (moment of inertia) for a given weight of steel.

### 1.2 Steel Reinforcement - Formal Definition

**Steel reinforcement (rebar)** is deformed or plain steel bar embedded inside concrete to compensate for concrete's inherent weakness in **tensile strength**. Concrete is strong in compression (~20–40 N/mm²) but weak in tension (~2–5 N/mm²). Steel, being strong in tension (250–550 N/mm²), is placed in the tension zones of structural members (beams, slabs, columns) to form a composite material called **Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept - Composite Action:**
> When steel and concrete act together, the bond between them (enhanced by ribs/deformations on the rebar) allows them to behave as a single unit. The coefficient of thermal expansion of steel (~12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C) is nearly equal to that of concrete, preventing thermal incompatibility cracks.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy - The "Skeletal System" of a Structure

Think of a building as a **human body**:

- **Concrete (RCC members)** → The **muscles and flesh** (good in compression, fills the volume, cheap, fire-resistant).
- **Steel reinforcement (rebar)** → The **bones and tendons** (carry tension, hold the structure together when bending).
- **Structural steel sections (beams, columns, trusses)** → The **internal skeleton** of a high-rise building or industrial shed (pre-engineered steel buildings, airport hangars, stadium roofs).

Just as our skeleton provides the rigid framework and muscles add bulk, **structural steel provides rigidity** while **reinforcement within concrete provides tensile strength** to handle bending and pulling forces.

### 1.4 Key Physical Properties (To Be Remembered)

| Property | Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Density of steel (ρ) | **7850 kg/m³** (or 0.00785 g/mm³) |
| Young's Modulus of Elasticity (E) | **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²** (200 GPa) |
| Poisson's Ratio (ν) | **0.30** |
| Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (α) | **12 × 10⁻⁶ per °C** |
| Melting Point | ~1500 °C |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** The density value **7850 kg/m³** is the single most used constant in steel weight calculations. Memorize it as **0.00785 kg per cm³** or **7.85 g/cm³** for fast numerical solving.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Structural Steel Sections

Structural steel sections are broadly classified based on their **cross-sectional geometry**. The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) has standardized them for universal use.

#### A. Rolled Steel Sections (Hot-Rolled)

These are produced by passing hot steel billets through a sequence of grooved rollers. As per **IS 2062** (for material) and shape-specific standards, the common rolled sections are:

1. **I-Sections (or I-Beams)**
   - Designated as **ISMB / ISJB / ISLB / ISHB / ISWB**
   - Used as **beams, girders, columns, and purlins**
   - The "I" shape gives high moment of inertia in the major axis with minimum material

2. **Channel Sections (C-Sections)**
   - Designated as **ISMC / ISJC / ISLC**
   - Used as **chords of roof trusses, purlins, built-up columns, and crane girders**
   - Open cross-section; often used in pairs back-to-back for symmetry

3. **Angle Sections (L-Sections)**
   - **Equal angles** (e.g., ISA 50 × 50 × 6) and **Unequal angles** (e.g., ISA 75 × 50 × 6)
   - Used in **truss members (web/chord), bracing, tower legs, and built-up sections**

4. **T-Sections**
   - Cut from I-sections or hot-rolled as **ISST**
   - Used as **stiffeners, flange plates, and stringers in built-up girders**

5. **Rolled Plates, Sheets, Flats, and Bars**
   - **Plates**: Thickness > 5 mm; used in gusset plates, base plates
   - **Flats**: Rectangular, used as lacing bars, battens
   - **Round/Square Bars**: Used in bracing, hangers, small tension members

#### B. Hollow Steel Sections (HSS)

Manufactured by cold/hot forming and welding seamless or welded tubes, governed by **IS 1161** and **IS 4923**.

1. **Circular Hollow Sections (CHS)** - used in **towers, poles, scaffolding, and truss members** (excellent in compression, no weak axis, attractive appearance)
2. **Rectangular Hollow Sections (RHS)** - used in **columns, beams, frames** (high torsional rigidity, modern aesthetic)
3. **Square Hollow Sections (SHS)** - preferred for **architectural exposed columns, sign posts, and trusses**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Trend (PEB):** Pre-Engineered Buildings (PEB) predominantly use **hot-rolled H-sections** (ISMB/ISHB) for primary frames and **cold-formed sections** (Z, C, sigma profiles) for secondary members like purlins and girts.

---

### 2.2 Designation System - How to Read Rolled Steel Section Codes

Understanding the nomenclature is critical for KTU exams. Take **ISMB 500** as an example:

- **I** → Section type (I-shaped)
- **S** → Steel
- **MB** → Medium-weight Beam
- **500** → Depth of the section in **millimeters**

For an **angle**: **ISA 75 × 75 × 8** means **75 mm leg × 75 mm leg × 8 mm thick**.

For a **channel**: **ISMC 200** means **Medium-weight Channel of 200 mm depth**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why medium/light/heavy?** The same depth can have different flange widths, web thicknesses, and weight per meter. For instance, **ISMB 500** weighs ~86.9 kg/m, while **ISLB 500** (light) weighs ~75 kg/m, and **ISWB 500** (wide flange) weighs ~95.2 kg/m. The moment-carrying capacity differs accordingly.

---

### 2.3 Classification of Steel Reinforcement (Rebars)

Rebars are classified by **strength grade**, **manufacturing process**, and **surface condition**.

#### A. Based on Material Grade (Yield Strength in N/mm²)

| Grade | Yield Strength (N/mm²) | Standard | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fe 250** (Mild Steel) | 250 | IS 432 Part 1 | Small residential, lightly loaded structures |
| **Fe 415** (HYSD) | 415 | IS 1786 | General RCC construction (most common) |
| **Fe 500** (HYSD) | 500 | IS 1786 | Modern buildings, bridges (preferred today) |
| **Fe 550** (HYSD) | 550 | IS 1786 | Heavy infrastructure, seismic zones |
| **Fe 600** (Ultra-high) | 600 | IS 1786 | Special applications |

> **Notation:** "Fe" stands for **Ferrum (Iron)**, and the number denotes the **minimum yield stress in N/mm²** (MPa).

#### B. Based on Manufacturing Process

1. **Mild Steel (MS) Bars / Plain Bars** - Smooth surface, lower strength (Fe 250). Older construction, now largely discontinued in major projects due to poor bond with concrete.
2. **Hot Rolled Deformed (HYSD) Bars** - Ribs and lugs rolled onto the surface during hot rolling. Better bond.
3. **Cold Worked Steel Bars** - Cold twisted / cold drawn. Grades like Fe 415, Fe 500.
4. **Thermo-Mechanically Treated (TMT) Bars** - Modern, manufactured by quenching the red-hot bar in water after the final rolling pass. The outer surface becomes hard (tempered martensite) while the core remains ductile (ferrite-pearlite). **Best balance of strength, ductility, weldability, and earthquake resistance.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TMT vs HYSD - Exam Favourite Question:**
> - **HYSD bars** rely on **cold working** (twisting/drawing), which reduces ductility and makes them less weldable.
> - **TMT bars** use **thermal + mechanical** treatment, giving a strong outer martensite layer and a ductile ferrite-pearlite core. Hence TMT bars have **better seismic performance, higher fatigue resistance, and superior weldability**.

#### C. Special Reinforcement Types

- **CRS (Corrosion-Resistant Steel) Bars** - For coastal/marine structures
- **Epoxy-Coated Rebars** - Epoxy layer prevents chloride attack (used in bridges, parking decks)
- **Galvanized Rebars** - Zinc coating for corrosion protection
- **Stainless Steel Rebars** - Premium, used in critical structures (lifespan > 100 years)
- **GFRP / CFRP Bars** - Fibre-reinforced polymer bars (non-metallic, non-corrosive) for special needs

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Expression | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Weight per meter of a section | $W = \rho \times A = 0.00785 \times A$ | kg/m | Where A = cross-sectional area in **mm²** |
| 2 | Cross-sectional Area (given weight) | $A = \dfrac{W}{0.00785}$ | mm² | To find area from catalogue weight |
| 3 | Total Weight of a Bar (Length L) | $W_{total} = \dfrac{d^{2} \times L}{162}$ | kg | For round rebar of diameter **d (mm)** and length **L (m)** |
| 4 | Number of bars (n) for required area | $n = \dfrac{A_{st,req}}{\dfrac{\pi}{4} \times d^{2}}$ | — | Where A_st,req is total steel area required |
| 5 | Moment of Inertia (rectangle) | $I_{xx} = \dfrac{b d^{3}}{12}$ | mm⁴ | For rectangular section b × d |
| 6 | Section Modulus | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | mm³ | Maximum bending stress: σ = M / Z |
| 7 | Radius of Gyration | $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | mm | Slenderness check: λ = L_eff / r |
| 8 | Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}}$ | — | Column classification |
| 9 | Bending Stress | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | N/mm² | For beam design checks |
| 10 | Buckling Load (Euler) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{eff}^{2}}$ | N | Long slender columns |

> **Memory Trick for Weight Formula (Rebar):**
> $W = \dfrac{d^{2}}{162} \text{ kg/m}$
> Derived from: $A = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$ mm², $W = 0.00785 \times A = 0.00785 \times \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \approx \dfrac{d^{2}}{162}$ kg/m.

### 2.5 Why This System is Useful in Engineering

- **Standardization** allows engineers to design using published section properties without re-deriving.
- **Mass production** of standardized sections lowers cost.
- **Interchangeability** simplifies procurement and construction.
- **Code compliance** with IS standards ensures structural safety.
- The composite action of **RCC** (concrete + steel rebar) is the backbone of modern infrastructure - from residential slabs to metro rails to nuclear containment structures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Weight per Meter of a Steel Section

**Problem Setup:** Given any rolled steel section, we need a general expression to calculate the weight per running meter using its cross-sectional area.

**Step 1:** Recall the density of steel.
$$\rho_{steel} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^{3} = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^{3}$$

**Step 2:** Convert density into convenient engineering units. We want the weight (in kg) of a 1-meter length of section whose area is in **mm²**.

**Step 3:** For a section of area **A mm²** and length **1 m = 1000 mm**, the volume is:
$$V = A \times 1000 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

**Step 4:** Since 1 mm³ = 10⁻³ cm³, the volume in cm³ is:
$$V = A \times 1000 \times 10^{-3} = A \text{ cm}^{3}$$

**Step 5:** Mass = Density × Volume
$$m = 7.85 \times A \text{ g} = 0.00785 \times A \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6:** Final expression for weight per meter:
$$W = 0.00785 \times A \text{ kg/m}$$

> This formula is universally valid for any steel section - rebar, channel, angle, plate, etc., provided the area A is in **mm²**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Weight per Meter of a Circular Rebar (Memorize-Friendly Form)

**Step 1:** Area of a circular rebar of diameter **d**:
$$A = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Apply weight per meter formula:
$$W = 0.00785 \times \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \text{ kg/m}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute π ≈ 3.14159:
$$W = 0.00785 \times 0.7854 \times d^{2} = 0.006166 \times d^{2} \text{ kg/m}$$

**Step 4:** Approximate for ease of calculation (used in IS tables):
$$W \approx \dfrac{d^{2}}{162} \text{ kg/m}$$

> The constant 162 is the rounded value of (1 / 0.006166) ≈ 162.2. This simplification introduces an error of < 0.2%, which is acceptable in civil engineering calculations.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 1: Weight of a Channel Section

**Problem:** Find the total weight of an **ISMC 300** channel section of length **6 meters**. Given: Cross-sectional area = 4636 mm².

**Step 1:** Identify the given data.
- Area A = 4636 mm²
- Length L = 6 m

**Step 2:** Apply weight per meter formula.
$$W = 0.00785 \times A$$
$$W = 0.00785 \times 4636 = 36.39 \text{ kg/m}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate total weight for 6 m length.
$$W_{total} = 36.39 \times 6 = 218.34 \text{ kg}$$

> **Model Answer:** Total weight = **218.34 kg**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 2: Number of Rebars Required

**Problem:** A beam needs a total tensile steel area of **1200 mm²**. How many **16 mm diameter** rebars are required?

**Step 1:** Compute area of one 16 mm bar.
$$A_{bar} = \dfrac{\pi \times 16^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 256}{4} = 201.06 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the number of bars.
$$n = \dfrac{A_{st,req}}{A_{bar}} = \dfrac{1200}{201.06} = 5.97$$

**Step 3:** Round up to next whole number (you cannot have 0.97 of a bar).
$$n = 6 \text{ bars of 16 mm diameter}$$

> **Model Answer:** Provide **6 nos. of 16 mm ϕ bars**.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 3: Section Modulus of a Rectangular Timber-Steel Composite Beam

**Problem:** A rectangular steel plate 200 mm wide × 20 mm thick is to be used as a beam spanning 3 m. Calculate its section modulus, moment of inertia, and self-weight.

**Step 1:** Identify geometry. For a rectangle with width **b = 200 mm** and depth **d = 20 mm**.

**Step 2:** Calculate moment of inertia about the neutral axis (bending about the major axis, i.e., the horizontal axis through the centroid).
$$I_{xx} = \dfrac{b d^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{200 \times 20^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{200 \times 8000}{12} = 133333.33 \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate section modulus.
$$Z_{xx} = \dfrac{I_{xx}}{y_{max}} = \dfrac{I_{xx}}{d/2} = \dfrac{133333.33}{10} = 13333.33 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate cross-sectional area.
$$A = 200 \times 20 = 4000 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate self-weight per meter.
$$W = 0.00785 \times 4000 = 31.4 \text{ kg/m}$$

> **Interpretation:** The plate is very thin in the direction of bending (only 20 mm), so the moment of inertia and section modulus are small. This is why beams are designed with most material far from the neutral axis (e.g., I-sections have flanges at top and bottom).

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix - When to Use Which Section

| Engineering Requirement | Preferred Steel Section | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Long-span roof beam (Bending dominant) | **I-Section (ISMB/ISHB)** | High $I_{xx}$ with minimum self-weight |
| Compression column in a multi-story building | **H-Section (ISHB) or SHS/RHS** | Equal strength in both axes; no weak axis buckling |
| Top chord of a roof truss | **Channel (ISMC) or Double Angle** | Easy connection of web members between flanges |
| Lacing/Battening of a built-up column | **Flat / Angle** | Slender secondary member, easy to bolt |
| Industrial floor beams carrying moving loads | **I-Section (ISMB) with cover plates** | High fatigue resistance, easy to add plates |
| Architectural exposed columns in showrooms | **Circular Hollow Section (CHS)** | Aesthetic, no flat surfaces to collect dust |
| Tower for power transmission | **Angle (ISA) or CHS** | Wind resistance, light weight, easy transportation |
| Foundation bolt/anchor | **Plain round bar (M.S.)** | Ductile, easy to bend into L or J shape |

---

### 3.7 Comparative Table - Reinforcement Grades (For KTU Theory Answers)

| Property | Fe 250 (M.S.) | Fe 415 | Fe 500 | Fe 550 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Yield Strength (N/mm²) | 250 | 415 | 500 | 550 |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength | 410 | 485 | 545 | 585 |
| % Elongation (Ductility) | 23% | 14.5% | 12% | 10% |
| Bond with Concrete | Poor (plain) | Good (deformed) | Good | Good |
| Weldability | Excellent | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate |
| Seismic Suitability | Low | Moderate | **High** | High |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| Common Use | Old houses | General RCC | **Today's standard** | Bridges/heavy infra |
