## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A QUESTIONS (Each 3 Marks)

#### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]**
**(a)** Differentiate between **HYSD bars** and **TMT bars** in terms of manufacturing process and properties. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | HYSD Bars | TMT Bars |
|---|---|---|
| **Manufacturing** | Hot rolled and then **cold twisted** to deform the surface and increase strength | Hot rolled and then **quenched in water** for a controlled time to form a tempered martensite outer layer |
| **Strength Mechanism** | Strain hardening due to cold work | Thermal + mechanical treatment; hard outer + ductile core |
| **Ductility** | Lower (cold work reduces ductility) | Higher (core remains ductile ferrite-pearlite) |
| **Weldability** | Difficult (residual stresses from cold work) | Excellent (no cold work, uniform microstructure) |
| **Seismic Resistance** | Moderate | **High** - preferred in earthquake zones |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Slightly higher but worth the performance |

---

#### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]**
**(b)** Briefly explain the use of **Channel sections** and **Angle sections** in steel structures with examples. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

- **Channel Sections (ISMC):** These C-shaped sections are used as **purlins in roof trusses** to support roofing sheets, as **chords of trusses**, as **components of built-up columns** (placed back-to-back to form a box-like section), and as **crane girders** in industrial buildings. Their open section makes them easy to connect to gusset plates using bolts or welds.

- **Angle Sections (ISA):** Available as **equal angles** (both legs equal) and **unequal angles**, these L-shaped sections are used as **web members and chord members of roof trusses**, in **bracing systems** (cross bracing in roof and wall planes), as **tower legs in transmission towers**, and in **lacing/battening** of built-up columns. Their versatility and ease of connection make them the most widely used rolled section.

---

### PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each - Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Students must answer ONE question out of the two internal choices.

---

### **Question A. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Classify the various **structural steel sections** used in construction. Explain any **four types** in detail with their designation, cross-section sketch (in words), and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Broad Classification**
Structural steel sections are classified as:
1. Rolled Steel Sections (IS 2062)
2. Hollow Steel Sections (IS 1161, IS 4923)
3. Built-up Sections
4. Cold-Formed Sections

**Step 2: Detailed Explanation of Four Types**

**1. I-Sections (ISMB/ISLB/ISHB)**
- **Cross-Section:** Two horizontal flanges connected by a vertical web. Shape resembles the capital letter "I".
- **Designation Example:** ISMB 500 (depth 500 mm, medium weight beam)
- **Why This Shape:** Most material is placed far from the neutral axis → maximum moment of inertia with minimum weight.
- **Applications:** Main beams, girders, columns, crane girders, bridge girders.
- **Standard Reference:** IS 808 (Dimensions of Hot Rolled Steel Sections)

**2. Channel Sections (ISMC/ISLC)**
- **Cross-Section:** Two flanges with a single web, forming a "C" shape. Open section.
- **Designation Example:** ISMC 200 (depth 200 mm, medium channel)
- **Property:** Strong axis is the major axis (x-x); weak in the minor axis (y-y). Often used in pairs to overcome asymmetry.
- **Applications:** Purlins, chords of trusses, built-up columns, vehicle chassis.

**3. Angle Sections (ISA)**
- **Cross-Section:** Two legs meeting at 90°, available in equal and unequal leg sizes.
- **Designation Example:** ISA 75 × 75 × 6 (75 mm leg × 75 mm leg × 6 mm thick)
- **Applications:** Truss members, bracing, tower legs, lacing bars.
- **Key Advantage:** Easy to connect along two perpendicular faces.

**4. Hollow Steel Sections (SHS/RHS/CHS)**
- **Cross-Section:** Square, rectangular, or circular closed hollow tube.
- **Designation Example:** SHS 100 × 100 × 5 mm
- **Property:** Closed section → high **torsional rigidity**, no weak axis, excellent buckling resistance.
- **Applications:** Architectural columns, sign posts, trusses in modern glass-facade buildings, offshore structures.

---

**(b)** A **steel column** is made of **2 ISMC 300 channels placed back-to-back with a 200 mm gap** between them. Calculate the **radius of gyration** of the built-up section about the **x-x axis (perpendicular to the plane of the back-to-back channels)**. Given for each ISMC 300: Area A₁ = 4636 mm², Moment of Inertia I_{xx1} = 6047.9 × 10⁴ mm⁴, Depth h = 300 mm. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Understand the configuration.**
Two channels are placed back-to-back with 200 mm clear gap. Total depth of built-up section D = 300 + 200 + 300 = **800 mm** (if the 200 mm is measured as the gap between the outermost webs).

**Step 2: Compute the moment of inertia of the built-up section about the x-x axis.**

For back-to-back channels about the axis perpendicular to the plane of the channels (i.e., the x-x axis passing through the centroid of each channel itself), the moment of inertia of each channel is **simply I_{xx1}** because the channel's own centroidal axis x-x lies along this direction. There is **no parallel axis shift** in this direction.

$$I_{xx,built} = 2 \times I_{xx1} = 2 \times 6047.9 \times 10^{4} = 12095.8 \times 10^{4} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 3: Compute total area of built-up section.**
$$A_{total} = 2 \times A_{1} = 2 \times 4636 = 9272 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 4: Compute radius of gyration.**
$$r_{xx} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{xx,built}}{A_{total}}}$$
$$r_{xx} = \sqrt{\dfrac{12095.8 \times 10^{4}}{9272}} = \sqrt{130453.84} \approx 361.18 \text{ mm}$$

> **Model Answer:** $r_{xx} \approx 361.18$ mm

---

### **Question B. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 - Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** What are the **different grades of steel reinforcement** used in RCC construction? Compare the **physical and mechanical properties** of **Fe 415 and Fe 500** bars. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: List of Grades (as per IS 1786):**
- **Fe 250** (Mild Steel) - now deprecated
- **Fe 415** (HYSD)
- **Fe 500** (HYSD / TMT)
- **Fe 550** (TMT)
- **Fe 600** (Ultra-high strength TMT)

**Step 2: Comparison Table for Fe 415 vs Fe 500**

| Property | Fe 415 | Fe 500 |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum Yield Strength (N/mm²) | 415 | 500 |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength (N/mm²) | 485 | 545 |
| Minimum Elongation (%) | 14.5 | 12 |
| Bond with Concrete | Good | Better (deeper ribs permitted) |
| Ductility | Higher | Slightly Lower |
| Steel Quantity Required | ~10-12% more than Fe 500 | Less steel → economical |
| Seismic Performance | Good | Better (higher energy absorption) |
| Cost per kg | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Availability | Common | Most commonly specified today |

**Step 3: Selection Guidelines.**
- **Fe 500** is the current default choice for new construction due to its higher strength, leading to **~15-20% reduction in steel quantity** and hence cost savings.
- **Fe 415** is preferred in **earthquake-prone areas** where higher ductility is critical for energy dissipation.
- For heavily loaded structures (bridges, multi-story columns), **Fe 550** is used.

---

**(b)** A simply supported beam of **span 5 m** carries a **uniformly distributed load of 25 kN/m** (including self-weight). Design the **tensile reinforcement** using **Fe 500 grade steel** and **M25 grade concrete**. The cross-section of the beam is **250 mm wide × 500 mm deep**, with effective cover = 50 mm. Adopt M25 = f_ck = 25 N/mm², Fe 500 = f_y = 500 N/mm². **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute design moment.**
$$M = \dfrac{w L^{2}}{8} = \dfrac{25 \times 5^{2}}{8} = 78.125 \text{ kN·m} = 78.125 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Compute effective depth.**
$$d = 500 - 50 = 450 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Compute the limiting moment of resistance of the section for Fe 500.**
For Fe 500, IS 456 limits $x_{u,max}/d = 0.46$. The limiting moment is:
$$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times f_{ck} \times b \times d^{2} = 0.138 \times 25 \times 250 \times 450^{2}$$
$$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 25 \times 250 \times 202500 = 174,656,250 \text{ N·mm} \approx 174.66 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 4: Check if section is singly or doubly reinforced.**
Since the applied moment M = 78.125 kN·m < M_{u,lim} = 174.66 kN·m, the section is **singly reinforced** (under-reinforced).

**Step 5: Apply the design equation for singly reinforced section.**
Using IS 456:
$$M_{u} = 0.87 \times f_{y} \times A_{st} \times d \times \left(1 - \dfrac{A_{st} \times f_{y}}{b \times d \times f_{ck}}\right)$$

Let $p = \dfrac{A_{st}}{b \times d}$. Then:
$$M_{u} = 0.87 \times f_{y} \times p \times b \times d^{2} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{p \times f_{y}}{f_{ck}}\right)$$

Substituting values and solving (using iterative or tabular approach):
$$78.125 \times 10^{6} = 0.87 \times 500 \times A_{st} \times 450 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{500 \times A_{st}}{250 \times 450 \times 25}\right)$$

After solving (using p ≈ 0.0022):
$$A_{st} \approx 248 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 6: Provide reinforcement.**
- Choose **16 mm diameter bars.** Area of one bar = 201 mm².
- Number of bars: $n = 248 / 201 = 1.23 \rightarrow$ round up to **2 nos. of 16 mm ϕ bars.**
- Provided area A_{st,provided} = 2 × 201 = **402 mm²**.

> **Model Answer:** Provide **2 nos. of 16 mm ϕ Fe 500 bars** at the bottom of the beam. Spacing and anchorage length should be checked as per IS 456 (not included in this calculation).

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Wrong Density Value:** Some students use 785 kg/m³ instead of **7850 kg/m³**. This makes the weight calculation wrong by a factor of 10. Always check the units.
>
> 2. **Confusing Section Designations:** Writing "ISMB 500" when meaning "ISMC 500" - I, C, and T are different. Use the correct section code in the answer.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Factor 0.87 in Design:** The 0.87 factor in the flexural equation $M_u = 0.87 f_y A_{st} d$ accounts for partial safety factor. Omitting it loses 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Rounding Down the Number of Bars:** You cannot provide 1.6 bars - always **round UP** to the next integer. Providing 1 bar instead of 2 is a structural safety error.
>
> 5. **Mixing TMT with HYSD:** TMT and HYSD are not the same. TMT = Thermo-Mechanically Treated; HYSD = High Yield Strength Deformed (cold-worked). Use the correct term.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to Mention IS Codes:** Always cite **IS 2062** for structural steel and **IS 1786** for rebar. Examiners award marks for standard references.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Steel in construction is used in two distinct ways:** (1) as **structural steel sections** (I, C, L, T, hollow, plates) for load-bearing frameworks, and (2) as **reinforcement bars** embedded in concrete for RCC members.

- **The "weight per meter" formula** $W = 0.00785 \times A$ (A in mm²) is universal for any steel section. For round rebar, the convenient shortcut is $W = d^{2} / 162$ kg/m.

- **TMT bars** are the modern industry standard - they combine high strength with ductility and excellent weldability, making them ideal for earthquake-resistant design.

- **I-sections** are the workhorse of beam design because they maximize the moment of inertia with minimum self-weight - material is concentrated at the flanges, far from the neutral axis.

- **Hollow steel sections (CHS, SHS, RHS)** offer superior torsional rigidity and aesthetic appeal - preferred in modern architectural applications.

- **Key grades to remember:** Fe 415 (older standard), Fe 500 (current default), Fe 550 (heavy infrastructure), Fe 600 (special applications). Higher grade → less steel quantity → more economical design.

- **Standard IS references to memorize:** IS 2062 (structural steel material), IS 808 (rolled section dimensions), IS 1786 (HYSD/TMT bars), IS 432 (mild steel bars), IS 1161 (CHS tubes), IS 4923 (RHS/SHS sections).

- **The density of steel is 7850 kg/m³ (or 0.00785 g/mm³)** - this is your most-used constant.

- **Always round UP the number of bars** - never round down. Structural safety is non-negotiable.

- **For built-up sections**, the moment of inertia about the axis that passes through the centroids of the individual components (without parallel axis shift) is simply the sum of the individual moments of inertia.

- **Composite action of RCC** is the key to modern construction: concrete resists compression, steel resists tension, and the bond between them (provided by the ribs on deformed bars) ensures they work as a single unit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soil: Origin of Soil, Weathering of Rocks, and Types of Weathering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST104 - INTRODUCTION TO MECHANICAL ENGINEERING & CIVIL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Soil: Origin of Soil, Weathering of Rocks, and Types of Weathering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Soil? — The Engineering Definition

From a **Civil Engineering** perspective (KTU 2024 terminology), **soil** is defined as the unconsolidated material lying above the bedrock on the Earth's surface, formed through the prolonged **weathering of parent rocks** under the combined action of physical, chemical, and biological agencies, mixed with organic matter, water, and air.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"Soil is the thin uppermost layer of the earth's crust, composed of mineral particles derived from the physical and chemical weathering of rocks, along with organic matter, water, and air, capable of supporting plant life and sustaining civil engineering structures."*

### 1.2 Origin of Soil — The Genesis Story

Soil does not appear overnight. It is the result of a continuous geological cycle:

1. **Parent Rock** (Bedrock) forms deep within the Earth.
2. Rocks are exposed to the atmosphere through tectonic uplift, volcanic activity, or erosion of overlying strata.
3. **Weathering processes** begin acting on exposed rock surfaces.
4. Over thousands to millions of years, rocks disintegrate into smaller particles.
5. Organic matter from decomposed plants and animals mixes with mineral particles.
6. **Pedogenesis** (soil formation) occurs, creating distinct **soil horizons (layers)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geological Time Scale for Soil Formation:**
> - **1 cm of topsoil** takes approximately **100 to 1000 years** to form.
> - Full agricultural soil profile (1 m) may take **10,000 to 500,000 years**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ice Cube Melting" Analogy

Imagine placing a solid ice cube (rock) on a kitchen counter (Earth's surface). Over time, it melts (weathering) into water (fine particles) and evaporates (chemical transformation). The leftover tiny droplets represent soil particles. Just as the ice cube takes time to disappear and leaves residues, rocks take geological time to break down into soil, with different "melting" mechanisms producing different particle sizes and mineral compositions.

### 1.4 The Tripartite Composition of Soil (Phase Diagram)

Soil is a **three-phase system**:

| Phase | Component | Typical % by Volume |
|-------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Solid** | Mineral grains + Organic matter | ~50% |
| **Liquid** | Water (pore water) | ~25–30% |
| **Gas** | Air (pore air) | ~20–25% |

> [!NOTE]
> The relative proportions of these three phases determine the engineering properties such as **shear strength**, **compressibility**, and **permeability**.

### 1.5 What is Weathering?

**Weathering** is the in-situ breakdown and alteration of rocks and minerals at or near the Earth's surface, caused by exposure to atmospheric conditions, water, temperature variations, and living organisms, **without** significant transportation of the weathered material.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Do NOT confuse **weathering** with **erosion**.
> - **Weathering** = Breakdown *in place* (no movement).
> - **Erosion** = Transport of weathered material by wind, water, ice, or gravity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Fundamental Types of Weathering

#### 2.1.1 Physical (Mechanical) Weathering
Disintegration of rocks into smaller fragments **without any change in chemical composition**.

**Mechanisms:**
- **Thermal Expansion & Contraction** — Diurnal temperature variations cause differential expansion of minerals (e.g., dark basalt vs. light quartz), creating stresses along grain boundaries.
- **Frost Action (Freeze-Thaw)** — Water enters cracks → freezes (expands by ~9%) → exerts pressure up to **207 MPa** at -22°C → cracks propagate.
- **Exfoliation (Unloading)** — Removal of overlying rock layers reduces confining pressure → outer layers spall off in sheets (e.g., granite domes).
- **Abrasion** — Wind-borne or water-borne particles grind against rock surfaces.
- **Crystal Growth / Salt Weathering** — Salt crystals grow in pores → exert crystallization pressure.
- **Hydraulic Action** — Force of moving water dislodges rock fragments.

#### 2.1.2 Chemical Weathering
Decomposition of rocks through **chemical reactions** that alter mineralogical composition.

**Key Chemical Processes:**

1. **Oxidation** — Reaction of minerals with atmospheric oxygen.
   - Example: $4\text{Fe} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ (Rusting of iron-bearing minerals)

2. **Hydration** — Addition of water molecules to mineral structure.
   - Example: $\text{CaSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (Anhydrite → Gypsum)

3. **Hydrolysis** — Reaction of minerals with water (H⁺ and OH⁻ ions).
   - Example: $2\text{KAlSi}_3\text{O}_8 + 2\text{H}^+ + 9\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{Si}_2\text{O}_5(\text{OH})_4 + 4\text{H}_4\text{SiO}_4 + 2\text{K}^+$
   - (Orthoclase Feldspar → Kaolinite clay)

4. **Carbonation** — Reaction with carbonic acid (H₂CO₃) from dissolved CO₂.
   - Example: $\text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Ca}(\text{HCO}_3)_2$ (Limestone dissolution)

5. **Solution** — Direct dissolution of soluble minerals (e.g., rock salt, gypsum).

6. **Chelation** — Organic acids form complexes with metal ions.

#### 2.1.3 Biological Weathering
Disintegration and decomposition caused by **living organisms**.

**Agents:**
- **Root Wedging** — Plant roots grow into cracks and exert pressure (up to **1.5 MPa**).
- **Burrowing Animals** — Earthworms, ants, rabbits, termites mix and break soil.
- **Microbial Action** — Bacteria and fungi secrete organic acids that dissolve minerals.
- **Lichens & Mosses** — Secrete weak acids that etch rock surfaces.

### 2.2 Factors Influencing Weathering

| Factor | Effect on Weathering |
|--------|---------------------|
| **Climate** (Temperature + Rainfall) | Hot & humid = intense chemical weathering; Cold & dry = dominant physical weathering |
| **Rock Type** | Limestone weathers chemically; Granite weathers physically; Basalt weathers to clay |
| **Vegetation Cover** | More vegetation = more biological & chemical weathering |
| **Topography** | Steep slopes = more physical weathering (exposure); Flat = more chemical |
| **Time** | Longer exposure = greater weathering depth |
| **Human Activity** | Mining, construction accelerate weathering |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since this topic is largely **conceptual and descriptive** (geological/geo-morphological), the "formulas" are primarily key reaction equations, physical constants, and quantitative metrics that frequently appear in KTU theory examinations.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Significance / Application |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Volume expansion of water on freezing | $\Delta V / V \approx 9\%$ | Drives frost wedging pressure |
| 2 | Frost wedging pressure | $P \approx 207 \text{ MPa}$ at $-22°\text{C}$ | Explains crack propagation |
| 3 | Soil formation rate (topsoil) | $\approx 0.01 \text{ to } 0.1 \text{ mm/year}$ | Geological time scale |
| 4 | Root wedging pressure | $P \approx 1.0 \text{ to } 1.5 \text{ MPa}$ | Biological weathering force |
| 5 | Oxidation reaction (general) | $\text{Metal} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Oxide}$ | Rust formation in soils |
| 6 | Hydrolysis (Feldspar to Clay) | $\text{K-feldspar} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Kaolinite} + \text{K}^+ + \text{SiO}_2$ | Primary clay mineral source |
| 7 | Carbonation (Limestone) | $\text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Ca}(\text{HCO}_3)_2$ | Karst topography formation |
| 8 | Soil void ratio | $e = V_v / V_s$ | Quantifies pore space |
| 9 | Soil porosity | $n = V_v / V_t \times 100\%$ | % of void space in soil |
| 10 | Degree of saturation | $S = V_w / V_v$ | Water content in voids |

### 2.4 Engineering Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Civil Engineers Study Weathering:**
> - Determines **bearing capacity** of foundation soils.
> - Influences **slope stability** (residual soils on hills).
> - Affects **tunnel and excavation design** (weathered rock classification).
> - Critical for **dam site selection** and **reservoir leakage assessment**.
> - Governs **laterite, bauxite, and clay mineral deposits** used in construction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Analysis of the Rock-to-Soil Transformation (Pedogenesis)

The transformation from a massive parent rock body to a mature soil profile follows a logical, sequential engineering-geological pathway. We will derive each step:

**Step 1: Parent Rock Exposure**
Initially, the parent rock is buried deep beneath the Earth's crust under high lithostatic pressure. Tectonic forces (mountain building) or erosion of overlying layers causes the rock to approach the surface.

$$
\sigma_{\text{lithostatic}} = \rho_{\text{rock}} \cdot g \cdot h
$$

where $\sigma_{\text{lithostatic}}$ is the overburden pressure (Pa), $\rho_{\text{rock}}$ is rock density (~2700 kg/m³), $g$ is gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**), and $h$ is depth (m).

**Step 2: Stress Relief (Exfoliation Begins)**
As the overlying material erodes, the confining pressure decreases. The outer rock layers, no longer compressed, expand outward and fracture parallel to the surface. This is **exfoliation**.

$$
\Delta P = P_{\text{original}} - P_{\text{eroded}}
$$

**Step 3: Physical Weathering Dominates Initial Stages**
Temperature fluctuations, freeze-thaw cycles, and abrasion create **mechanical cracks** and fragment the rock. The volume of the rock mass increases as joint density rises (creating void space).

$$
V_{\text{fragmented}} = V_{\text{parent}} + \Delta V_{\text{cracks}}
$$

**Step 4: Chemical Weathering Modifies Mineralogy**
Water, oxygen, and carbon dioxide penetrate the new cracks. Reactive minerals (feldspars, micas, carbonates) decompose into clay minerals and oxides.

For orthoclase feldspar undergoing hydrolysis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\text{KAlSi}_3\text{O}_8 + 2\text{H}^+ + 9\text{H}_2\text{O} &\rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{Si}_2\text{O}_5(\text{OH})_4 \\
&\quad + 4\text{H}_4\text{SiO}_4 + 2\text{K}^+
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The H⁺ ions (from rainwater & dissolved CO₂) attack the K-feldspar structure, displacing K⁺ ions (leached away), releasing silica (H₄SiO₄), and forming kaolinite clay.

**Step 5: Biological Colonization**
Mosses, lichens, and pioneer plants colonize the disintegrated rock. Their roots penetrate deeper cracks. Organic acids accelerate chemical reactions. Decomposing vegetation adds **humus** to the mineral mixture.

**Step 6: Pedogenesis and Horizon Development**
A mature soil profile develops distinct **horizons**:

$$
\text{Profile} = \text{O} + \text{A} + \text{E} + \text{B} + \text{C} + \text{R}
$$

where:
- **O** = Organic (humus, leaf litter)
- **A** = Topsoil (dark, organic-rich, leached)
- **E** = Eluviation zone (clay & oxides washed out)
- **B** = Subsoil (accumulation of clay, iron, aluminum — illuviation)
- **C** = Weathered parent rock (partially disintegrated)
- **R** = Bedrock (unweathered parent rock)

**Step 7: Soil Maturity**
The soil is now classified based on its development stage, mineralogy, organic content, and horizon distinctness.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Study of Weathering Types

| Parameter | Physical Weathering | Chemical Weathering | Biological Weathering |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Dominant Agent** | Temperature, water, wind, ice | Water, O₂, CO₂, acids | Plants, animals, microbes |
| **Process Type** | Disintegration | Decomposition | Both disintegration & decomposition |
| **Chemical Change** | **None** (only size reduction) | **Major** (new minerals formed) | Minor to moderate |
| **Typical Climates** | Cold, arid, high altitude | Hot, humid, tropical | Any climate with life |
| **End Products** | Rock fragments, sand, silt | Clay minerals, oxides, dissolved ions | Organic-mineral complexes |
| **Rate of Action** | Moderate to fast (instant on geological time) | Slow but continuous | Variable |
| **Volumetric Change** | Increases (voids created) | Can increase or decrease | Increases |
| **Example Process** | Frost wedging in Himalayas | Limestone karst in Western Ghats | Root action in forests |
| **Engineering Concern** | Talus slopes, scree deposits | Sinkholes, collapsible soils | Organic peat soils |
| **Effect on Foundations** | Uneven settlements, boulder obstructions | Cavities, weak clay layers | Highly compressible organic layers |

### 3.3 Quantitative Estimation — Time Required for Soil Formation

Theoretical rate of soil formation can be estimated using:

$$
T = \frac{D}{R_w}
$$

where $T$ = time to form soil profile (years), $D$ = desired soil depth (mm), $R_w$ = weathering rate (mm/year).

**Example Calculation:**
For a soil profile depth $D = 1000 \text{ mm}$ and average weathering rate $R_w = 0.05 \text{ mm/year}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{1000 \text{ mm}}{0.05 \text{ mm/year}} \\
&= 20,000 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why soil is considered a **non-renewable resource** on human timescales and must be conserved carefully in civil engineering projects (especially in highway and dam construction).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Soil Formation Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PARENT ROCK BEDROCK] --> B[Tectonic Uplift / Erosion of Overburden]
    B --> C[Rock Exposure to Atmosphere]
    C --> D[WEATHERING PHASE]
    D --> D1[Physical Weathering]
    D --> D2[Chemical Weathering]
    D --> D3[Biological Weathering]
    D1 --> E[Disintegrated Rock Fragments]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Addition of Organic Matter Humus]
    F --> G[Pedogenesis Soil Profile Development]
    G --> H1[O Horizon Organic Litter]
    G --> H2[A Horizon Topsoil]
    G --> H3[E Horizon Eluviation]
    G --> H4[B Horizon Subsoil]
    G --> H5[C Horizon Weathered Rock]
    G --> H6[R Horizon Bedrock]
    H1 --> I[MATURE SOIL PROFILE]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> I
    H6 --> I
```

### 4.2 Architecture Flow: Types and Sub-Processes of Weathering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[WEATHERING OF ROCKS] --> P[PHYSICAL WEATHERING]
    W --> CH[CHEMICAL WEATHERING]
    W --> BI[BIOLOGICAL WEATHERING]
    P --> P1[Thermal Expansion Contraction]
    P --> P2[Frost Action Freeze Thaw]
    P --> P3[Exfoliation Unloading]
    P --> P4[Abrasion Wind Water]
    P --> P5[Salt Crystal Growth]
    P --> P6[Hydraulic Action]
    CH --> C1[Oxidation]
    CH --> C2[Hydration]
    CH --> C3[Hydrolysis]
    CH --> C4[Carbonation]
    CH --> C5[Solution Dissolution]
    CH --> C6[Chelation]
    BI --> B1[Root Wedging]
    BI --> B2[Burrowing Organisms]
    BI --> B3[Microbial Acid Secretion]
    BI --> B4[Lichens and Mosses]
    P --> R[REGOLITH Weathered Material]
    CH --> R
    BI --> R
    R --> S[SOIL FORMATION]
```

### 4.3 Cross-Sectional Block: Soil Profile Horizons

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Surface Vegetation and Litter] --> L1
    L1[O HORIZON Organic Layer Dark Brown Humus Rich] --> L2
    L2[A HORIZON Topsoil Mixed Mineral Organic Dark] --> L3
    L3[E HORIZON Eluviation Leached Light Colored] --> L4
    L4[B HORIZON Subsoil Accumulation of Clay Iron Reddish Brown] --> L5
    L5[C HORIZON Weathered Parent Rock Fragments] --> L6
    L6[R HORIZON Unweathered Bedrock Massive Rock]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Diagram Limitations:** The soil profile is fundamentally a *vertical, layered physical structure*. Since Mermaid cannot render true stratigraphic cross-sections with depth scaling, the above block-level architecture flow is used to convey the sequential vertical arrangement of horizons from the surface downward to the bedrock.

### 4.4 Interaction Flow: Climate-Rock-Vegetation Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CL[CLIMATE Temperature Rainfall] --> W[Weathering Intensity]
    RK[ROCK TYPE Mineralogy] --> W
    VG[VEGETATION COVER] --> W
    W --> SP[Soil Profile Characteristics]
    SP --> ENG[Engineering Properties Bearing Capacity Permeability]
    ENG --> DS[DESIGN OF FOUNDATIONS AND STRUCTURES]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]**
**Define soil. List the three phases that constitute a soil mass.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Soil is the thin, unconsolidated uppermost layer of the Earth's crust, formed by the prolonged physical, chemical, and biological weathering of parent rocks, mixed with organic matter, water, and air, and capable of supporting plant life and civil engineering structures.

**Three Phases (1 Mark):**
1. **Solid phase** — Mineral grains and organic matter.
2. **Liquid phase** — Pore water (with dissolved salts).
3. **Gaseous phase** — Pore air (with CO₂, water vapor, O₂).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]**
**Differentiate between weathering and erosion.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Weathering | Erosion |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| **Definition** | In-situ breakdown of rocks | Removal and transport of weathered material |
| **Movement** | No significant movement of material | Material is transported by agents |
| **Agents** | Temperature, water, organisms | Wind, water, ice, gravity |
| **Process Type** | Disintegration + decomposition | Transportation + deposition |
| **Example** | Feldspar converting to clay | River carrying sand to the sea |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often use *erosion* and *weathering* interchangeably. Marks are deducted if you do not clearly state that **weathering occurs in-situ**, while **erosion involves transportation**.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**(a) Explain the origin of soil. Describe the role of parent rock and time in soil formation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Origin of Soil — Detailed Explanation:**

Soil originates from the **weathering of parent rocks** (also called **bedrock** or **C/R horizon material**). The process is part of the **rock cycle** and is influenced by several factors collectively termed *CLORPT* (Climate, Organisms, Relief/Topography, Parent material, Time) — a model proposed by soil scientist **Hans Jenny** in 1941.

**Role of Parent Rock (3 Marks):**
- The parent rock determines the **mineralogical composition** of the resulting soil.
- Example: Weathering of **granite** (rich in quartz, feldspar, mica) produces sandy, silty residual soils.
- Weathering of **basalt** (rich in iron, magnesium) produces clayey soils (laterites, black cotton soils).
- **Limestone** weathering produces thin, calcareous residual soils (terra rossa).
- The **colour, texture, and chemical properties** of the soil are inherited from the parent rock.

**Role of Time (2 Marks):**
- Soil formation is an extremely slow geological process.
- Approximately **0.01 to 0.1 mm of soil** is formed per year.
- A 1 m thick mature soil profile requires **10,000 to 500,000 years**.
- Young soils (< 1000 years) resemble parent rock material closely.
- Old, mature soils (millions of years) have well-developed horizons and may bear little resemblance to the parent rock.

**Geological Context (2 Marks):**
- Tectonic uplift exposes parent rocks to the atmosphere.
- Once exposed, weathering commences and progresses through **physical → chemical → biological** stages.
- Over geological time, soil horizons (O, A, E, B, C, R) develop in sequence.

---

**(b) Describe the three main types of weathering of rocks with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Physical (Mechanical) Weathering (2.5 Marks):**
Disintegration of rocks into smaller fragments **without any chemical change**.

- **Thermal expansion/contraction:** Diurnal temperature variations cause differential expansion of mineral grains, leading to granular disintegration (e.g., desert boulders crumbling into sand).
- **Frost action:** Water in cracks freezes and expands by ~9%, exerting pressure up to 207 MPa, prying rocks apart (e.g., talus slopes in the Himalayas).
- **Exfoliation:** Reduction of overburden pressure causes outer rock layers to spall off in curved sheets (e.g., granite domes in Joshua Tree National Park).
- **Abrasion:** Wind-borne sand particles polish rock surfaces (e.g., ventifacts in the Thar Desert).

**2. Chemical Weathering (2.5 Marks):**
Decomposition of rocks through chemical reactions that form **new minerals** (mainly clays and oxides).

- **Oxidation:** $4\text{Fe} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ — causes yellow-brown staining in lateritic soils.
- **Hydrolysis:** Orthoclase feldspar + water → Kaolinite clay + K⁺ ions — primary source of clay minerals in tropical soils.
- **Carbonation:** $\text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Ca}(\text{HCO}_3)_2$ — dissolves limestone, forming **karst topography** (caves, sinkholes in the Western Ghats).
- **Hydration:** Anhydrite + water → Gypsum — causes volume expansion and rock breakdown.
- **Solution:** Rock salt (halite) and gypsum dissolve directly in water.

**3. Biological Weathering (2 Marks):**
Caused by living organisms.

- **Root wedging:** Plant roots grow into rock joints; pressures up to 1.5 MPa widen cracks (e.g., trees splitting pavements and retaining walls).
- **Burrowing animals:** Earthworms, ants, termites break up and mix soil/rock (Darwin's "ploughman" theory).
- **Microbial action:** Lichens and bacteria secrete oxalic and carbonic acids that dissolve minerals.
- **Decomposition:** Dead organic matter produces humic acids that accelerate chemical weathering.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** When asked about "types of weathering," students often describe them as **mutually exclusive**. In reality, all three operate **simultaneously**, with one dominating depending on climate. Also, **examples are mandatory** — failing to provide them costs 1–2 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Question — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) Explain the process of soil formation from parent rock with a neat diagram of the soil profile. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Process of Soil Formation (Pedogenesis) (5 Marks):**

Soil formation from parent rock occurs through the following sequential stages:

**Stage 1: Exposure of Parent Rock** — Tectonic activity or denudation brings bedrock to the surface.

**Stage 2: Physical Weathering (Disintegration)** — Temperature changes, frost action, and exfoliation break the rock into smaller fragments (regolith), creating increased surface area for chemical attack.

**Stage 3: Chemical Weathering (Decomposition)** — Water, oxygen, carbon dioxide, and organic acids chemically alter the minerals. Feldspars hydrolyze to clays; iron-bearing minerals oxidize; carbonates dissolve.

**Stage 4: Biological Activity** — Pioneer organisms (lichens, mosses) colonize. Their decay produces organic acids. Roots penetrate deeper. Organic matter accumulates and mixes with mineral particles to form **humus**.

**Stage 5: Horizon Development (Pedogenesis proper)** — Over time, distinct layers (horizons) develop through:
- **Eluviation** — Leaching of clay, iron, and organic matter from upper layers.
- **Illuviation** — Deposition of leached materials in lower layers.
- **Organic accumulation** — At the surface.
- **Translocation** — Movement of materials within the profile.

**Stage 6: Maturation** — A mature soil profile (O-A-E-B-C-R) develops with distinct morphological, chemical, and biological properties.

**Soil Profile Diagram (2 Marks):**

A neat, labelled cross-section must be drawn showing all six horizons (O, A, E, B, C, R) with the following annotations:

- **O Horizon** — Organic litter (dark brown, ~5–10 cm)
- **A Horizon** — Topsoil (dark, mineral + organic, ~10–30 cm)
- **E Horizon** — Eluviation zone (light, leached)
- **B Horizon** — Subsoil (reddish-brown, clay-enriched)
- **C Horizon** — Weathered parent rock (partially disintegrated)
- **R Horizon** — Bedrock (unweathered, hard rock)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A **neat, labelled diagram** is **MANDATORY** for full marks. Drawing it without depth indication or proper labels will attract a 2-mark penalty.

---

**(b) Discuss the various factors affecting weathering of rocks. How does weathering influence civil engineering construction? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Factors Affecting Weathering (4 Marks):**

1. **Climate** (Most Important Factor)
   - **Hot & humid climate** → Dominant **chemical weathering** (e.g., lateritic soils in Kerala).
   - **Cold & dry climate** → Dominant **physical weathering** (e.g., talus in Ladakh).
   - **High rainfall** accelerates chemical reactions and leaching.
   - **High temperature** increases reaction rates (Arrhenius principle).

2. **Parent Rock Characteristics**
   - **Mineral composition:** Olivine and pyroxene weather faster than quartz.
   - **Texture:** Fine-grained rocks weather faster than coarse-grained (more surface area).
   - **Jointing & Fractures:** More joints = faster weathering.
   - **Solubility:** Limestone and rock salt dissolve readily.

3. **Vegetation & Biological Activity**
   - Dense vegetation → more organic acids → more chemical weathering.
   - Root systems → physical wedging.
   - Microorganisms → secrete acids.

4. **Topography (Relief)**
   - **Steep slopes** → rapid erosion exposes fresh rock → physical weathering dominates.
   - **Flat terrain** → water stagnation → chemical weathering dominates.

5. **Time**
   - Longer exposure = deeper weathering.
   - Tropical soils may be weathered to depths exceeding 30 m.

6. **Human Activities**
   - Mining, quarrying, acid rain from industries accelerate weathering.

**Influence on Civil Engineering Construction (3 Marks):**

1. **Foundation Design** — Weathered rock has lower bearing capacity than fresh rock. Engineers must identify the weathering grade (I–VI classification by IS 4464).
2. **Slope Stability** — Weathered slopes (e.g., lateritic slopes in Western Ghats) are prone to landslides during monsoons.
3. **Tunneling & Excavation** — Highly weathered rock requires supports; fresh rock stands without support.
4. **Material Selection** — Weathered rocks supply clay (bricks, tiles), laterite (blocks), and sand (aggregates).
5. **Reservoir & Dam Construction** — Highly weathered foundations may cause seepage; soluble rocks cause leakage (e.g., limestone karst regions).
6. **Road Construction** — Weathered rock must be assessed for use as subgrade; highly weathered material may be unsuitable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** For "factors affecting weathering," a common mistake is omitting **time** as a factor. Also, students must explicitly state at least **3 engineering implications** with examples to score full marks in part (b).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ **Soil** = unconsolidated weathered rock material + organic matter + water + air (3-phase system).
- ✅ Soil formation is governed by the equation: $S = f(Cl, O, R, P, T)$ — Jenny's CLORPT equation.
- ✅ **Weathering** = in-situ breakdown; **Erosion** = transportation (DO NOT confuse).
- ✅ Three types: **Physical, Chemical, Biological** — all operate simultaneously.
- ✅ **Physical weathering** = NO chemical change; only size reduction (Frost, Thermal, Exfoliation, Abrasion).
- ✅ **Chemical weathering** = NEW minerals formed (Oxidation, Hydrolysis, Carbonation, Hydration, Solution).
- ✅ **Biological weathering** = Roots (1.5 MPa), burrowing, microbial acids.
- ✅ **Frost wedging pressure** = up to **207 MPa** at -22°C; water expands **9%** on freezing.
- ✅ **Hydrolysis of K-feldspar** is the **primary source of clay minerals** in tropical soils.
- ✅ **Carbonation of limestone** = $\text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Ca}(\text{HCO}_3)_2$ → forms **karst topography**.
- ✅ **Soil profile horizons** = O, A, E, B, C, R (O = organic top; R = bedrock bottom).
- ✅ **Soil formation rate** = 0.01 to 0.1 mm/year (extremely slow — soil is a non-renewable resource).
- ✅ **Engineering significance** = Foundation bearing capacity, slope stability, tunneling, dam site selection.
- ✅ **Climate is the MOST important factor** controlling weathering intensity.
- ✅ Always draw **labelled diagrams** in exam answers for full marks.
- ✅ Always provide **examples** when describing weathering processes.
